taharah-intro-1-1:
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

Allah, the Exalted, said: "O you who believe, when you rise to perform prayer, wash your faces and your hands up to the elbows, wipe your heads, and wash your feet up to the ankles."

The obligatory acts of purification are:

Washing the three limbs and wiping the head. The elbows and ankles are included in the washing. The obligatory extent of wiping the head is the area of the forelock, based on the narration of al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah [that the Prophet, peace be upon him, came to the refuse dump of a people, urinated, performed ablution, and wiped over his forelock and his leather socks].

The sunnahs of purification are:

Washing the hands before inserting them into the vessel when the one performing ablution has awoken from sleep, mentioning the name of Allah at the beginning of ablution,

using the tooth-stick (siwak), rinsing the mouth, sniffing water into the nose, wiping the ears, combing through the beard and fingers, and repeating each washing up to three times.

It is recommended for the one performing ablution to intend purification, to wipe the entire head, and to maintain the order of ablution by beginning with what Allah, the Exalted, mentioned first, and to begin with the right side.

The matters that nullify ablution are:
taharah-intro-1-2:
Everything that exits from the two passages (front and back), and blood, pus, and serum when they exit from the body and spread to a place that requires purification; vomiting when it fills the mouth; sleep while lying down, reclining, or leaning against something such that if it were removed one would fall; and being overcome in one's mind by fainting,

insanity, and laughing out loud in any prayer that contains bowing and prostration.

The obligatory acts of the ritual bath (ghusl) are: rinsing the mouth, sniffing water into the nose, and washing the entire body.

The sunnah of the ritual bath is:

That the one bathing begins by washing his hands and private parts, and removes any impurity on his body. Then he performs ablution as he would for prayer, except for his feet. Then he pours water over his head and the rest of his body three times. Then he moves to a different spot and washes his feet.

A woman is not required to undo her braids during the ritual bath if the water reaches the roots of her hair.

The matters that obligate the ritual bath are:

The emission of semen with force and desire, from both men and women; the meeting of the two circumcised parts even without emission; menstruation; and post-natal bleeding.

The Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, prescribed the ritual bath for Friday, the two Eids, and entering the state of ihram.

There is no ritual bath required for pre-seminal fluid (madhi) or prostatic fluid (wadi), but they require ablution.
taharah-intro-1-3:
Purification from ritual impurities (hadath) is permissible with rainwater, water from rivers, springs, wells, and seawater. It is not permissible with liquid extracted from trees or fruits, nor with water that has been overwhelmed by another substance such that it is no longer in the nature of water,

such as beverages, vinegar, rosewater, bean-water, broth, and turmeric-water.

Purification is permissible with water that has been mixed with a pure substance that has changed one of its qualities, such as water from flood mud, and water mixed with potash, soap, or saffron.

Every body of water into which impurity has fallen may not be used for ablution, whether it be small or large, [because the Prophet, peace be upon him, commanded the protection of water from impurity, saying: "Let none of you urinate in standing water, nor bathe in it from major ritual impurity."] And [he, peace be upon him, said: "When one of you wakes from sleep, let him not dip his hand into the vessel until he has washed it three times, for he does not know where his hand has been."]

As for flowing water:

If impurity falls into it, ablution from it is permissible if no trace of the impurity is seen, since impurity does not settle in flowing water. And the large pond in which movement on one side does not cause movement on the other side
taharah-intro-1-4:
if impurity falls into one of its sides, ablution from the other side is permissible, because the apparent assumption is that the impurity does not reach it.

The death of creatures that do not have flowing blood in water does not make it impure, such as bedbugs, flies, wasps, and scorpions. And the death of creatures that live in water does not render it impure, such as fish, frogs, and crabs.

Used water may not be used for purification from ritual impurities.

Used water is: any water with which a ritual impurity was removed, or which was used on the body as an act of worship.

Every hide that has been tanned is purified, and prayer in it and ablution from it are permissible, except the hide of a pig or a human.

The hair of a dead animal,

its bones,

its sinews, its hooves, and its horns are pure.

When impurity falls into a well, it is to be drained, and draining its water constitutes its purification.

If a mouse, sparrow, swift, or gecko dies in it, between twenty and thirty buckets are to be drawn out,

depending on the size of the animal.

If a pigeon, chicken, or cat dies in it, between forty and sixty buckets are to be drawn out.

If a dog, sheep, or human dies in it, all the water in it is to be drained.
taharah-intro-1-5:
If the animal has swollen or decomposed in the well, all the water is to be drained, whether the animal was small or large.

The number of buckets is measured by the medium-sized bucket commonly used for wells in the locality. If a large bucket that holds the equivalent of twenty medium buckets is used, it counts accordingly.

If the well is a spring that cannot be fully drained, they should draw out the equivalent of what was in it. It has been narrated from Muhammad ibn al-Hasan, may Allah have mercy on him, that he said: Two hundred to three hundred buckets should be drawn from it.

If a dead creature is found in a well or elsewhere, and they do not know when it fell in, and it has not swollen or decomposed, they should repeat the prayers of one day and one night if they had been performing ablution from it, and wash everything that its water had touched.

If it had swollen or decomposed, they should repeat the prayers of three days and their nights according to Abu Hanifah, may Allah have mercy on him. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad, may Allah have mercy on them, said: They are not required to repeat anything until they ascertain when it fell in.
taharah-intro-1-6:
The leftover water of a human and of animals whose meat is eaten is pure. The leftover water of a dog, pig, and predatory beasts is impure. The leftover water of a cat, a free-roaming chicken, predatory birds, and creatures that dwell in houses such as snakes and mice is disliked (makruh).

The leftover water of a donkey and a mule is of doubtful status. If one finds nothing else, one performs ablution with it and also performs dry ablution (tayammum); whichever one begins with is permissible.
taharah-bab-1-1:
Whoever cannot find water and is a traveler, or is outside the city with approximately a mile or more between him and the city,

or can find water but is ill and fears that using water will worsen his illness, or the one in a state of major ritual impurity fears that washing with water will kill him from the cold or make him ill, then he performs dry ablution (tayammum) with clean earth.

Tayammum consists of two strikes:

With one he wipes his face, and with the other he wipes his arms up to the elbows. Tayammum for major and minor ritual impurity is the same.

Tayammum is permissible, according to Abu Hanifah and Muhammad, with anything that is of the genus of earth, such as soil, sand, stone, gypsum, calciumite, antimony, and arsite.

Abu Yusuf, may Allah have mercy on him, said: It is only permissible with soil and sand specifically.

Intention is obligatory in tayammum but recommended in ablution.

Everything that nullifies ablution also nullifies tayammum, and it is additionally nullified by seeing water when one is able to use it. Tayammum is only permissible with pure clean earth.

It is recommended for one who cannot find water but expects to find it at the end of the prayer time to delay the prayer to the end of the time. If he finds water, he performs ablution and prays; otherwise, he performs tayammum.

One may pray with a single tayammum as many obligatory and voluntary prayers as one wishes.

Tayammum is permissible for a healthy person within the city if a funeral is present and the guardian is someone else, and he fears that if he occupies himself with ablution the funeral prayer will pass him by -- he may perform tayammum and pray. Likewise, one who is present for the Eid prayer and fears that if he occupies himself with ablution the Eid prayer will pass him by -- he may perform tayammum and pray. However, if one attending the Friday prayer fears that occupying himself with ablution will cause him to miss the Friday prayer, he does not perform tayammum; rather he performs ablution, and if he catches the Friday prayer he prays it, otherwise he prays the Zuhr prayer as four units. Similarly, if the prayer time becomes tight and he fears that performing ablution will cause the time to expire, he does not perform tayammum; rather he performs ablution and prays it as a make-up prayer.

If a traveler forgets the water in his luggage and performs tayammum and prays, then remembers the water while still within the prayer time, he does not repeat the prayer according to Abu Hanifah and Muhammad, may Allah have mercy on them.

Abu Yusuf said: He must repeat it.

The one performing tayammum, if he does not have a prevailing assumption that water is nearby, is not required to search for water. But if he has a prevailing assumption that water is nearby, he may not perform tayammum until he searches for it. If his companion has water, he should ask him for it before performing tayammum; if the companion refuses, he performs tayammum and prays.
taharah-bab-2-1:
Wiping over the leather socks (khuffayn) is permissible according to the Sunnah for every minor ritual impurity requiring ablution, provided one put on the leather socks while in a state of complete purification and then subsequently broke one's ablution.

If one is a resident, one may wipe for one day and one night; if one is a traveler, one may wipe for three days and their nights. The period begins from the occurrence of the ritual impurity after putting them on.

Wiping over the leather socks is done on their upper surface in lines with the fingers, beginning from the tips of the toes up to the shins.

The obligatory minimum is the area of three fingers from the smallest fingers of the hand.

Wiping is not permissible on a leather sock that has a large hole exposing the equivalent of three toes. If it is less than that, it is permissible.

Wiping over the leather socks is not permissible for one who is obligated to perform the ritual bath (ghusl).

What nullifies wiping is everything that nullifies ablution, and it is additionally nullified by removing the leather sock and by the expiration of the wiping period. When the period expires, one removes the leather socks, washes one's feet, and prays, without needing to repeat the rest of the ablution.

One who begins wiping as a resident and then travels before the completion of one day and one night may wipe for three days and their nights. One who begins wiping as a traveler and then becomes a resident: if he has wiped for one day and one night or more, he must remove his leather socks and wash his feet; if he has wiped for less than one day and one night, he completes the wiping of one day and one night.

One who wears an outer boot (jurmūq) over the leather sock may wipe over it.

Wiping over fabric socks (jawrabayn) is not permissible according to Abu Hanifah, may Allah have mercy on him,

unless they are soled or leather-lined. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad, may Allah have mercy on them, said: Wiping over fabric socks is permissible if they are thick enough that water does not seep through.

Wiping over the turban, cap, face-veil, and gloves is not permissible.

Wiping over splints is permissible, even if they were applied without ablution. If the splint falls off before healing, the wiping is not invalidated. If it falls off after healing, the wiping is invalidated.
taharah-bab-3-1-1:
The minimum duration of menstruation is three days and their nights.

Anything less than that is not menstruation but rather irregular bleeding (istihādah).

The maximum duration of menstruation is ten days and their nights, and anything beyond that is irregular bleeding.

Whatever a woman sees of redness, yellowness, or muddiness during her menstrual days is considered menstruation, until she sees pure white discharge.

Menstruation exempts the menstruating woman from prayer and forbids her from fasting. She makes up the fasts but does not make up the prayers. She may not enter the mosque, nor circumambulate the House (Ka'bah), nor may her husband have intercourse with her.

Neither a menstruating woman nor one in a state of major ritual impurity (junub) may recite the Quran.

One who is in a state of minor ritual impurity may not touch the written copy of the Quran (mushaf), unless he takes it with its cover.

If menstrual bleeding stops before ten days, intercourse is not permissible until she performs the ritual bath, or until the time of one complete prayer has passed over her. If her bleeding stops at ten days, intercourse is permissible before the ritual bath.

Purity that intervenes between two periods of bleeding within the menstrual duration is considered as continuous bleeding.

The minimum duration of purity between two menstrual periods is fifteen days, and there is no maximum limit.

Irregular bleeding (istihādah) is blood that a woman sees for less than three days or more than ten days. Its ruling is like that of a constant nosebleed: it does not prevent fasting, prayer, or intercourse.
taharah-bab-3-1-2:
If the bleeding exceeds ten days and the woman has a known regular cycle,

she is referred back to her habitual days, and what exceeds that is irregular bleeding. If she began experiencing irregular bleeding from the onset of puberty, her menstruation is ten days of each month and the rest is irregular bleeding.

The woman with irregular bleeding, the one suffering from urinary incontinence, the one with a constant nosebleed, and the one with an unceasing wound -- each of them performs ablution for the time of each prayer, and they may pray with that ablution during that time whatever they wish of obligatory and voluntary prayers. When the prayer time expires, their ablution becomes invalid, and they must perform a fresh ablution for the next prayer.

Post-natal bleeding (nifas): It is the blood that exits following childbirth. The blood that a pregnant woman sees, and what a woman sees during labor before the emergence of the child, is irregular bleeding.

There is no minimum duration for post-natal bleeding, and its maximum is forty days. What exceeds that is irregular bleeding. If the blood exceeds forty days and the woman had previously given birth and had a known post-natal bleeding habit, she is referred back to her habitual days. If she had no previous habit, then the default for her post-natal bleeding is forty days. If a woman gives birth to twins in a single pregnancy,
taharah-bab-3-1-3: her post-natal bleeding is counted from the blood that exits after the first child, according to Abu Hanifah and Abu Yusuf. Muhammad and Zufar said: Her post-natal bleeding is counted from the blood that exits after the second child.
taharah-bab-4-1:
Purifying impurity is obligatory from the body of the one praying, his garment,

and the place upon which he prays.

It is permissible to purify impurity with water and with every pure liquid capable of removing it, such as vinegar and rosewater.

If impurity with a physical substance strikes a leather sock and dries, then rubbing it on the ground suffices.

Semen is impure; its wet form must be washed. If it dries on a garment, scraping it off suffices.

If impurity strikes a mirror or sword, wiping them suffices.

If impurity strikes the ground and dries by the sun and its trace disappears, prayer on that spot is permissible, but tayammum from that earth is not permissible.

If a person is affected by a heavy impurity -- such as blood, urine, feces, or wine -- in an amount equal to a dirham coin or less, prayer with it is permissible. If it exceeds that, prayer is not permissible. If a light impurity affects him, such as the urine of animals whose meat is eaten,

prayer with it is permissible as long as it does not reach a quarter of the garment.

Purifying impurity that must be washed is of two types:

As for impurity that has a visible trace, its purification is the removal of its substance, except for a remaining trace that is difficult to remove. As for impurity that has no visible trace, its purification is to wash it until the one washing has a prevailing assumption that it has become pure.

Cleansing after relieving oneself (istinja) is a sunnah. Stones or their equivalent suffice for it -- one wipes until clean -- and there is no prescribed number. Washing with water is better. If the impurity has spread beyond its point of exit, only water suffices. One should not perform istinja with a bone, dung, food, or with the right hand except out of necessity.
salah-intro-1:
The first time of the Fajr prayer is when the second dawn rises -- that is, the white light that spreads horizontally across the horizon -- and its last time is before the sun rises. The first time of the Zuhr prayer is when the sun declines from its zenith, and its last time according to Abu Hanifah is when the shadow of everything becomes twice its length, excluding the shadow at the time of zenith.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: When the shadow of everything becomes equal to its length.

The first time of the Asr prayer is when the Zuhr time exits, according to both opinions, and its last time is before the sun sets.

The first time of the Maghrib prayer is when the sun sets, and its last time is before the disappearing of the twilight -- which is the white light on the horizon after the redness,

according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: It is the redness. The first time of the Isha prayer is when the twilight disappears, and its last time is before the dawn rises. The first time of the Witr prayer is after the Isha prayer, and its last time is before the dawn rises.

It is recommended to perform the Fajr prayer when the dawn light is clearly visible (isfar),

to delay the Zuhr prayer in summer (ibrad) and advance it in winter, to delay the Asr prayer as long as the sun has not changed color, to hasten the Maghrib prayer, and to delay the Isha prayer until before one-third of the night has passed. It is recommended for the Witr prayer that one who regularly prays at night delay it to the end of the night; if he is not confident of waking, he should pray Witr before sleeping.
salah-bab-1-1:
The call to prayer (adhan) is a sunnah for the five daily prayers and the Friday prayer,

and not for any others.

The form of the adhan is that one says: "Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar" -- to its end, and there is no repetition (tarji') in it. In the Fajr adhan, after "hayya 'ala al-falah" (come to success), one adds "al-salatu khayrun min al-nawm" (prayer is better than sleep) twice.

The iqamah is like the adhan, except that after "hayya 'ala al-falah" one adds "qad qamat al-salah" (the prayer has commenced) twice.

One should recite the adhan slowly and the iqamah quickly. One faces the qiblah for both, and when reaching "al-salah" and "al-falah," one turns one's face to the right and to the left.

The adhan is called for a missed prayer and the iqamah is given. If multiple prayers were missed, the adhan is called for the first one and the iqamah is given, and for the rest one has a choice: one may call the adhan and iqamah, or one may suffice with the iqamah alone.

One should call the adhan and iqamah in a state of ritual purity. If one calls the adhan without ablution, it is permissible, but it is disliked to give the iqamah without ablution or to call the adhan while in a state of major ritual impurity. The adhan is not to be called for any prayer before its time has entered.
salah-bab-2-1:
The one praying is required to first perform purification from ritual impurities and physical impurities as we have previously described, and to cover his nakedness ('awrah). The nakedness of a man is from below the navel to the knee, and the knee is part of the nakedness.

The entire body of a free woman is nakedness except her face, palms, and feet. Whatever is nakedness for a man is also nakedness for a female slave, and her belly and back are nakedness, but what is besides that of her body is not nakedness.

One who cannot find anything to remove impurity with prays with the impurity and does not repeat the prayer.

One who cannot find a garment prays naked while sitting, gesturing for bowing and prostrating. If he prays standing, it is acceptable, but the former is better. He intends the prayer he is entering with an intention not separated from the opening takbir by any action. He faces the qiblah, unless he is in a state of fear, in which case he prays facing whichever direction he is able. If the qiblah is unclear to him and there is no one present to ask, he exercises his own judgment and prays.

If he discovers after completing the prayer that he was wrong, there is no obligation to repeat it. If he discovers this while still in prayer, he turns toward the qiblah and continues from where he left off.
salah-bab-3-1-1:
The obligatory acts of prayer are six:

The opening takbir (tahrimah),

standing, recitation, bowing, prostration, and the final sitting for the duration of the tashahhud.

Anything beyond that is sunnah. When a man enters the prayer, he says "Allahu Akbar" and raises his hands with the takbir until his thumbs are level with his earlobes. If he says instead of the takbir: "Allah is the most majestic" or "the most magnificent"

or "the Most Merciful is the greatest," it suffices him according to Abu Hanifah and Muhammad. Abu Yusuf said: Nothing suffices except the specific wording of the takbir. He places his right hand over his left hand and places them below his navel. Then he says: "Glory be to You, O Allah, and praise be to You. Blessed is Your name, exalted is Your majesty, and there is no god other than You." He then seeks refuge in Allah from Satan the accursed, and recites "In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful" -- both said silently. Then he recites the Opening of the Book (al-Fatihah) and a surah after it, or three verses from any surah he wishes. When the imam says "wa la al-dallin" (nor of those who went astray), he says: "Amin" -- and they say it silently. Then he says "Allahu Akbar" and bows.
salah-bab-3-1-2:
He places his hands on his knees, spreads his fingers, straightens his back, neither raising his head nor lowering it. He says in his bowing: "Glory be to my Lord, the Magnificent" three times -- and that is its minimum. Then he raises his head and says ["sami'a Allahu liman hamidah"] and prostrates, placing his hands on the ground and placing his face between his palms, prostrating on his nose and forehead. If he prostrates on only one of them, it is permissible according to Abu Hanifah.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Prostrating on the nose alone is not permissible except with an excuse. If he prostrates on the turban wrapped around his head or on a loose part of his garment, it is permissible. He exposes his upper arms, keeps his belly away from his thighs, and points his toes toward the qiblah. He says in his prostration: "Glory be to my Lord, the Most High" three times -- and that is its minimum. Then he raises his head

and says "Allahu Akbar." When he is settled in sitting, he says "Allahu Akbar" and prostrates. When he is settled in prostration, he says "Allahu Akbar" and rises to standing on the balls of his feet, without sitting down or supporting himself with his hands on the ground.

He does in the second unit (rak'ah) what he did in the first, except that he does not recite the opening supplication nor the seeking of refuge, and he does not raise his hands except in the first takbir.
salah-bab-3-1-3:
When he raises his head from the second prostration in the second unit, he spreads his left foot and sits on it, and positions the right foot upright with his toes pointing toward the qiblah. He places his hands on his thighs, spreads his fingers, and recites the tashahhud.

The tashahhud is to say: "All greetings, prayers, and pure words are for Allah. Peace be upon you, O Prophet, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings. Peace be upon us and upon the righteous servants of Allah. I bear witness that there is no god but Allah, and I bear witness that Muhammad is His servant and messenger." He does not add anything beyond this in the first sitting.

He recites in the last two units the Opening of the Book (al-Fatihah) only. When he sits at the end of the prayer, he sits as in the first sitting, recites the tashahhud, sends blessings upon the Prophet, peace be upon him, and supplicates with whatever he wishes using words that resemble the expressions of the Quran and transmitted supplications. He should not supplicate with words that resemble ordinary human speech. Then he gives the salutation to his right saying: "Peace be upon you and the mercy of Allah," and to his left likewise.

He recites audibly in the Fajr prayer and the first two units
salah-bab-3-1-4:
of the Maghrib and Isha prayers if he is the imam, and he recites silently in what comes after the first two units. If he is praying alone, he has a choice: if he wishes he recites audibly enough to hear himself, and if he wishes he recites silently. The imam recites silently in the Zuhr and Asr prayers.

The Witr prayer:

is three units without separating them with a salutation.

He recites the qunut supplication in the third unit before bowing, throughout the year. He recites in each unit of the Witr the Opening of the Book and a surah after it. When he intends to recite the qunut, he says "Allahu Akbar" and raises his hands, then recites the qunut.

The qunut is not recited in any other prayer.

There is no specific surah in any prayer that nothing else will suffice in its place. It is disliked to designate a specific surah for a prayer such that one never recites anything else in it.

The minimum recitation that suffices in prayer is whatever bears the name of Quran, according to Abu Hanifah.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Less than three short verses or one long verse does not suffice.

The one following the imam does not recite behind him.

One who wishes to join another person's prayer needs two intentions: the intention of the prayer and the intention of following.

The congregational prayer is an emphasized sunnah.

The most entitled person to lead the prayer is the most knowledgeable
salah-bab-3-1-5:
of the Sunnah. If they are equal, then the one who recites the Quran best. If they are equal, then the most pious. If they are equal, then the eldest.

It is disliked to put forward a slave, a Bedouin, an open sinner, a blind person, or a child of fornication as imam, but if they lead the prayer it is valid.

The imam should not prolong the prayer for the congregation.

It is disliked for women to pray in congregation by themselves, but if they do so, the female imam stands in the middle of their row.

One who prays with a single person places him to his right.

If there are two people, the imam steps forward in front of them.

It is not permissible for men to follow a woman or a child as imam.

The rows are arranged: men first, then boys, then women.

If a woman stands beside a man and they share the same prayer, his prayer is invalidated.

It is disliked for women to attend the congregational prayer. Abu Hanifah said there is no objection to an elderly woman going out for the Fajr, Maghrib, and Isha prayers. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: It is permissible for an elderly woman to go out for every
salah-bab-3-1-6:
prayer. One who is ritually pure does not pray behind one who suffers from urinary incontinence, nor do ritually pure women pray behind a woman with irregular bleeding, nor does one who can recite pray behind one who cannot, nor does one who is clothed pray behind one who is naked. It is permissible for one who has performed tayammum to lead those who have performed ablution, and for one who has wiped over leather socks to lead those who have washed their feet. One who is standing may pray behind one who is sitting, but one who bows and prostrates does not pray behind one who gestures. One performing an obligatory prayer does not pray behind one performing a voluntary prayer, nor does one performing one obligatory prayer pray behind one performing a different obligatory prayer. One performing a voluntary prayer may pray behind one performing an obligatory prayer.

One who followed an imam and then discovered that the imam was without ablution

must repeat the prayer.

It is disliked for the one praying to fidget with his garment or his body. He should not turn pebbles over, unless he cannot prostrate, in which case he levels them once. He should not crack his fingers, nor place his hands on his hips, nor let his garment hang loosely, nor tie his hair up, nor tuck his garment up,

nor turn his head, nor squat (iq'a), nor return a greeting verbally or by hand, nor sit cross-legged except with an excuse, nor eat, nor drink.

If his ablution is involuntarily broken, he leaves the prayer. If he is the imam, he appoints a deputy, performs ablution, and continues from where he left off -- though starting the prayer over is better.

If he sleeps and has a wet dream, or becomes insane, or faints, or laughs out loud, he must restart both the ablution and the prayer.
salah-bab-3-1-7:
If he speaks during his prayer, whether intentionally or forgetfully, his prayer is invalidated.

If his ablution is involuntarily broken after the tashahhud, he performs ablution and gives the salutation. If he intentionally breaks his ablution in this state, or speaks, or performs an action that is incompatible with prayer, his prayer is complete.

If one performing tayammum sees water during his prayer, his prayer is invalidated.

If he sees water after sitting for the duration of the tashahhud, or if one who was wiping over leather socks has his wiping period expire, or removes his leather socks with a gentle action, or if an illiterate person learns a surah, or a naked person finds a garment, or one who was gesturing becomes able to bow and prostrate, or he remembers that he has a prayer before this one that must be made up, or if the Asr time enters during the Friday prayer, or if one wiping over a splint has it fall off after healing, or if one with a chronic excuse has his excuse cease --

his prayer is invalidated according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: His prayer is complete.
salah-bab-4-1: Whoever misses a prayer makes it up when he remembers it, and he must give it priority over the current prayer, unless he fears that the current prayer's time will also expire, in which case he prays the current prayer first and then makes up the missed one. If he has missed multiple prayers, he makes them up in the order they were originally obligated, unless the missed prayers exceed six, in which case the order is dropped.
salah-bab-5-1:
Prayer is not permissible at sunrise, nor when the sun is at its zenith at midday, nor at sunset. One does not pray the funeral prayer nor prostrate for a Quranic recitation except during the Asr prayer of one's day at sunset.

It is disliked to perform voluntary prayers after the Fajr prayer until the sun rises, and after the Asr prayer until the sun sets. There is no objection to praying missed prayers during these two times, prostrating for Quranic recitation, or praying the funeral prayer.

But one does not pray the two units of circumambulation (tawaf).

It is disliked to perform voluntary prayers after the dawn has risen beyond the two sunnah units of Fajr, and one should not perform voluntary prayers before the Maghrib prayer.
salah-bab-6-1:
The sunnah regarding prayer is to pray two units after the rise of dawn, four units before Zuhr, two units after it, four units before Asr -- or two if one wishes -- two units after Maghrib, four units before Isha, and four units after it -- or two if one wishes.

For voluntary prayers during the day, if one wishes one may pray two units with a single salutation, and if one wishes, four. Adding beyond that is disliked.

As for voluntary prayers at night, Abu Hanifah said: If one prays eight units with a single salutation, it is permissible, and adding beyond that is disliked. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: One should not exceed two units with a single salutation at night.

Recitation in the obligatory prayer is required in the first two units, and in the last units one has a choice: one may recite, glorify Allah, or remain silent.

Recitation is required in all units of the voluntary prayer and in all units of the Witr. One who enters into a voluntary prayer and then invalidates it must make it up.

If one prays four units and sat in the first two, then invalidates the last two, one makes up two units.

One may pray voluntary prayers while sitting even with the ability to stand. If one begins standing and then sits, it is permissible according to Abu Hanifah.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: It is not permissible except with an excuse.

One who is outside the city may perform voluntary prayers on his riding animal, facing whichever direction it turns, gesturing for bowing and prostration.
salah-bab-7-1:
The prostration of forgetfulness (sujud al-sahw) is obligatory for additions and omissions, performed after the salutation. Then one prostrates twice, recites the tashahhud, and gives the salutation.

Forgetfulness requires prostration when one adds to the prayer an action of its kind that is not part of it, or omits a sunnah act, or omits the recitation of the Opening of the Book (al-Fatihah), the qunut supplication, the tashahhud, the takbirs of the Eid prayer, or when the imam recites audibly where he should recite silently or recites silently where he should recite audibly.

The imam's forgetfulness obligates the follower to prostrate. If the imam does not prostrate, the follower does not prostrate. If the follower forgets, neither the imam nor the follower is required to prostrate.

One who forgets the first sitting and then remembers while still closer to the sitting position returns, sits, and recites the tashahhud. If he is closer to the standing position, he does not return.

He prostrates for forgetfulness. One who forgets the final sitting and stands for a fifth unit returns to sitting as long as he has not yet prostrated, and the fifth unit is disregarded. He prostrates for forgetfulness. If he completes the fifth unit with a prostration, his obligatory prayer is invalidated and his prayer becomes a voluntary one, and he must add a sixth unit to it.

If he sat in the fourth unit for the duration of the tashahhud and then stood without giving the salutation, thinking it was the first sitting, he returns to sitting as long as he has not prostrated in the fifth unit, gives the salutation. If he completes the fifth unit with a prostration, he adds

another unit to it, prostrates for forgetfulness, and his obligatory prayer is complete, with the two extra units counting as voluntary.

One who is uncertain in his prayer, not knowing whether he prayed three or four units, and this is the first time such doubt has occurred to him, restarts the prayer. If such doubt occurs to him frequently, he acts on his prevailing assumption if he has one. If he has no prevailing assumption, he builds upon certainty.
salah-bab-8-1:
If the sick person is unable to stand, he prays sitting, bowing and prostrating. If he cannot bow and prostrate, he gestures with his head and makes the prostration lower than the bowing.

He does not raise anything to his face to prostrate upon.

If he cannot sit, he lies on his back with his feet toward the qiblah and gestures for bowing and prostration. If he lies on his side with his face toward the qiblah and gestures, it is permissible. If he cannot gesture with his head, he delays the prayer and does not gesture with his eyes, heart, or eyebrows. If he is able to stand but cannot bow or prostrate, he is not required to stand,

and he may pray sitting, gesturing. If a healthy person prays part of his prayer standing and then becomes ill,

he completes it sitting, bowing and prostrating, or gesturing if he cannot bow and prostrate, or lying down if he cannot sit. If he prays by gesturing and then becomes able to bow and prostrate, he restarts the prayer.

One who loses consciousness for five prayers or fewer makes them up when he recovers. If more than that are missed due to unconsciousness, he does not make them up.
salah-bab-9-1:
The prostrations of recitation in the Quran are fourteen: at the end of Surah al-A'raf, in Surah al-Ra'd, al-Nahl, Bani Isra'il (al-Isra'), Maryam, the first one in Surah al-Hajj, al-Furqan, al-Naml, "Alif Lam Mim Tanzil" (al-Sajdah), Sad, "Ha Mim" al-Sajdah (Fussilat), "Idha al-Sama'u Inshaqqat" (al-Inshiqaq), and "Iqra' Bismi Rabbik" (al-'Alaq).

Prostration is obligatory at all these places, equally upon the reciter and the listener, whether or not one intended to hear the Quran. If the imam recites a verse of prostration, he prostrates and the followers prostrate with him. If a follower recites it, neither the imam nor the follower prostrates.

If they hear a verse of prostration while in prayer from someone not praying with them, they do not prostrate during the prayer but prostrate after it. If they prostrate during the prayer, it does not count for them,

but their prayer is not invalidated.

If one recites a verse of prostration outside of prayer and does not prostrate for it, then enters into prayer and recites it again and prostrates, the prostration counts for both recitations. If one recites it outside of prayer and prostrates, then enters into prayer and recites it again, one must prostrate again, and the first prostration does not suffice. If one repeats the recitation of a single verse of prostration in a single sitting, a single prostration suffices.

When one intends to prostrate, one says "Allahu Akbar" without raising the hands, prostrates, then says "Allahu Akbar" and raises one's head. There is no tashahhud or salutation required.
salah-bab-10-1:
The journey that changes the rulings:

is that a person intends a destination between which and himself is a distance of three days and their nights by the pace of camels and walking on foot. This is not measured by travel on water.

The obligation of the traveler according to us is: two units in every four-unit prayer, and he is not permitted to add beyond that. If he prays four units and sat in the second unit for the duration of the tashahhud, two units count as his obligation and the last two are voluntary for him. If he did not sit for the duration of the tashahhud in the first two units, his prayer is invalidated.

When one sets out traveling, he prays two units once he has passed the buildings of the city. He remains under the ruling of travel until he intends to reside in a town for fifteen days or more, at which point he must pray in full. If he intends to stay for less than that, he does not pray in full.

One who enters a town without intending to stay for fifteen days, saying "I will leave tomorrow" or "the day after tomorrow" until years pass in this manner, prays two units.

If the army enters enemy territory and intends to stay for fifteen days, they do not pray in full.

If a traveler joins the prayer of a resident while the time remains, he completes the prayer in full.

If he joins him in a make-up prayer, his prayer behind him is not valid.

If a traveler leads residents and prays two units, he gives the salutation, and then the residents complete their prayer. It is recommended that he say when he gives the salutation: "Complete your prayer, for we are travelers." When a traveler enters his hometown, he prays in full even if he does not intend to reside there.

One who had a home and then moved away and took up residence elsewhere, and then travels and enters his original home, does not pray in full. If a traveler intends to stay in Makkah and Mina for fifteen days, he does not pray in full.

One who misses a prayer while traveling makes it up as two units when at home.

One who misses a prayer while at home makes it up as four units while traveling.

The disobedient and the obedient are equal in the concessions of travel.
salah-bab-11-1:
The Friday prayer is not valid except in a major city (misr jami') or in the prayer grounds of the city.

It is not valid in villages, nor may it be established except by the ruler or one whom the ruler has authorized.

Among its conditions is the time: it is valid during the time and not valid after it.

Among its conditions is the sermon (khutbah) before the prayer. The imam delivers two sermons, separating them by sitting. He delivers the sermons standing and in a state of ritual purity. If he limits himself to the remembrance of Allah, it is sufficient according to Abu Hanifah.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: A lengthy remembrance that can be called a sermon is necessary. If he delivers the sermon sitting or without ritual purity, it is valid but disliked. Among its conditions is the congregation, and its minimum according to Abu Hanifah

is three people besides

the imam. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Two people besides the imam. The imam recites audibly in the two units, and there is no specific surah required for them.

The Friday prayer is not obligatory upon a traveler, a woman, a sick person, a slave, or a blind person. If they attend and pray with the people, it counts for them as the obligatory prayer of the time.

It is permissible for a traveler, a slave, a sick person, and the like to lead the Friday prayer.

One who prays Zuhr at home on Friday before the imam prays, without a valid excuse -- it is disliked for him but his prayer is valid. If he then decides to attend the Friday prayer and sets out,

his Zuhr prayer is invalidated by his setting out according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: It is not invalidated until he actually joins the imam.

It is disliked for those with valid excuses to pray Zuhr in congregation on Friday, and likewise for the people of a prison.

One who catches the imam on Friday prays with him whatever he catches and builds the Friday prayer upon it. If he catches the imam during the tashahhud or during the prostration of forgetfulness, he builds the Friday prayer upon it according to Abu Hanifah and Abu Yusuf. Muhammad said: If he catches most of the second unit with the imam, he builds the Friday prayer upon it; if he catches less than that, he builds the Zuhr prayer upon it.

When the imam ascends the pulpit on Friday, the people cease praying and talking until he finishes his sermon. When the muezzins call the first adhan on Friday, the people leave off buying and selling and head to the Friday prayer.

When the imam ascends the pulpit, he sits and the muezzins call the adhan in front of the pulpit. When the imam finishes his sermon, they give the iqamah and they pray.
salah-bab-12-1:
It is recommended on the day of Eid al-Fitr that a person eat before going out to the prayer grounds, perform the ritual bath, apply perfume, and head to the prayer grounds. He does not say the takbir on the way to the prayer grounds according to Abu Hanifah; according to Abu Yusuf and Muhammad, he does say it.

One does not perform voluntary prayers at the prayer grounds before the Eid prayer. The time of the prayer enters when the sun has risen to a certain height, and it remains until the sun reaches its zenith; when the sun passes its zenith, its time has expired.

The imam prays with the people two units: in the first, he says the opening takbir and three additional takbirs after it, then recites the Opening of the Book and a surah after it, then says the takbir and bows with it. Then in the second unit, he begins with the recitation, then says three takbirs, and says a fourth takbir with which he bows. He raises his hands during the takbirs of the two Eids. Then he delivers two sermons after the prayer, in which he teaches the people about the charity of Fitr and its rulings.

One who misses the Eid prayer with the imam does not make it up.

If the crescent is obscured and people testify before the ruler that they saw the crescent after the sun passes its zenith, the Eid prayer is performed the next day. If a hindrance prevents the people from praying on the second day, it is not prayed after that.

It is recommended on the day of Eid al-Adha that one performs the ritual bath, applies perfume, delays eating until after the prayer is completed, and heads to the prayer grounds while saying the takbir.

The Adha prayer is prayed as two units like the Fitr prayer, and two sermons are delivered after it in which the people are taught about the sacrificial animal and the takbirs of the days of Tashriq. If a hindrance prevents the people from praying on the day of Adha, it is prayed the next day or the day after, but not after that.

The takbir of Tashriq begins after the Fajr prayer on the day of

Arafah and ends after the Asr prayer on the day of Sacrifice according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Until the Asr prayer

of the last of the days of Tashriq.

The takbir is said after the obligatory prayers, and it is to say: "Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar, la ilaha ill-Allah, wa-Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar, wa lillahi al-hamd."
salah-bab-13-1:
When the sun is eclipsed, the imam prays with the people two units in the manner of a voluntary prayer, with one bowing in each unit. He prolongs the recitation in them and recites silently according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: He recites audibly. Then he supplicates after the prayer until the sun clears.

The imam who leads the Friday prayer leads this prayer. If no one gathers the people, they pray individually.

There is no congregational prayer for a lunar eclipse; rather, each person prays individually. There is no sermon for the eclipse prayer.
salah-bab-14-1:
Abu Hanifah, may Allah have mercy on him, said: There is no prescribed congregational prayer for the prayer for rain (istisqa'). If the people pray individually, it is permissible. The prayer for rain consists of supplication and seeking forgiveness.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: The imam prays two units with the people, reciting audibly. He does not deliver a sermon. He faces the qiblah with supplication, and the imam turns his cloak inside out, but the people do not turn their cloaks. The people of the dhimmi (protected non-Muslim communities) do not attend the prayer for rain.
salah-bab-15-1:
It is recommended that the people gather in the month of Ramadan after the Isha prayer,

and their imam leads them in five sets of tarawih, each set consisting of two salutations (four units). He sits between every two sets for the duration of one set. Then he leads them in the Witr prayer. The Witr prayer is not prayed in congregation outside the month of Ramadan.
salah-bab-16-1:
When fear intensifies, the imam divides the people into two groups:

one group facing the enemy and one group behind him. He prays with this group one unit with two prostrations. When he raises his head from the second prostration, this group proceeds to face

the enemy and the other group comes. He prays with them one unit with two prostrations, recites the tashahhud, and gives the salutation, but they do not give the salutation. They go to face the enemy, and the first group comes and they each individually pray one unit with two prostrations without recitation, recite the tashahhud, and give the salutation. Then they proceed to face the enemy, and the other group comes and they each individually pray one unit with two prostrations with recitation, recite the tashahhud, and give the salutation.

If the imam is a resident, he prays two units with the first group and two units with the second. He prays two units of Maghrib with the first group and one unit with the second.

They do not fight during the prayer; if they do, their prayer is invalidated. If the fear intensifies greatly, they pray while mounted, individually, gesturing for bowing and prostration toward whichever direction they can, when they are unable to face the qiblah.
salah-bab-17-1-1:
When a man is dying, he is turned toward the qiblah on his right side and prompted to say the two testimonies of faith. When he dies, his jaw is tied shut and his eyes are closed. When they wish to wash him, they place him on a washing board and cover his private parts with a cloth.

They remove his clothes, give him ablution -- but do not rinse his mouth or nose -- then pour water over him. They fumigate the washing board with incense an odd number of times. The water is heated with lotus leaves or alkali; if neither is available, then plain water. His head and beard are washed with marsh mallow.

Then he is laid on his left side and washed with water and lotus leaves until the washer believes the water has reached the side touching the board. Then he is laid on his right side and washed with water and lotus leaves until the washer believes the water has reached the side touching the board.

Then he is sat up and leaned against the washer, who gently wipes his belly. If anything comes out, it is washed, but the ritual washing is not repeated.

Then he is dried with a cloth and placed in his shrouds. Perfume (hinnit) is placed on his head and beard, and camphor on his places of prostration.

The sunnah is that a man be shrouded in three garments:

a lower wrap (izar), a shirt (qamis), and an outer wrap (lafafah). If they limit it to two garments, it is permissible.
salah-bab-17-1-2:
When they wish to wrap the outer wrap around him, they begin with the left side, folding it over him, then the right side. If they fear the shroud will come undone, they tie it.

A woman is shrouded in five garments: a lower wrap, a shirt, a head-cover, a cloth to bind her chest, and an outer wrap. If they limit it to three garments, it is permissible. The head-cover goes over the shirt and under the outer wrap. Her hair is placed on her chest. The hair of the deceased is not combed, nor is his beard, nor are his nails clipped, nor is his hair knotted. The shrouds are fumigated with incense an odd number of times before the body is wrapped in them.

When they have finished, they pray over him. The person most entitled to lead the funeral prayer is the ruler if he is present. If the ruler is not present, it is recommended to put forward the imam of the neighborhood, then the next of kin.

If someone other than the next of kin and the ruler prays over the deceased, the next of kin may repeat the prayer. If the next of kin prayed, no one else may pray after him. If the deceased is buried without being prayed over, the prayer is performed at his grave.

The prayer consists of: saying "Allahu Akbar," then praising Allah after it; then saying "Allahu Akbar" and sending blessings upon the Prophet, peace be upon him; then saying "Allahu Akbar" and supplicating for himself, the deceased, and the Muslims; then saying a fourth "Allahu Akbar" and giving the salutation. The funeral prayer is not performed

for a deceased person inside a congregational mosque.
salah-bab-17-1-3:
When they carry him on the bier, they take it by its four legs and walk with it quickly but not at a trot. When they reach his grave, it is disliked for the people to sit before the bier is set down from the men's shoulders. The grave is dug with a lateral niche (lahd), and the deceased is entered from the side facing the qiblah. When he is placed in his niche, the one placing him says: "In the name of Allah and upon the way of the Messenger of Allah." He is turned to face the qiblah, the knots are untied, and the bricks are leveled over him. Baked bricks

and wood are disliked, but reeds are acceptable. Then the earth is heaped over him, and the grave is mounded -- not flattened. A newborn who cried after birth is named, washed, and prayed over. If the newborn did not cry, it is wrapped in a cloth and not prayed over.
salah-bab-18-1:
The martyr (shahid) is one who was killed by the polytheists,

or was found on the battlefield with signs of wounds, or was killed unjustly by Muslims without any blood-money being due for his killing. He is shrouded and prayed over but not washed. If one who was in a state of major ritual impurity (junub) is martyred, he is washed according to Abu Hanifah, and likewise a child. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Neither of them is washed. The blood is not washed from the martyr, nor are his clothes removed. However, fur garments, leather socks, padded garments, and weapons are removed from him.

One who was attended to after being wounded (irtithath) is washed. Being attended to means: that he ate, drank, received medical treatment, remained alive until a prayer time passed while conscious, or was moved from the battlefield alive.

One who is killed in the enforcement of a prescribed punishment (hadd) or retaliatory punishment (qisas) is washed and prayed over. One who is killed from among the rebels or highway robbers is not prayed over.
salah-bab-19-1:
Prayer inside the Ka'bah is permissible, whether obligatory or voluntary. If the imam prays with a congregation and some of them have their backs to the imam, it is valid. But if any of them has his back facing the imam's face, his prayer is not valid.

When the imam prays in the Sacred Mosque, the people form a circle

around the Ka'bah and pray following the imam. If any of them is closer to the Ka'bah than the imam, his prayer is valid as long as he is not on the same side as the imam.

One who prays on the roof of the Ka'bah -- his prayer is valid.
zakah-intro-1:
Zakat is obligatory upon the free, Muslim, adult, sane person when he possesses the minimum taxable amount (nisab) in complete ownership and a full lunar year has passed over it. There is no zakat upon a child, an insane person, or a slave under a contract of emancipation (mukatab).

One who has a debt that encompasses his wealth owes no zakat.

If his wealth exceeds the debt, he pays zakat on the surplus if it reaches the nisab. There is no zakat on one's dwelling, personal clothing, household furnishings, riding animals, service slaves, or weapons for personal use.

The payment of zakat is not valid except with an intention accompanying the payment or accompanying the setting aside of the obligatory amount.

One who gives away all his wealth in charity without intending zakat -- the obligation of zakat is lifted from him.
zakah-bab-1-1:
There is no charity (zakat) on fewer than five head of camels. When they reach five pasturing camels

and a full year has passed over them, the zakat on them is one sheep -- up to nine. When they are ten, the zakat is two sheep -- up to fourteen. When they are fifteen, the zakat is three sheep -- up to nineteen. When they are twenty, the zakat is four sheep -- up to twenty-four. When they are twenty-five, the zakat is one bint makhad (a one-year-old female camel) -- up to thirty-five. When they are thirty-six, the zakat is one bint labun (a two-year-old female camel) -- up to forty-five. When they are forty-six, the zakat is one hiqqah (a three-year-old female camel) -- up to sixty. When they are sixty-one, the zakat is one jadha'ah (a four-year-old female camel) -- up to seventy-five. When they are seventy-six, the zakat is two bint labun -- up to ninety. When they are ninety-one, the zakat is two hiqqah -- up to one hundred and twenty.

Then the calculation restarts: on five there is one sheep along with the two hiqqah, on ten there are two sheep, on fifteen there are three sheep, on twenty there are four sheep, on twenty-five there is one bint makhad -- up to one hundred and fifty, in which there are

three hiqqah. Then the calculation restarts: on five there is one sheep, on ten two sheep, on fifteen three sheep, on twenty four sheep, on twenty-five one bint makhad, on thirty-six one bint labun. When they reach one hundred and ninety-six, the zakat is four hiqqah -- up to two hundred. Then the calculation restarts perpetually as it restarted in the fifty after the one hundred and fifty.

Bactrian and Arabian camels are treated equally.
zakah-bab-2-1:
There is no charity (zakat) on fewer than thirty head of cattle. When they are thirty pasturing cattle and a full year has passed over them, the zakat on them is one tabi' or tabi'ah (a one-year-old calf). On forty, the zakat is one musinnah or musinn (a two-year-old cow or bull). When they exceed forty, the zakat on the excess is proportional up to sixty according to Abu Hanifah: on one extra there is one-fortieth of a musinnah, on two extra there is one-twentieth of a musinnah, on three extra there is three-fortieths of a musinnah, on four extra there is one-tenth.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: There is nothing on the excess until they reach sixty, in which case there are two tabi' or tabi'ah. On seventy there is one musinnah and one tabi'. On eighty there are two musinnah. On ninety there are three tabi'. On one hundred there are two tabi' and one musinnah. Water buffalo and domestic cattle are treated equally.
zakah-bab-3-1: There is no charity (zakat) on fewer than forty sheep. When they are forty pasturing sheep and a full year has passed over them, the zakat on them is one sheep -- up to one hundred and twenty. When they exceed by one, the zakat is two sheep -- up to two hundred. When they exceed by one, the zakat is three sheep. When they reach four hundred, the zakat is four sheep, then one sheep for every hundred. Woolly sheep (da'n) and goats (ma'z) are treated equally.
zakah-bab-4-1:
If horses are pasturing, both males and females, their owner has a choice: if he wishes, he gives one dinar per horse; if he wishes, he assesses their value and gives five dirhams for every two hundred dirhams. There is no zakat on males alone.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: There is no zakat on horses.

Nor on mules and donkeys, unless they are held for trade.

There is no charity on young camels, young lambs, and young calves according to Abu Hanifah

and Muhammad, unless there are adult animals with them. Abu Yusuf said: One young animal of their kind is due.

If the age required is not found among one's animals, the collector takes a higher age and returns the difference, or takes a lower age and collects the difference.

It is permissible to pay the monetary value in zakat. There is no charity on working animals or animals that are fed (i.e., not pasturing).

The collector does not take the best of the wealth nor the worst; he takes the middle quality. One who has a nisab and acquires more of the same type during the year

adds it to his wealth and pays zakat on it together.

Pasturing animals (sa'imah) are those that subsist on grazing for most of their year. If they are fed for half the year or more, there is no zakat on them.

Zakat, according to Abu Hanifah and Abu Yusuf, is due on the nisab amount only, not on the excess. Muhammad said: It is due on both. If the wealth is destroyed after zakat has become obligatory, the zakat obligation lapses. If one pays zakat in advance of the year while possessing the nisab, it is valid.
zakah-bab-5-1:
There is no charity on less than two hundred dirhams. When it is two hundred dirhams and a full year has passed over it, the zakat on it is five dirhams. There is nothing on the excess until it reaches forty dirhams, in which case one dirham is due, then one dirham for every forty dirhams. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Whatever exceeds two hundred, its zakat is proportional.

If the predominant metal in the coins is silver, they are treated as silver. If the predominant metal is base alloy, they are treated as trade goods, and their value must reach a nisab.
zakah-bab-6-1:
There is no charity on less than twenty mithqals of gold. When it is twenty mithqals and a full year has passed over it, the zakat on it is half a mithqal. Then for every four mithqals, the zakat is two qirats. There is nothing on less than four mithqals according to Abu Hanifah.

Zakat is due on gold and silver in their raw form, their ornamental form, and vessels made from them.
zakah-bab-7-1:
Zakat is obligatory on trade goods of whatever kind, when their value reaches a nisab of gold or silver. They are assessed according to whichever is more beneficial for the poor and needy.

If the nisab is complete at both ends of the year, its decrease in between does not cause the zakat to lapse.

The value of trade goods is added to gold and silver. Likewise, gold is added to silver by value to complete the nisab, according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Gold is not added to silver by value; rather, they are added by proportion.
zakah-bab-8-1:
Abu Hanifah, may Allah the Exalted have mercy on him, said: On whatever the earth produces, whether small or large, one-tenth is due equally, whether irrigated by flowing water or by rain, except for firewood, reeds, and grass.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: The one-tenth is not required except on produce that has lasting fruit, when it reaches five wasqs.

A wasq is sixty sa's by the measure of the Prophet, peace be upon him. There is no one-tenth on vegetables according to them.

What is irrigated by a bucket, a water-wheel, or a camel-drawn water device,

on that there is one-twentieth, according to both opinions. Abu Yusuf said: On produce that is not measured by the wasq, such as saffron and cotton, the one-tenth is due when its value reaches five wasqs of the lowest produce that falls under the wasq measure.

Muhammad said: The one-tenth is due when the produce reaches five times the highest unit by which its type is measured. So for cotton he considered five loads, and for saffron five manns. On honey, one-tenth is due when it is taken from land of the one-tenth, whether small or large. Abu Yusuf said: Nothing is due until it reaches ten vessels (azqaq). Muhammad said: Five farqs.

There is no one-tenth on produce from land subject to the kharaj tax.
zakah-bab-9-1:
Allah, the Exalted, said: "Indeed, charitable offerings are only for the poor, the needy..." -- the verse. These are eight categories. The category of those whose hearts are to be reconciled (mu'allafat al-qulub) has been dropped, because Allah, the Exalted, has strengthened Islam and made them unnecessary.

The poor person (faqir) is one who has some meager possessions. The destitute (miskin) is one who has nothing. The collector ('amil) is given by the ruler according to his work, if he works. Regarding freeing of slaves (fi al-riqab): those under a contract of emancipation (mukatab) are assisted in gaining their freedom. The debtor (gharim) is one burdened with debt. In the cause of Allah (fi sabil Allah): stranded warriors. The wayfarer (ibn al-sabil) is one who has wealth in his homeland but is in a place where he has nothing. These are the categories of zakat.

The owner of wealth may distribute to each of them, and he may also restrict it to a single category.

It is not permissible to give zakat to a dhimmi (non-Muslim under protection).

Zakat may not be used to build a mosque, nor to shroud the dead, nor to purchase a slave for emancipation. It may not be given to a wealthy person. The one paying zakat does not give it to his father, grandfather, or anyone above them; nor to his child, grandchild, or anyone below them; nor to his wife. A woman does not give her zakat to her husband, according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: She may give it to him.

He may not give it to his own mukatab (slave under emancipation contract),

his own slave, the slave of a wealthy person, the young child of a wealthy person, nor to the Banu Hashim -- who are: the family of Ali, the family of Abbas, the family of Ja'far, the family of Aqil, and the family of al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib -- and their freed slaves.

Abu Hanifah and Muhammad said: If one gives zakat to a person thinking him poor, and it then becomes apparent that he is wealthy, or a Hashimi, or a non-Muslim, or if one gives in darkness to a poor person and it then becomes apparent that it was one's father or son -- there is no obligation to repeat it.

Abu Yusuf said: He must repeat it.

If one gives to a person and then discovers it was one's own slave or mukatab, it is not valid in the view of all of them.

It is not permissible to give zakat to one who possesses a nisab of any type of wealth. It is permissible to give it to one who possesses less than that, even if he is healthy and able to earn.

It is disliked to transfer zakat from one town to another. Rather, the charity of each people should be distributed among them, unless a person transfers it to his relatives or to people who are more in need than the people of his town.
zakah-bab-10-1:
The charity of Fitr (zakat al-fitr) is obligatory upon the free Muslim who possesses the equivalent of the nisab in excess of his dwelling, clothing, household furnishings, horse, weapons, and service slaves. He pays it on behalf of himself, his minor children, and his owned slaves. He does not pay on behalf of his wife or his adult children, even if they are in his household.

He does not pay on behalf of his mukatab, nor on behalf of slaves held for trade. A slave owned jointly by two partners -- neither of them owes fitr for him. A Muslim master pays the fitr charity on behalf of his non-Muslim slave.

The fitr amount is: half a sa' of wheat, or a full sa' of dates, raisins, or barley.

The sa' according to Abu Hanifah and Muhammad is eight Iraqi ratls.

Abu Yusuf said: Five and one-third ratls.

The obligation of fitr is connected to the rising of dawn on the day of Eid al-Fitr. One who dies before that -- his fitr is not obligatory. One who accepts Islam or is born after the rising of dawn -- his fitr is not obligatory.

It is recommended that the people pay the fitr charity on the day of Eid before going out to the prayer grounds. If they pay it before the day of Eid, it is valid. If they delay it past the day of Eid, it does not lapse and they remain obligated to pay it.
sawm-intro-1-1:
Fasting is of two types: obligatory and voluntary. The obligatory is of two types: one that is connected to a specific time, such as the fast of Ramadan and a specified vow -- its fast is valid with an intention from the night. If one does not intend until the morning,

the second type is what is established as an obligation in general, such as making up missed days of Ramadan, an unspecified vow, and expiations -- these are not valid except with an intention from the night. All voluntary fasts are valid with an intention made before the sun reaches its zenith.

The people should look for the crescent on the twenty-ninth day of Sha'ban. If they see it, they fast. If it is obscured from them, they complete Sha'ban as thirty days and then fast. One who sees the crescent of Ramadan alone fasts even if the ruler does not accept his testimony. If there are clouds in the sky, the ruler accepts the testimony of a single upright person for sighting the crescent, whether male or female, free or slave.

If there are no clouds in the sky, the testimony is not accepted until a large group sees it such that their report produces certainty.

The time of fasting is from the rising of the second dawn until the setting of the sun.
sawm-intro-1-2:
Fasting is: abstaining from eating, drinking, and sexual intercourse during the day with the intention. If the fasting person eats, drinks, or has intercourse forgetfully, his fast is not broken. If he sleeps and has a wet dream, or looks at a woman and ejaculates, or applies oil, or has cupping done, or applies kohl, or kisses without ejaculation, his fast is not broken. But if he ejaculates from a kiss or from touching, he must make up that day.

There is no harm in kissing if one is confident in controlling oneself, and it is disliked if one is not confident. If vomiting overcomes him involuntarily, his fast is not broken. If he deliberately induces vomiting that fills his mouth, he must make up the fast. One who swallows a pebble or piece of iron breaks his fast.

One who deliberately has intercourse in either of the two passages, or deliberately eats or drinks something nourishing or medicinal, must make up the fast and pay an expiation like the expiation for zihar (a specific oath of marital abstention). One who has intercourse short of the private parts and ejaculates must make up the fast, but there is no

expiation on him. There is no expiation for invalidating the fast outside of Ramadan. One who takes an enema, or applies medicine through the nose, or drops medicine into the ear, or treats a deep body wound or a head wound with medicine that reaches his stomach or brain, breaks his fast. If medicine is dropped into the urethra, the fast is not broken according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf said: It breaks the fast.
sawm-intro-1-3:
One who tastes something with his mouth does not break his fast, but it is disliked for him. It is disliked for a woman to chew food for her infant if she can avoid it.

Chewing gum does not break the fast, but it is disliked.

One who is ill during Ramadan and fears that fasting will worsen his illness may break his fast and make it up later. If one is a traveler who is not harmed by fasting, then fasting is better; if he breaks his fast and makes it up later, it is permissible. If the ill person or the traveler dies while still in their respective states, they are not required to make anything up. If the ill person recovers or the traveler settles and then they die, they must make up an amount proportional to their days of health and residence.

The make-up of Ramadan -- if one wishes, one may spread the days out, and if one wishes, one may fast them consecutively. If one delays the make-up until the next Ramadan enters, one fasts the current Ramadan and makes up the first one after it, and there is no redemption payment (fidyah) on him.

The pregnant woman and the nursing woman, if they fear for their children,

may break their fast and make it up, and there is no redemption payment on them.

The very elderly person who is unable to fast may break his fast and feed one poor person for each day, as one feeds in the expiations.

One who dies while owing make-up days of Ramadan and left a bequest for it -- his guardian feeds on his behalf for each day one poor person: half a sa' of wheat, or a full sa' of dates, or a full sa' of barley.
sawm-intro-1-4:
One who enters into a voluntary fast or voluntary prayer and then invalidates it must make it up. If a child reaches puberty or a non-Muslim accepts Islam during Ramadan, they abstain for the rest of their day and fast the days that follow, but they do not make up what has passed.

One who loses consciousness during Ramadan does not make up the day on which the unconsciousness occurred, but makes up the days after it. If an insane person regains sanity during part of Ramadan, he makes up what has passed. When a woman begins menstruating, she breaks her fast and makes it up. When a traveler arrives home or a menstruating woman becomes pure during part of the day, they abstain from food and drink for the rest of their day.

One who eats the pre-dawn meal thinking that dawn had not yet risen, or breaks his fast thinking

that the sun has set, and then it becomes clear that dawn had already risen or that the sun had not set -- he makes up that day, and there is no expiation on him.

One who sees the crescent of Eid alone does not break his fast.

If there are clouds in the sky, the testimony for the crescent of Eid al-Fitr is not accepted except from two men or one man and two women. If there are no clouds in the sky, it is not accepted except from a large group whose report produces certainty.
sawm-bab-1-1:
Spiritual retreat (i'tikaf) is recommended. It is remaining in the mosque accompanied by fasting and the intention of i'tikaf.

It is forbidden for the one in i'tikaf to have sexual intercourse, to touch with desire, or to kiss. He does not leave the mosque except for human necessities or for the Friday prayer. There is no harm in his buying and selling in the mosque as long as the goods are not brought there.

He should only speak what is good, and silence (as a form of worship) is disliked for him. If the one in i'tikaf has sexual intercourse, whether by night or by day, his i'tikaf is invalidated. One who makes i'tikaf for a specified number of days obligatory upon himself must observe i'tikaf for those days and their nights, and they must be consecutive even if he did not stipulate consecutiveness.
hajj-intro-1:
Hajj is obligatory upon free, adult, sane, healthy persons when they are able to afford the provisions and the mount, in excess of their dwelling and what they cannot do without,

and in excess of the expenses of their dependents until their return, and the route is safe.

It is required of a woman that she have a mahram (unmarriageable male relative) to perform hajj with her, or a husband. She may not perform hajj without one of them if the distance between her and Makkah is three days and their nights. If a child reaches puberty after entering ihram, or a slave is emancipated, and they continue in that state, it does not count for them as the obligatory Hajj of Islam.
hajj-bab-1-1-1:
The stations (mawaqit) that a person may not pass without being in a state of ihram are: for the people of Madinah, Dhu al-Hulayfah; for the people of Iraq, Dhat Irq; for the people of Sham (Greater Syria), al-Juhfah;

for the people of Najd, Qarn al-Manazil; and for the people of Yemen, Yalamlam. If one enters ihram before these stations, it is valid.

One whose dwelling is beyond the stations -- his station is the boundary of the sacred territory (al-hill). One who is in Makkah -- his station for Hajj is the Sacred Territory (al-haram), and for Umrah it is the area outside the Sacred Territory (al-hill).

When one intends to enter ihram, he performs a ritual bath or ablution -- the bath being preferable -- puts on two garments, new or washed, a lower wrap (izar) and an upper wrap (rida'), applies perfume if he has any, and prays two units. He says: "O Allah, I intend to perform Hajj, so make it easy for me and accept it from me."

Then he recites the talbiyah following his prayer.

If he is performing Hajj alone (ifrad), he intends Hajj with his talbiyah. The talbiyah is to say: "Labbayk Allahumma labbayk, labbayk la sharika laka labbayk, inna al-hamda wa al-ni'mata laka wa al-mulk, la sharika lak" (Here I am, O Allah, here I am. Here I am, You have no partner, here I am. Indeed, all praise, bounty, and sovereignty are Yours. You have no partner). He should not omit anything from these words, but if he adds to them, it is permissible.
hajj-bab-1-1-2:
Once he has recited the talbiyah, he has entered ihram. He must then avoid what Allah has forbidden of obscenity, sinful conduct, and arguing. He must not kill game, nor point to it, nor guide anyone to it. He must not wear a shirt, trousers, turban, cap, or a long outer garment (qaba'),

nor leather socks -- unless he cannot find sandals, in which case he cuts them below the ankles. He must not cover his head or

his face. He must not apply perfume, shave his head or body hair, trim his beard or nails, or wear a garment dyed with wars (a yellow dye), saffron, or safflower -- unless it has been washed such that the dye no longer runs.

There is no harm in bathing, entering a bathhouse, taking shade under a structure or tent, and tying a money-belt around his waist.

He should not wash his head or beard with marsh mallow. He should frequently recite the talbiyah: after prayers, whenever he ascends a hill or descends a valley, when he meets fellow travelers, and at the pre-dawn time.

When he enters Makkah, he begins with the Sacred Mosque. When he catches sight of the House (Ka'bah), he says "Allahu Akbar" and "La ilaha ill-Allah." Then he goes to the Black Stone, faces it, says "Allahu Akbar," raises his hands, touches it, and kisses it if he is able -- without harming a Muslim. Then he proceeds to his right from the side nearest the door, having already placed his upper garment under his right arm (idtiba') before that, and he circumambulates the House seven circuits.
hajj-bab-1-1-3:
He makes his circumambulation from behind the Hijr (Hatim). He walks briskly (raml) in the first three circuits and walks at his normal pace in the rest. He touches the Black Stone each time he passes it if he is able, and he concludes the circumambulation by touching it.

Then he goes to the Station of Ibrahim (Maqam Ibrahim) and prays two units there, or wherever is convenient in the mosque.

This circumambulation is the circumambulation of arrival (tawaf al-qudum), and it is a sunnah, not obligatory. The people of Makkah do not perform the circumambulation of arrival.

Then he goes out to al-Safa, ascends it, faces the House, says "Allahu Akbar" and "La ilaha ill-Allah," sends blessings upon the Prophet, peace be upon him, and supplicates to Allah, the Exalted, for his needs.

Then he descends toward al-Marwah and walks at his normal pace. When he reaches the bottom of the valley, he hastens between the two green markers at a run until he reaches al-Marwah, ascends it, and does as he did at al-Safa. This is one circuit. He performs seven circuits, beginning at al-Safa and ending at al-Marwah.

Then he stays in Makkah in the state of ihram, circumambulating the House whenever he wishes. On the day before the Day of Tarwiyah (the 7th of Dhul Hijjah), the imam delivers a sermon in which he instructs the people about going to Mina, the prayer at Arafat, the standing, and the departure. When the Fajr prayer is performed on the Day of Tarwiyah in Makkah, he goes out to Mina and stays there until he prays the Fajr prayer on the Day of Arafah.
hajj-bab-1-1-4:
Then he proceeds to Arafat and stays there.

When the sun passes its zenith on the Day of Arafah, the imam prays with the people Zuhr and Asr. Then he begins and delivers two sermons before the prayer, teaching the people therein

about the prayer, the standing at Arafat, Muzdalifah, the stoning of the pillars, the sacrifice, the shaving, and the circumambulation of visitation (tawaf al-ziyarah). He prays Zuhr and Asr with them at the time of Zuhr, with one adhan and two iqamahs. One who prays alone in his camp prays each prayer at its own time, according to Abu Hanifah, may Allah the Exalted have mercy on him.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad, may Allah have mercy on them, said: The individual also combines them. Then he proceeds to the standing place and stands near the mountain. All of Arafat is a valid standing place except the valley of Uranah.
