The imam should stand at Arafat on his mount and supplicate and teach the people the rites. It is recommended to perform the ritual bath before the standing. He should exert himself in supplication. When the sun sets, the imam and the people with him depart in
hajj-bab-1-1-5:
a calm manner until they come to Muzdalifah, where they stop. It is recommended to stop near the hill upon which the fire beacon stands, called Quzah. The imam prays Maghrib and Isha with the people with one adhan and one iqamah. One who prays Maghrib on the road -- it is not valid according to Abu Hanifah and Muhammad.

When dawn rises, the imam prays Fajr with the people at early dawn. Then he stands and the people stand with him and he supplicates. All of Muzdalifah is a valid standing place except the valley of Muhassir.

Then the imam and the people with him depart before sunrise until they come to Mina. He begins with Jamrat al-Aqabah and stones it from the middle of the valley with seven pebbles the size of chickpeas,

saying "Allahu Akbar" with each pebble. He does not stop there. He ceases the talbiyah with the first pebble.

Then he slaughters if he wishes, then shaves his head or trims his hair -- shaving being better. Now everything has become permissible for him except women.
hajj-bab-1-1-6:
Then he comes to Makkah on that day, or the next day, or the day after, and circumambulates the House with seven circuits -- the circumambulation of visitation (tawaf al-ziyarah). If he had already performed the sa'y between al-Safa and al-Marwah after the circumambulation of arrival, he does not walk briskly (raml) in this circumambulation and there is no sa'y upon him. If he had not previously performed the sa'y, he walks briskly in this circumambulation and performs the sa'y after it as we have described. Now women have become permissible for him. This circumambulation is the obligatory one in Hajj. It is disliked to delay it beyond these days, and if it is delayed beyond them, a sacrificial animal is required according to Abu Hanifah.

Then he returns to Mina and stays there. When the sun passes its zenith on the second day of the Days of Sacrifice, he stones the three pillars. He begins with the one nearest the mosque and stones it with seven pebbles, saying "Allahu Akbar" with each pebble, and he stands and supplicates at it. Then he stones the next one in the same manner and stands at it. Then he stones Jamrat al-Aqabah in the same manner but does not stand at it. On the next day, he stones the three pillars after the sun passes its zenith in the same manner. If he wishes to hasten his departure, he leaves for Makkah. If he wishes to remain, he stones the three pillars on the fourth day after the sun passes its zenith. If on this day he stones them before the sun passes its zenith but after the rise of dawn, it is valid according to
hajj-bab-1-1-7:
Abu Hanifah, may Allah have mercy on him. It is disliked for a person to send his luggage ahead to Makkah and stay there until he has completed the stoning. When he departs to Makkah, he stops at al-Muhassab. Then he circumambulates the House seven circuits without walking briskly (raml). This is the farewell circumambulation (tawaf al-sadr), and it is obligatory except for the people of Makkah. Then he returns to his family.

If the one in ihram does not enter Makkah and instead proceeds directly to Arafat and stands there as we have described, the circumambulation of arrival is waived for him and he owes nothing for omitting it.

Whoever catches the standing at Arafat between the declining of the sun on the Day of Arafah and the rising of dawn on the Day of Sacrifice has caught the Hajj.

One who passes through Arafat while asleep, unconscious, or not knowing it was Arafat -- that suffices as his standing.

A woman is in all of this like a man, except that she does not uncover her head, though she uncovers her face. She does not raise her voice in the talbiyah. She does not walk briskly in the circumambulation. She does not hasten between the two green markers. She does not shave her head but rather trims her hair.
hajj-bab-2-1:
Qiran (combining Hajj and Umrah) is superior in our view to Tamattu' and Ifrad (performing each separately).

The manner of Qiran is:

To enter ihram for both Umrah and Hajj together from the stations.

He says after his prayer: "O Allah, I intend Hajj and Umrah, so make them easy for me and accept them from me." When he enters Makkah, he begins by circumambulating the House seven circuits, walking briskly in the first three. He then performs the sa'y between al-Safa and al-Marwah -- these are the rites of Umrah.

Then he circumambulates after the sa'y the circumambulation of arrival and performs the sa'y between al-Safa and al-Marwah as we described for the one performing Hajj alone. When he stones the pillar on the Day of Sacrifice, he slaughters a sheep, or a cow, or a camel, or one-seventh of a camel. This is the sacrificial animal of Qiran.

If he does not have anything to slaughter, he fasts three days during the Hajj, the last of which is the Day of Arafah. If the fasting passes him until the Day of Sacrifice arrives, only the sacrificial animal suffices. Then he fasts seven days when he returns to his family; if he fasts them in Makkah after completing the Hajj, it is valid.

If the one performing Qiran does not enter Makkah and proceeds directly to Arafat, he has abandoned his Umrah by standing there, and the sacrificial animal of Qiran is waived from him. He owes a sacrificial animal for abandoning his Umrah, and he must make it up.
hajj-bab-3-1:
Tamattu' (enjoying the interval between Umrah and Hajj) is superior to Ifrad in our view.

Tamattu' is of two types:

one who drives a sacrificial animal and one who does not drive a sacrificial animal.

The manner of Tamattu' is:

To begin from the station and enter ihram for Umrah. He enters Makkah, circumambulates for it, performs the sa'y, and shaves his head or trims his hair, and he has exited his Umrah.

He ceases the talbiyah when he begins the circumambulation. He stays in Makkah in a state of being out of ihram. When the Day of Tarwiyah comes, he enters ihram for Hajj from the mosque and does what the one performing Hajj alone does. He owes the sacrificial animal of Tamattu'. If he cannot find one, he fasts three days during the Hajj and seven when he returns.

If the one performing Tamattu' wishes to drive a sacrificial animal, he enters ihram and drives his sacrificial animal. If it is a camel, he garlands it with a water-skin or a sandal and marks the camel according to Abu Yusuf and Muhammad -- which is to cut its hump on the right side. He does not mark it according to Abu Hanifah.

When he enters Makkah, he circumambulates and performs the sa'y but does not exit ihram until he enters ihram for Hajj on the Day of Tarwiyah. If he enters ihram before that day, it is valid, and he owes a sacrificial animal. When he shaves on the Day of Sacrifice, he has exited both states of ihram.

The people of Makkah may not perform Tamattu' or Qiran; they may only perform Ifrad.

If the one performing Tamattu' returns to his family after completing his Umrah without having driven a sacrificial animal, his Tamattu' is invalidated.

One who enters ihram for Umrah before the months of Hajj and circumambulates less than four circuits for it, and then the months of Hajj begin and he completes it and then performs Hajj that same year, he is considered a Mutamatti' (performing Tamattu').

If he circumambulated four or more circuits for his Umrah before the months of Hajj and then performs Hajj that same year, he is not considered a Mutamatti'.

The months of Hajj are:

Shawwal, Dhul Qa'dah, and ten days of Dhul Hijjah. If one enters ihram for Hajj before these months, his ihram is valid and counts as Hajj.

When a woman menstruates at the time of entering ihram, she performs the ritual bath, enters ihram, and does everything the pilgrim does,

except that she does not circumambulate the House until she becomes pure. If she menstruates after the standing and the circumambulation of visitation, she departs from Makkah and owes nothing for omitting the farewell circumambulation.
hajj-bab-4-1-1:
If the one in ihram applies perfume, expiation is due upon him. If he perfumes an entire limb or more, a sacrificial animal is due. If he perfumes less than an entire limb, charity is due.

If he wears a sewn garment or covers his head for an entire day, a sacrificial animal is due. If it is less than that, charity is due. If he shaves a quarter of his head or more, a sacrificial animal is due. If he shaves less than a quarter, charity is due.

If he shaves the areas of cupping (on the body), a sacrificial animal is due according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Charity is due. If he clips the nails of both his hands and feet, a sacrificial animal is due.

If he clips the nails of one hand or one foot, a sacrificial animal is due. If he clips fewer than five nails scattered across his hands and feet, charity is due according to Abu Hanifah and Abu Yusuf. Muhammad said: A sacrificial animal is due.

If he applies perfume, shaves, or wears sewn garments out of necessity, he has a choice: if he wishes, he slaughters a sheep; if he wishes, he gives charity to six poor persons of three sa's of food; if he wishes, he fasts three days.

If he kisses or touches with desire, a sacrificial animal is due. One who has intercourse in either of the two passages before the standing at Arafat -- his Hajj is invalidated, a sheep is due upon him, and he continues in his Hajj as one whose Hajj is not invalidated does. He must make it up, and he is not required to separate from his wife when he performs the make-up Hajj with her.
hajj-bab-4-1-2:
One who has intercourse after the standing at Arafat -- his Hajj is not invalidated, and a camel is due upon him. If he has intercourse after shaving, a sheep is due upon him. One who has intercourse during Umrah before completing four circuits -- his Umrah is invalidated, he continues in it and makes it up, and a sheep is due upon him. If he has intercourse after completing four circuits, a sheep is due upon him, his Umrah is not invalidated, and he does not need to make it up. One who has intercourse forgetfully is treated the same as one who does so deliberately.

One who performs the circumambulation of arrival in a state of minor ritual impurity -- charity is due upon him. If he performs it in a state of major ritual impurity, a sheep is due. One who performs the circumambulation of visitation in a state of minor impurity -- a sheep is due. If he was

in a state of major ritual impurity, a camel is due. The best course is to repeat the circumambulation while still in Makkah, in which case nothing is due. One who performs the farewell circumambulation in a state of minor impurity -- charity is due. If he performs it in a state of major impurity, a sheep is due.

One who omits three circuits or fewer from the circumambulation of visitation -- a sheep is due. If he omits four circuits, he remains in ihram permanently until he performs them. One who omits three circuits from the farewell circumambulation -- charity is due. If he omits the entire farewell circumambulation or four circuits of it, a sheep is due.

One who omits the sa'y between al-Safa and al-Marwah -- a sheep is due, and his Hajj is complete.

One who departs from Arafat before the imam -- a sacrificial animal is due upon him.
hajj-bab-4-1-3:
One who omits the standing at Muzdalifah -- a sacrificial animal is due upon him.

One who omits the stoning of the pillars on all the days -- a sacrificial animal is due. If he omits the stoning of a single day, a sacrificial animal is due. If he omits the stoning of one of the three pillars, charity is due. If he omits the stoning of Jamrat al-Aqabah on the Day of Sacrifice, a sacrificial animal is due.

One who delays the shaving until the Days of Sacrifice have passed -- a sacrificial animal is due according to Abu Hanifah. Likewise if one delays the circumambulation of visitation, according to Abu Hanifah, may Allah have mercy on him.

If the one in ihram kills game or guides someone who kills it, the penalty is due. The deliberate and the forgetful, the first-time offender and the repeat offender, are all treated equally in this regard. The penalty according to Abu Hanifah and Abu Yusuf is that the game is appraised at its value in the place where it was killed, or the nearest place to it if it was in the wilderness. It is appraised by two just persons. Then the offender has a choice regarding the value: if he wishes, he purchases a sacrificial animal with it; if he wishes, he buys food with it and gives each poor person half a sa' of wheat or a full sa' of dates or barley; if he wishes, he fasts one day for each half sa' of wheat or each full sa' of barley. If the remaining food is less than half a sa', he has a choice: he may give it in charity or fast one full day for it.
hajj-bab-4-1-4:
Muhammad said: The penalty for game is the like-for-like equivalent where one exists. For a gazelle, a sheep. For a hyena, a sheep. For a rabbit, a young goat ('anaq). For an ostrich, a camel. For a jerboa, a young kid (jafrah). One who wounds game, plucks its hair, or cuts off a limb is liable for its diminishment in value. If he plucks the feathers of a bird or cuts the legs of game such that it can no longer escape, he is liable for its full value. One who breaks the egg of a game bird owes its value. If a chick emerges from the egg dead, he owes the value of the chick alive. There is no penalty for killing a crow, kite, wolf, snake, scorpion, or mouse.

There is nothing due for killing mosquitoes, fleas, or ticks.

One who kills a louse gives whatever charity he wishes. One who kills a locust gives whatever charity he wishes, and a date is worth more than a locust.

One who kills an inedible game animal such as predators and the like owes the penalty, but the value does not exceed a sheep.

If

a predatory animal attacks the one in ihram and he kills it, there is nothing due upon him.
hajj-bab-4-1-5:
If the one in ihram is compelled to eat game meat and kills it, the penalty is due upon him. There is no harm in the one in ihram slaughtering sheep, cattle, camels, chickens, and domesticated ducks. If he kills a domesticated pigeon or a tame gazelle, the penalty is due.

If the one in ihram slaughters game, his slaughtering renders it carrion, and eating it is not permissible. There is no harm in the one in ihram eating game meat that was hunted or slaughtered by a person not in ihram, as long as the one in ihram did not guide him to it or order him to hunt it. Regarding game of the Sacred Territory: if a person not in ihram slaughters it, the penalty is due. If one cuts the grass or trees of the Sacred Territory that are not privately owned and are not cultivated by people, he owes their value.

Everything that a person performing Qiran does of what we have mentioned as requiring a sacrificial animal from the one performing Hajj alone -- he owes two sacrificial animals: one for his Hajj and one for his Umrah. The exception is passing the station without ihram and then entering ihram for Umrah and Hajj, in which case only one sacrificial animal is due. If two people in ihram jointly kill game, each of them owes the full penalty. If two people not in ihram jointly kill game of the Sacred Territory, they owe one penalty between them.

If the one in ihram sells or purchases game, the sale is void.
hajj-bab-5-1:
If the one in ihram is obstructed by an enemy or afflicted with an illness that prevents him from continuing, he may exit ihram. He is told: Send a sheep to be slaughtered in the Sacred Territory, and arrange with the one carrying it a specific day on which to slaughter it, then exit ihram. If he was performing Qiran, he sends two sacrificial animals.

The sacrificial animal for obstruction may only be slaughtered in the Sacred Territory. It may be slaughtered before the Day of Sacrifice according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad, may Allah have mercy on them, said: The slaughter for one obstructed from Hajj is not valid except on the Day of Sacrifice.

One obstructed from Umrah may slaughter whenever he wishes. The one obstructed from Hajj, if he exits ihram,

owes a Hajj and an Umrah. The one obstructed from Umrah owes a make-up. The one performing Qiran owes a Hajj and two Umrahs.

If the obstructed person sends his sacrificial animal and arranges for it to be slaughtered on a specific day, and then the obstruction is removed: if he can catch both the sacrificial animal and the Hajj, he may not exit ihram and must continue. If he can catch the sacrificial animal but not the Hajj, he exits ihram. If he can catch the Hajj but not the sacrificial animal, he may exit ihram as a matter of juristic preference (istihsan).

One who is obstructed in Makkah itself and is prevented from both the standing and the circumambulation is considered obstructed. If he is able to do one of them, he is not considered obstructed.
hajj-bab-6-1:
One who enters ihram for Hajj and then misses the standing at Arafat until the dawn rises

on the Day of Sacrifice -- the Hajj has passed him by. He must circumambulate, perform the sa'y, and exit ihram, and make up the Hajj the following year. No sacrificial animal is due upon him.

The Umrah cannot be missed. It is valid throughout the entire year, except for five days during which it is disliked: the Day of Arafah, the Day of Sacrifice, and the three Days of Tashriq.

The Umrah is a sunnah. It consists of: entering ihram, circumambulating, performing the sa'y, and shaving or trimming the hair.
hajj-bab-7-1:
The sacrificial animal (hady): its minimum is a sheep.

It is of three types: camels, cattle, and sheep. What suffices is a thaniyy (two-year-old) or older, except for sheep, where a jadha' (six-month-old) suffices. The following are not acceptable as sacrificial animals: one whose ear is cut off or mostly cut off, one whose tail is cut off, one missing a leg, one that has lost an eye, one that is emaciated, or one that is lame and cannot walk to the place of slaughter. A sheep is acceptable for everything except two situations: one who performs the circumambulation of visitation in a state of major impurity, and one who has intercourse after the standing at Arafat -- for these nothing less than a camel suffices.

A camel or a cow each suffices for seven persons, if each

of the partners intends an act of worship. If one of them intends by his share only meat, it does not count for the rest. It is permissible to eat from the sacrificial animal of voluntary offering, Tamattu', and Qiran.

It is not permissible to eat from the remaining types of sacrificial animals. It is not permissible to slaughter the voluntary sacrificial animal,

the Tamattu' animal, or the Qiran animal except on the Day of Sacrifice. The remaining sacrificial animals may be slaughtered at any time. The sacrificial animals may only be slaughtered in the Sacred Territory. They may be given in charity to the poor of the Sacred Territory and others. The sacrificial animals need not be publicly identified. The preferred method for camels is to slaughter them while standing (nahr), and for cattle and sheep, while lying down (dhabh).

It is preferable for a person to slaughter his own sacrificial animal if he knows how. He gives its coverings and halter in charity and does not give any of it as the butcher's fee.

One who drives a camel as a sacrificial animal and is compelled to ride it may ride it; if he has no need, he should not ride it. If the camel has milk, he should not milk it; rather, he should splash cold water on its udder until the milk dries up.

One who drives a sacrificial animal and it becomes injured: if it was a voluntary offering, nothing else is due upon him. If it was for an obligation, he must replace it. If it develops a significant defect, he replaces it

and does with the defective one as he wishes. If the sacrificial camel becomes injured on the road and it was a voluntary offering, he slaughters it, dips its sandal in its blood, strikes its flank with it, and neither he nor any wealthy person eats from it. If it was obligatory, he replaces it and does with it as he wishes. The voluntary, Tamattu', and Qiran sacrificial animals are garlanded, but the sacrificial animal for obstruction and for violations is not garlanded.
buyu-intro-1-1:
A sale is concluded by offer (ijab) and acceptance (qabul) when both are expressed in the past tense.

When one of the two contracting parties makes an offer of sale, the other has the option: if he wishes, he may accept in the contractual session, and if he wishes, he may reject it. If either of them leaves the session before acceptance, the offer becomes void. Once the offer and acceptance have occurred, the sale becomes binding, and neither party has any option except on account of a defect or lack of prior inspection. Goods that are physically present and pointed to do not require knowledge of their exact quantity for the validity of the sale. Prices stated in absolute terms are not valid unless they are known in quantity and description.

A sale may be concluded with a price payable immediately or on a deferred basis, provided the term is known. Whoever states the price in a sale without specifying the currency, it is understood to be the prevailing currency of the locality. If the currencies in circulation differ, the sale is defective unless one of them is specified.

The sale of foodstuffs and grains is permissible by measure, by estimate, by a specific vessel whose capacity is unknown, or by the weight of a specific stone whose weight is unknown. If someone sells a heap of food at a rate of one dirham per qafiz, the sale is valid for one qafiz only according to Abu Hanifa, unless the total number of qafiz is specified. If someone sells a flock of sheep at one dirham per sheep, the sale is defective for all of them. Likewise, if someone sells a cloth by the cubit at one dirham per cubit.
buyu-intro-1-2:
per dirham and does not specify the total number of cubits. If someone buys a heap on the basis that it is one qafiz for one hundred dirhams and finds it to be less, the buyer has the option: if he wishes, he may take what is found at its proportionate share of the price, and if he wishes, he may rescind the sale. If he finds it to be more, the excess belongs to the seller. If someone buys a cloth on the basis that it is ten cubits for ten dirhams, or land on the basis that it is one hundred cubits for one hundred dirhams, and finds it to be less, the buyer has the option: if he wishes, he may take it for the full price, and if he wishes, he may leave it. If he finds it to be more than the cubits specified, it belongs to the buyer and the seller has no option. If the seller said: "I sell it to you on the basis that it is one hundred cubits for one hundred dirhams, each cubit for one dirham," and it is found to be less, the buyer has the option: if he wishes, he may take it at its proportionate share of the price, and if he wishes, he may leave it. If it is found

to be more, the buyer has the option: if he wishes, he may take the entirety at one dirham per cubit, and if he wishes, he may rescind the sale. Whoever sells a house, its structure is included in the sale even if it is not mentioned.
buyu-intro-1-3:
Whoever sells land, what is in it is included even if it is not mentioned. However, crops are not included in the sale of land except by explicit mention. Whoever sells palm trees or trees bearing fruit, the fruit belongs to the seller unless the buyer stipulates it. The seller is told: harvest it and deliver the sold property. Whoever sells fruit whose ripeness has not yet appeared, or has appeared, the sale is valid and the buyer is required to pick it immediately. If he stipulates leaving it on the palm tree, the sale becomes defective.

It is not permissible to sell fruit while excluding a known number of ratls from it. The sale of wheat in its ears and broad beans in their pods is permissible. Whoever sells a house, the keys to its locks are included in the sale. The cost of the measurer and the assayer of the price is upon the seller, and the cost of the weigher of the price is upon the buyer. If someone sells a commodity for a price, the buyer is told: pay the price first; then when he pays, the seller is told: deliver the sold property. If someone sells a commodity for another commodity, or currency for currency, both are told: deliver simultaneously.
buyu-bab-1-1:
The option of stipulation (khiyar al-shart) is permissible in sales for both the seller and the buyer. They may stipulate the option for three days or less, and it is not permissible for more than that according to Abu Hanifa (may Allah have mercy on him). Abu Yusuf and Muhammad (may Allah have mercy on them) said: it is permissible if a known period is specified.

The seller's option prevents the sold property from leaving his ownership. If the buyer takes possession of it and it perishes in his hands, he is liable for its value. The buyer's option does not prevent the sold property from leaving the seller's ownership, except that the buyer does not own it according to Abu Hanifa. According to them [Abu Yusuf and Muhammad], he does own it. If it perishes in his hands, it perishes at the expense of the price. Likewise if a defect occurs in it. Whoever has been given the option may rescind during the option period or may ratify it. If he ratifies it in the absence of the other party, it is valid. If he rescinds, it is not valid unless the other party is present. If the one who has the option dies, his option is extinguished and does not pass to his heirs.

If someone sells a slave on the condition that he is a baker or a scribe, and he turns out to be otherwise, the buyer has the option: if he wishes, he may take him for the full price, and if he wishes, he may return him.
buyu-bab-2-1:
Whoever buys something he has not seen, the sale is valid.

He has the option when he sees it: if he wishes, he may take it, and if he wishes, he may return it. Whoever sells what he has not seen has no option. Whoever looks at the surface of a heap, or the exterior of a folded cloth, or the face of a slave-girl, or the face and rump of an animal, has no option. If he sees the courtyard of a house, he has no option even if he has not inspected

its rooms. The sale and purchase of a blind person are valid, and he has the option when he buys. His option lapses if he touches the sold property when it is something recognized by touch, or smells it when it is something recognized by smell, or tastes it when it is something recognized by taste. His option in real property does not lapse until it is described to him.

If someone sells another's property without his authorization, the owner has the option: if he wishes, he may ratify the sale, and if he wishes, he may rescind it. He may ratify it as long as the subject of the contract is still in existence and the contracting parties are in their original state.

If someone sees one of two cloths and buys them both, then sees the other, he may return them both.

If someone dies while having the option of inspection, his option is extinguished. If someone sees a thing and then buys it after a period of time: if it is in the condition in which he saw it, he has no option; if he finds it changed, he has the option.
buyu-bab-3-1:
When the buyer discovers a defect in the sold property, he has the option: if he wishes, he may take it for the full price, and if he wishes, he may return it. He is not entitled to keep it and claim a reduction.

Every defect that causes a reduction in the price in the custom of merchants is considered a defect. Running away, bedwetting, and stealing are defects in a minor as long as he has not reached puberty. Once he reaches puberty,

these are not considered defects unless they recur after puberty. Bad breath and body odor are defects in a slave-girl but not in a male slave, unless they arise from an illness. Fornication and being born of fornication are defects in a slave-girl but not in a male slave.

If a defect arises with the buyer and then a prior defect from the seller's time is discovered, the buyer may claim a reduction for the defect but may not return the property, unless the seller agrees to take it back with its defect. If the buyer cuts a cloth and then finds a defect, he may claim the defect's reduction. If he sews it, dyes it, or mixes meal with ghee and then discovers a defect, he may claim its reduction, but the seller is not entitled to take it back. If someone buys a slave and then manumits him, or the slave dies, and then a defect is discovered, he may claim its reduction. If the buyer kills the slave, or if it was food and he ate it, he cannot claim anything against the seller according to Abu Hanifa. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: he may claim.

If someone buys a slave and then resells him, and the slave is returned to him due to a defect: if the return was by judicial decree, then he may return it to his seller; but if the return was without judicial decree, he may not return it to his seller.

If someone buys a slave and stipulates exemption from all defects, he may not return him for any defect, even if the defects were not specified or enumerated.
buyu-bab-4-1-1:
If one or both of the counter-values is prohibited, the sale is defective, such as a sale involving carrion, blood, wine, or pork. Likewise, if the subject is not owned, such as a free person. The sale of an umm walad (mother of a child), a mudabbar (conditionally manumitted slave), and a mukatab (contractually manumitted slave) is defective. It is not permissible to sell fish in water, birds in the air, an unborn fetus, the offspring of offspring, milk in the udder, wool on the backs of sheep, a cubit from a cloth, a beam in a ceiling, the catch of a hunter, or a muzabanah sale -- which is selling fruit on the tops of palm trees estimated as dried dates. A sale by throwing a stone or by touch is not permissible.

It is not permissible to sell one cloth from two cloths.

If someone sells a slave on condition that the buyer manumit him, grant him tadbir, enter a kitabah contract with him, or sells a slave-girl on condition that the buyer take her as an umm walad, the sale is defective. Likewise if he sells a slave on condition that the seller employ him for a month, or a house

on condition that the seller reside in it, or on condition that the buyer lend him a dirham, or give him a gift.

If someone sells a specific item on condition that he not deliver it until the beginning of the month, the sale is defective. If someone sells a slave-girl excluding her pregnancy, the sale is defective.
buyu-bab-4-1-2:
If someone buys a cloth on condition that the seller cut and sew it into a shirt or a coat, or sandals on condition that the seller shape and strap them, the sale is defective. A sale with payment deferred until Nawruz, Mihrajan, the Christian fast, or the Jewish festival -- if

the two parties do not know when those dates fall, the sale is defective.

A sale with payment deferred until harvest time, threshing, grape-picking, or the arrival of pilgrims is not permissible. But if they mutually agree to waive the deferral before people begin harvesting, threshing, or before the pilgrims arrive, the sale becomes valid.

When the buyer takes possession of the sold property in a defective sale with the seller's permission, and the contract involves two counter-values each of which is property, he acquires ownership of the sold item and is liable for its value. Either party may rescind it. If the buyer resells it, his sale is effective.

If someone combines a free person and a slave, or a lawfully slaughtered sheep and carrion, in one sale, the sale is void in both. If someone combines a slave and a mudabbar, or his own slave and someone else's slave, the contract is valid for the [permissible] slave at its proportionate share of the price.

The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) prohibited bid-rigging (najash), bidding over another's bid, intercepting goods before they reach the market, a resident selling on behalf of a desert-dweller, and selling at the time of the Friday call to prayer. All of these are disliked but do not invalidate the contract.
buyu-bab-4-1-3: If someone owns two young slaves, one of whom is a close relative (mahram) of the other, they should not be separated. Likewise if one of them is an adult and the other is young. If he separates them, it is disliked but the sale is valid. If both are adults, there is no objection to separating them.
buyu-bab-5-1:
Rescission (iqalah) is permissible in sales.

It is at the original price. If a lesser or greater amount is stipulated, the stipulation is void and the original price is returned. It is considered a rescission with respect to the contracting parties and a new sale with respect to third parties, according to Abu Hanifa.

The destruction of the price does not prevent the validity of the rescission, but the destruction of the sold property does prevent it. If part of the sold property is destroyed, rescission is valid for the remainder.
buyu-bab-6-1:
Murabahah (cost-plus sale) is the transfer of what one acquired by the original contract at the original price

plus an additional profit.

Tawliyah (transfer sale) is the transfer of what one acquired by the original contract at the original price without any additional profit.

Neither murabahah nor tawliyah is valid unless the counter-value is something that has a like (fungible). It is permissible to add to the capital cost the fees for bleaching, dyeing, embroidery, twisting, and the cost of transporting food, but one must say: "It cost me such-and-such," and not say: "I bought it for such-and-such."

If the buyer discovers fraud in the murabahah, he has the option according to Abu Hanifa: if he wishes, he may take it for the full price, and if he wishes, he may return it. If he discovers fraud in the tawliyah, the buyer deducts it from the price.

Abu Yusuf said: the deduction applies in both cases. Muhammad said: it does not apply in either case.

Whoever buys something that is movable may not resell it until he takes possession of it. The sale of real property before taking possession is permissible according to Abu Hanifa and Abu Yusuf. Muhammad said: it is not permissible.

Whoever buys a measured commodity by measure or a weighed commodity by weight, then measures or weighs it, and then resells it by measure or by weight, the second buyer may not resell or consume it until he repeats the measuring or weighing. Disposal of the price before taking possession is permissible.

The buyer may increase the price for the seller, and the seller may increase the sold goods for the buyer. A reduction of the price is permissible, and the right of recovery attaches to all of that.

Whoever sells at an immediate price and then grants a known deferral, it becomes deferred. Every immediate debt, if its creditor grants a deferral, becomes deferred -- except for a loan, for deferral of a loan is not valid.
buyu-bab-7-1:
Usury (riba) is prohibited. Every measured or weighed commodity, when sold for its own kind in unequal amounts -- the operative cause therein is measurement combined with genus, or weight combined with genus. When a measured or weighed commodity is sold for its own kind in equal amounts, the sale is permissible; if they differ in amount, it is not permissible.

It is not permissible to sell high quality for low quality of items subject to riba except in equal amounts. When both attributes -- genus and the factor joined to it -- are absent, excess and deferral are both permissible. When both are present, excess and deferral are both prohibited. When one is present and the other absent, excess is permissible but deferral is prohibited.

Everything on which the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) specifically prohibited excess by measure is measured forever, even if people abandon measuring it, such as wheat, barley, dates, and salt. Everything on which he specifically prohibited excess by weight is weighed forever, such as gold and silver. What he did not specifically mention is governed by the custom of people.

The contract of currency exchange (sarf) applies to the genus of monetary prices. It requires the taking of possession of both counter-values in the session. Everything else subject to riba requires designation but does not require mutual taking of possession. It is not permissible to sell wheat for flour or for parched grain (sawiq).

The sale of meat for a live animal is permissible according to Abu Hanifa and Abu Yusuf. Muhammad said: it is not permissible unless the meat in the live animal is less than the meat that is the subject of the contract.

The sale of fresh dates for dried dates in equal amounts is permissible, and grapes for raisins. It is not permissible to sell olives for olive oil, or sesame for sesame oil,

unless the oil is more than what is in the olives or sesame, so that the oil is matched by its like and the excess corresponds to the residue.

The sale of different types of meat for each other in unequal amounts is permissible. Likewise, cow's milk and sheep's milk, and date vinegar for grape vinegar. The sale of bread for wheat and ghee in unequal amounts is permissible.

There is no riba between a master and his slave, nor between a Muslim and a harbi (enemy combatant) in the abode of war.
buyu-bab-8-1:
Forward sale (salam) is permissible in measured commodities, weighed commodities, and countable items that do not vary -- such as walnuts and eggs -- and in measured lengths. Forward sale is not permissible in live animals, their parts, hides by number, firewood by bundles, or fresh fodder by bunches.

Forward sale is not valid unless the subject of the salam is available from the time of the contract until the time of maturity. Forward sale is only valid if deferred, and it is not valid except with a known term. Forward sale is not permissible using the measure of a specific person or the cubit of a specific person,

nor for the food of a specific village, nor the fruit of a specific palm tree.

Forward sale is not valid according to Abu Hanifa except with seven conditions that are mentioned in the contract: a known genus, a known type, a known description, a known term, knowledge of the amount of the capital (ra's al-mal) if it is of a type whose contract depends on its quantity -- such as measured, weighed, or counted items -- specification of the place of delivery if the goods have bulk and transport cost.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: specifying the capital is not needed if it is identified, nor is specifying the place of delivery needed -- it is delivered at the place of the contract.

Forward sale is not valid until the capital is received before the parties separate.

Disposal of the capital or the subject of the salam before taking possession is not permissible. Partnership or transfer in the subject of the salam before taking possession is not permissible.

Forward sale in cloths is permissible if length, width, and weave are specified. Forward sale in gems and beads is not permissible. There is no objection to forward sale in bricks and tiles if a known mold is specified.

Everything whose description can be precisely determined and whose quantity can be known is permissible for forward sale. What cannot be precisely described or whose quantity cannot be known is not permissible for forward sale.

The sale of dogs, cheetahs, and predatory animals is permissible. The sale of wine and pork is not permissible. The sale of silkworms is not permissible unless they are with the silk, nor bees unless they are with their hives.

The People of the Covenant (ahl al-dhimmah) are like Muslims in sales, except regarding wine and pork specifically. Their contract over wine is like a Muslim's contract over grape juice, and their contract over pork is like a Muslim's contract over sheep.
buyu-bab-9-1-1:
Currency exchange (sarf) is a sale in which both counter-values are from the genus of monetary prices. If silver is sold for silver, or gold for gold, it is not permissible except in equal amounts, even if they differ in quality and craftsmanship. Taking possession of both counter-values before separation is mandatory.

If gold is sold for silver, excess is permissible but mutual taking of possession is required. If the parties separate in a currency exchange before taking possession of both counter-values or one of them, the contract is void.

Disposal of the price of a currency exchange before taking possession is not permissible.

The sale of gold for silver by estimate is permissible.

If someone sells an ornamented sword for one hundred dirhams and its ornament is worth fifty dirhams, and he receives fifty from the price, the sale is valid and what is received counts as the share for the silver, even if that was not specified. Likewise if the seller says: "Take these fifty from the price of both." If they do not take mutual possession before separating, the contract is void regarding the ornament. If the sword cannot be separated [from the ornament] without damage, the contract is void for the sword as well. If it can be separated without damage, the sale of the sword is valid

and void for the ornament.
buyu-bab-9-1-2:
If someone sells a silver vessel and they separate having received only part of its price, the contract is void for the portion not received and valid for the portion received, and the vessel is shared between them. If part of the vessel is claimed by a third party (istihqaq), the buyer has the option: if he wishes, he takes the remainder at its proportionate share of the price, and if he wishes, he returns it.

If someone sells a silver ingot and part is claimed by a third party, the buyer takes what remains at its proportionate share and has no option. If someone sells two dirhams and a dinar for two dinars and a dirham, the sale is valid, with each genus matched against the other genus.

If someone sells eleven dirhams for ten dirhams and a dinar, the sale is valid: the ten are matched against their like, and the dinar is matched against one dirham.

The sale of two sound dirhams and one defective dirham for one

sound dirham and two defective dirhams is permissible.

When the predominant content of dirhams is silver, they are treated as silver. When the predominant content of dinars is gold, they are treated as gold. The prohibition of excess applies to them as it applies to pure coins. If the predominant content is base metal, they are not treated as dirhams or dinars.

If they are sold for their own kind in unequal amounts, it is permissible. If someone buys a commodity with such coins and then they become obsolete and people cease transacting with them, the sale is void according to Abu Hanifa.
buyu-bab-9-1-3:
Abu Yusuf said: the buyer owes their value on the day of the sale. Muhammad said: the buyer owes their value as of the last day people transacted with them.

Sale with circulating copper coins (fulus) is permissible even if they are not specified. If they are obsolete, sale with them is not permissible until they are specified. If someone sells with circulating copper coins and they subsequently become obsolete, the sale is void according to Abu Hanifa.

If someone buys something for half a dirham in copper coins, the sale is valid and he owes what is sold for half a dirham in copper coins.

If someone gives the money changer a dirham and says: "Give me copper coins for half of it and a half-dirham coin less a grain for the other half," the sale is void for the entire amount according to Abu Hanifa. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: the sale is valid for the copper coins and void for the remainder. If he says: "Give me half a dirham in copper coins and a half-dirham coin less a grain," the sale is valid and the copper coins and the half-dirham less a grain are together for one dirham.
rahn-main-1-1:
The pledge (rahn) is concluded by offer and acceptance and is completed by taking possession. When the pledgee takes possession of the pledge in an exclusive, unencumbered, and distinguished manner, the contract therein is completed. As long as he has not taken possession, the pledgor has the option: if he wishes, he may deliver it, and if he wishes, he may retract the pledge. Once the pledgor delivers it and the pledgee takes possession, it enters into his guarantee.

The pledge is not valid except for a guaranteed debt. It is guaranteed for the lesser of its value or the debt. If it perishes in the pledgee's hands and its value equals the debt, the pledgee is deemed to have recovered his debt constructively. If the value of the pledge is greater than the debt, the excess is a trust in his hands. If it is less, the corresponding amount of the debt is extinguished, and the pledgee has recourse for the remainder.

Pledging an undivided share (musha') is not permissible, nor is pledging fruit on palm trees without the trees, nor crops in the ground without the ground. Pledging the land and palms without the fruit or crops is not valid. The pledge is not valid for trust obligations (amanat) such as deposits, mudarabah capital, and partnership capital.

The pledge is valid for the capital of a salam contract, the price in a currency exchange, and the subject of a salam. If it perishes during the contractual session, the currency exchange and salam are completed and the pledgee is deemed to have recovered his debt.
rahn-main-1-2:
If the parties agree to place the pledge in the hands of a trustee, it is permissible. Neither the pledgee nor the pledgor may take it from his hands. If it perishes in his hands, it perishes at the expense of the pledgee.

Pledging dirhams, dinars, measured commodities, and weighed commodities is permissible. If they are pledged against their own kind and perish, they perish at their like-value from the debt, even if they differ in quality and craftsmanship.

If someone is owed a debt by another and takes from him the equivalent of his debt and spends it, then discovers it was counterfeit, he has no recourse according to Abu Hanifa. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: he returns the counterfeit equivalent and claims the genuine coins. If someone pledges two slaves for one thousand

dirhams and repays the share attributable to one of them, he is not entitled to retrieve him until he pays the remainder of the debt.

If the pledgor authorizes the pledgee, the trustee, or another person to sell the pledge when the debt falls due, the authorization is valid. If it was stipulated in the pledge contract, the pledgor may not revoke it. If he revokes it, the agent is not dismissed. If the pledgor dies, the agent is not dismissed.

The pledgee may demand payment from the pledgor and have him imprisoned for it. If the pledge is in his hands, he is not required to let the pledgor sell it until he pays the debt from its proceeds. If the debt is paid, the pledgee is told: deliver the pledge to him.
rahn-main-1-3:
If the pledgor sells the pledge without the pledgee's permission, the sale is suspended (mawquf). If the pledgee approves it, the sale is valid. If the pledgor pays the debt, the sale is valid.

If the pledgor manumits the pledged slave, his manumission is effective. If the debt is immediately due, the pledgor is required to pay the debt. If the debt is deferred, the value of the slave is taken from him and held as a pledge in his place until the debt falls due. If the pledgor is insolvent, the slave is required to work to earn his value, and the debt is paid from it.

Likewise if the pledgor destroys the pledge. If a third party destroys it, the pledgee is the one who pursues compensation from him. He takes the value, which becomes a pledge in his hands. The pledgor's damage to the pledge is compensable. The pledgee's damage to it extinguishes from his debt the corresponding amount. The pledge's damage to the pledgor, the pledgee, or their property is void (not compensable).

The rent of the room in which the pledge is stored is upon the pledgee. The cost of the shepherd and the maintenance of the pledge are upon the pledgor. Its growth

belongs to the pledgor but becomes a pledge along with the original.
rahn-main-1-4:
If the growth perishes, it perishes without any obligation. If the original perishes and the growth remains, the pledgor redeems it at its share. The debt is apportioned between the value of the pledge on the day of taking possession and the value of the growth on the day of redemption. What corresponds to the original is extinguished from the debt, and what corresponds to the growth the pledgor redeems.

An increase in the pledged item is permissible, but an increase in the debt is not according to Abu Hanifa and Muhammad, and the pledge does not become pledged for both debts. Abu Yusuf said: an increase in the debt is also permissible.

If someone pledges a single item with two persons for a debt owed to each of them, it is valid. The entire item is pledged with each of them. The guaranteed amount from each is in proportion to his debt. If one of them is paid his debt, the entire item remains pledged with the other until he recovers his debt.

If someone sells a slave on the condition that the buyer pledge a specific item as security for the price, and the buyer refuses to deliver the pledge, he is not compelled to do so. The seller then has the option: if he wishes, he may accept the omission of the pledge, and if he wishes, he may rescind the sale -- unless the buyer pays the price immediately or delivers the value of the pledge as a substitute.

The pledgee may safeguard the pledge himself and through his wife,
rahn-main-1-5:
his child, and his servant who is in his household. If he entrusts its safekeeping to someone outside his household, or deposits it, he is liable.

If the pledgee transgresses with respect to the pledge, he is liable for it with the liability of usurpation for its full value. If the pledgee lends the pledge to the pledgor and the pledgor takes possession of it, it exits the pledgee's guarantee. If it perishes in the pledgor's hands, it perishes without obligation. The pledgee may reclaim it. If he takes it back, the guarantee returns.

If the pledgor dies, his executor sells the pledge and pays the debt. If he has no executor, the judge appoints one for him and orders the sale.
hajr-main-1-1:
The causes necessitating legal incapacity (hajr) are three:

Minority, slavery, and insanity. The disposal of a minor is not valid except with the permission of his guardian. The disposal of a slave is not valid except with the permission of his master. The disposal of an insane person who is overwhelmed in his intellect is never valid.

If any of these persons

sells or buys something while understanding the sale and intending it, the guardian has the option: if he wishes, he may ratify it if there is benefit in it, and if he wishes, he may annul it.

These three attributes necessitate incapacity in statements but not in actions. The child and the insane person -- their contracts and acknowledgments are not valid, and their divorce and manumission do not take effect. If they destroy something, they are liable for compensation.

As for the slave, his statements are effective with respect to himself but not with respect to his master. If he acknowledges a financial obligation, it becomes binding upon him after freedom but not in the present. If he acknowledges a hadd punishment or retaliation, it is binding upon him in the present. His divorce is effective.

Abu Hanifa said: legal interdiction is not imposed on a spendthrift (safih) if he is an adult, sane, and free. His disposal of his property is valid even if he is wasteful and destructive, squandering his wealth in what has no purpose or benefit for him. However, he said: if a boy reaches puberty without being mature, his property is not handed over to him until he reaches twenty-five years of age.
hajr-main-1-2:
If he disposes of it before that, his disposal is effective. When he reaches twenty-five years, his property is handed over to him even if maturity is not discerned in him.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: interdiction is imposed on the spendthrift and he is prevented from disposing of his property. If he sells, his sale is not effective. If there is benefit in it, the judge may ratify it. If he manumits a slave, his manumission is effective, and the slave must work to earn his value. If he marries a woman, his marriage is valid. If he specifies a dower for her, whatever equals the dower of her peers is valid,

and the excess is void.

They [Abu Yusuf and Muhammad] said regarding one who reaches puberty without being mature: his property is never handed over to him until maturity is discerned in him, and his disposal of it is not valid. Zakat is taken out of the spendthrift's property.

Maintenance is provided from his property for his children, his wife, and those whose maintenance is obligatory upon him from among his close relatives. If he wishes to perform the obligatory Hajj, he is not prevented from it. The judge does not hand the expenses over to him but rather hands them to a trustworthy person among the pilgrims who spends on him during the journey. If he falls ill and makes bequests for charitable causes and good works, that is valid up to one-third of his property.

The puberty of a boy is established by nocturnal emission, impregnation, and ejaculation upon intercourse. If none of these occur, then upon completing eighteen years of age according to Abu Hanifa.
hajr-main-1-3:
The puberty of a girl is established by menstruation, nocturnal emission, and pregnancy. If none of these occur, then upon completing seventeen years of age. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: when a boy or a girl completes fifteen years of age, they have reached puberty.

When a boy and a girl reach adolescence and their puberty is uncertain, and they say: "We have reached puberty," their word is accepted and they are treated as adults.

Abu Hanifa said: I do not impose interdiction for debt. If debts are owed by a man and his creditors seek his imprisonment and interdiction, I do not impose interdiction on him. If he has property, the judge does not dispose of it but rather imprisons him indefinitely until he sells it to pay his debt. If he has dirhams and his debt is in dirhams, the judge pays it without his consent. If his debt is in dirhams and he has dinars, the judge sells them to pay his debt.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: if the creditors of an insolvent person seek interdiction upon him, the judge

imposes interdiction, prevents him from selling, disposing, and making acknowledgments so as not to harm the creditors, and sells his property if he refuses to sell it, distributing it among his creditors proportionally. If he makes an acknowledgment during interdiction, it becomes binding upon him after the debts are paid.
hajr-main-1-4:
Maintenance is provided for the insolvent person from his property, and for his wife, minor children, and close relatives. If the insolvent is not known to have property and his creditors seek his imprisonment while he says: "I have no property," the judge imprisons him for every debt he assumed as a substitute for property that came into his possession -- such as the price of a sold item and a loan replacement -- and for every debt arising from a contract, such as dower and suretyship. He does not imprison him for anything else, such as compensation for usurped property and tortious damages, unless evidence is established that he has property.

When the judge imprisons him

for two or three months, he inquires about his condition. If no property is discovered, he releases him. Likewise if he establishes evidence that he has no property. After his release from prison, a barrier is not placed between him and his creditors. They may accompany him but may not prevent him from transacting or traveling. They take the surplus of his earnings, which is divided among them proportionally.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: when the judge declares him insolvent, a barrier is placed between him and his creditors unless they establish evidence that property has come into his possession.

Interdiction is not imposed on a morally corrupt person if he manages his property well. Original and subsequent moral corruption are treated equally. If someone becomes insolvent and specific merchandise purchased from a seller is found with him, the seller shares equally with the other creditors in it.
ijarah-main-1-1:
Hiring (ijarah) is a contract over benefits in exchange for compensation.

It is not valid unless the benefits are known and the rent is known. Whatever may serve as a price in a sale may serve as rent in a hiring contract.

Benefits sometimes become known by duration -- such as hiring houses for residence and land for farming -- so the contract is valid for any known period, whatever it may be. They sometimes become known by the work and its specification -- such as hiring a man to dye a cloth or sew it, or hiring an animal to carry a known quantity or to ride it a specified distance. They sometimes become known by designation and pointing -- such as hiring a man to transport this food to a known place.

Hiring houses and shops for residence is permissible even without specifying what will be done in them. The tenant may do anything except blacksmithing, laundering, and milling.

Hiring land for farming is permissible, but the contract is not valid unless the crop to be planted is specified, or the tenant says: "to plant whatever I wish."
ijarah-main-1-2:
It is permissible to hire an open plot to build on or to plant palm trees or trees in it. When the hire period expires, the tenant is required to remove the buildings and plantings and return the land vacant -- unless the landowner chooses to pay him the value of those items uprooted and thereby own them, or agrees to leave them as they are, with the buildings belonging to one party and the land to the other.

Hiring riding animals for riding and carrying is permissible. If the hirer says: "So-and-so will ride it" or "So-and-so will wear the garment," and a different person rides it or wears it, the hirer is liable if the animal is injured or the garment is ruined.

Likewise for everything that varies with the user.

As for real property

and what does not vary with the user, there is no liability. If one-person residence is stipulated, another person may reside instead. If a specific type or amount of load for the animal is specified -- such as: "five qafiz of wheat" -- the hirer may load what is equivalent to wheat in terms of harm or less, such as barley and sesame. He may not load what is more harmful than wheat, such as salt and iron.
ijarah-main-1-3:
If he hires the animal to carry a named quantity of cotton, he may not load the equivalent weight in iron. If he hires it for riding and seats another person with him, and the animal is injured, he is liable for half its value, without regard to weight.

If he hires it to carry a specified quantity of wheat and loads more, and the animal is injured, he is liable for the excess weight.

If a person curbs an animal with its bridle or strikes it and it is injured, he is liable according to Abu Hanifa.

Hired workers are of two types:

A shared worker (ajir mushtarak) and an exclusive worker (ajir khass). The shared worker is one who does not earn the wage until he completes the work, such as the dyer and the launderer. The property in his hands is a trust: if it perishes, he is not liable for anything according to Abu Hanifa. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: he is liable for it. What is ruined by his work -- such as tearing a cloth from beating, a porter slipping, a rope breaking by which the transporter ties the load, and a ship sinking from its being pulled -- is compensable. However, he is not liable for human beings in such cases. Whoever drowns in a ship or falls from an animal, the worker is not liable. If a phlebotomist lets blood or a farrier bleeds an animal and does not exceed the customary area, there is no liability for what is damaged thereby.
ijarah-main-1-4:
The exclusive worker (ajir khass) is one who earns his wage by devoting himself for the period, even if he does not work -- such as one hired for a month for service or herding sheep. There is no liability on the exclusive worker for what perishes in his hands or what is ruined by his work.

Hiring contracts are rendered defective by conditions just as sales are. Whoever hires a slave for service may not travel with him unless he stipulates that.

Whoever hires a camel to carry a litter and two riders to Mecca, it is permissible. He is entitled to the customary litter. If the camel-driver has seen

the litter, that is better. If he hires a camel to carry a specified amount of provisions and consumes some during the journey, he may replace what he consumed.

Rent is not due by mere contract. It is earned by one of three means: either by stipulating advance payment, or by actual advance payment without stipulation, or by full utilization of the contracted benefit.

Whoever hires a house, the landlord may demand the rent for each day unless the time of accrual is specified in the contract. Whoever hires a camel to Mecca, the camel-driver may demand the rent for each stage. The launderer and tailor may not demand rent until the work is completed, unless advance payment is stipulated.
ijarah-main-1-5:
Whoever hires a baker to bake in his house one qafiz of flour for a dirham, the baker does not earn the wage until he removes the bread from the oven. Whoever hires a cook to cook food for a feast, the serving is upon him. Whoever hires a man to mold bricks for him, the man earns the wage when he stands them upright, according to Abu Hanifa. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: he does not earn it until he stacks them.

If someone says to a tailor: "If you sew this cloth in the Persian style, then one dirham; and if you sew it in the Roman style, then two dirhams," this is valid, and whichever work he performs, he earns that wage.

If he says: "If you sew it today, then one dirham; and if you sew it tomorrow, then half a dirham," and the tailor sews it today, he earns one dirham. If he sews it tomorrow, he earns a fair wage according to Abu Hanifa, not exceeding half a dirham.

If he says: "If you occupy this shop as a perfumer, then one dirham per month; and if you occupy it as a blacksmith, then two dirhams," this is valid, and whichever he does, he earns the stipulated amount, according to Abu Hanifa. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: the hire is defective.
ijarah-main-1-6:
Whoever hires a house at one dirham per month: the contract is valid for one month and defective for the remaining months, unless he specifies a total number of known months. If the tenant resides for even a moment of the second month, the contract becomes valid for that month and he is bound for that month, and the landlord may not evict him until it ends. Likewise for every month in whose beginning the tenant resides.

If someone hires a house for one year for ten

dirhams, it is valid even if he does not specify each month's share of the rent.

Taking the fee of a bathhouse attendant

and a cupper is permissible. Taking the stud fee of a male animal is not permissible. Hiring someone for the adhan, iqamah, Hajj, singing, or wailing is not permissible.

Hiring an undivided share is not permissible according to Abu Hanifa except from the co-owner.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: hiring an undivided share is permissible. Hiring a wet nurse for a known wage is permissible, and it is permissible to pay her with her food and clothing. The hirer may not prevent her husband from having intercourse with her. If she becomes pregnant, they may rescind the hire if they fear harm to the infant from her milk. She must prepare the infant's food. If she nurses the infant with sheep's milk during the period, she earns no wage.
ijarah-main-1-7:
Every craftsman whose work leaves a trace in the item -- such as the launderer and dyer -- may withhold the item after completing his work until he receives the wage.

One whose work does not leave a trace may not withhold the item for the wage, such as the porter and boatman.

If it is stipulated that the craftsman work personally, he may not employ another. If the work is left unrestricted, he may hire someone else to do it.

If the tailor and the cloth-owner disagree, and the cloth-owner says: "I ordered you to make it into a coat," and the tailor says: "a shirt" -- or the cloth-owner says to the dyer: "I ordered you to dye it red," but he dyed it yellow -- the word is the cloth-owner's with his oath. If he swears, the tailor is liable.

If the cloth-owner says: "You worked on it for me without charge," and the craftsman says: "for a fee," the word is the cloth-owner's with his oath, according to Abu Hanifa.

Abu Yusuf said: if the cloth-owner is a regular customer, then the craftsman earns a wage; if not, there is no wage. Muhammad said: if the craftsman is known to practice this craft for a fee, then his word that he did it for a fee is accepted.
ijarah-main-1-8:
In a defective hiring contract, the obligation is a fair wage (ajr al-mithl), not exceeding the stipulated amount. If the tenant takes possession of the house,

he owes the rent even if he does not reside in it. If a usurper seizes it from his possession, the rent is waived. If a defect is found that harms residence, the tenant may rescind. If

the house is ruined, the water supply to the estate is cut off, or the water to the mill is cut off, the hiring contract is automatically rescinded.

If one of the contracting parties dies and he contracted the hire for himself, the hire is rescinded. If he contracted it for another, it is not rescinded. The option of stipulation is valid in hiring contracts. Hiring contracts are rescinded for valid excuses, such as when someone hires a shop in the market to trade in and then loses his capital; or when someone rents out a house or shop and then becomes insolvent with debts he cannot pay except from the price of what he rented out, the judge rescinds the contract and sells the property for the debt.

Likewise, when someone hires an animal to travel on and then decides not to travel, that is a valid excuse. But if the animal-owner decides not to travel, that is not a valid excuse.
shufah-main-1-1:
Pre-emption (shuf'ah) is established for the co-owner (khalit) in the sold property itself, then for the co-owner in the rights appurtenant to the sold property -- such as water rights and the right of way -- then for the neighbor. The partner in the right of way, water rights, and the neighbor have no pre-emption alongside the co-owner in the property itself. If the co-owner waives, pre-emption passes to the partner in the right of way. If he waives, the neighbor takes it.

Pre-emption is established by the contract of sale, confirmed by calling witnesses, and ownership is acquired by taking possession -- when the buyer surrenders it or a judge rules in the pre-emptor's favor. When the pre-emptor learns of the sale, he must call witnesses in that very session to assert his claim. Then he proceeds to call witnesses before the seller -- if the sold property is in his possession -- or before the buyer, or at the property itself.

If he does this, his pre-emption is confirmed and is not lost by delay according to Abu Hanifa. Muhammad said: if he neglects it for a month after calling witnesses, his pre-emption is forfeited.

Pre-emption is established in real property even if it is not divisible. There is no pre-emption in movable goods or ships. A Muslim and a dhimmi are equal in pre-emption.
shufah-main-1-2:
When real property is acquired for a consideration that is property, pre-emption is established in it. There is no pre-emption in a house that a man takes as a dower, or in exchange for a khul' divorce, or as rent for a house, or as settlement for intentional homicide, or for manumitting a slave, or as settlement with denial or silence. If the settlement is with acknowledgment, pre-emption is established.

When the pre-emptor comes before the judge, claims the purchase and demands pre-emption, the judge asks the defendant. If the defendant acknowledges the pre-emptor's ownership that entitles him to pre-emption, fine; otherwise the pre-emptor is required to produce evidence. If he cannot produce evidence, the buyer is made to swear by Allah that he does not know the pre-emptor to be the owner of what he claims to use for pre-emption. If the buyer refuses the oath or evidence is established, the judge asks the buyer whether he purchased or not. If the buyer denies the purchase, the pre-emptor is told to produce evidence. If he cannot,

the buyer is made to swear by Allah that he did not purchase, or by Allah that the pre-emptor has no right of pre-emption against him in this property through the claim he described. Litigation over pre-emption is permissible even if the pre-emptor does not bring the price to the judge's session.

When the judge rules in the pre-emptor's favor, the pre-emptor must produce the price. The pre-emptor may return the property on account of a defect or the option of inspection.
shufah-main-1-3:
If the pre-emptor brings the seller and the sold property is in his hands, the pre-emptor may contest him regarding pre-emption. The judge does not hear evidence until the buyer is present, so that the sale is rescinded in his presence and pre-emption is decreed against the seller, with the guarantee of title (uhda) placed upon him.

If the pre-emptor abandons calling witnesses when he learns of the sale and he is able to do so,

his pre-emption is forfeited. Likewise if he calls witnesses in the session but does not then call witnesses before one of the two parties or at the property. If he settles his pre-emption for a consideration he takes, his pre-emption is forfeited and he must return the consideration.

If the pre-emptor dies, his pre-emption is forfeited.

If the buyer dies, pre-emption is not lost. If the pre-emptor sells the property by which he claims pre-emption before judgment is rendered in his favor, his pre-emption is forfeited. The seller's agent, if he sells and is himself the pre-emptor, has no pre-emption. Likewise if the pre-emptor guarantees the title warranty (darak) for the seller. The buyer's agent, if he purchases, has pre-emption. If someone sells with the option of stipulation, the pre-emptor has no pre-emption. If the option is waived, pre-emption is established. If someone buys with the option of stipulation, pre-emption is established.

If someone buys a house in a defective sale, the pre-emptor has no pre-emption. But each

of the contracting parties may rescind. If the right of rescission lapses, pre-emption is established.
shufah-main-1-4:
If a dhimmi buys a house for wine or pork, and the pre-emptor is a dhimmi, he takes it for the like of the wine and the value of the pork. If the pre-emptor is a Muslim, he takes it for the value of the wine and the pork.

There is no pre-emption in a gift unless it is given with a stipulated consideration. If the pre-emptor and the buyer disagree about the price, the word is the buyer's. If both produce evidence, the evidence of the pre-emptor prevails according to Abu Hanifa and Muhammad. If the buyer claims a higher price and the seller claims a lower one, and the price has not been received, the pre-emptor takes it at what the seller says, and that is treated as a reduction for the buyer.

If the price has been received, the pre-emptor takes it at what the buyer says, and

the seller's statement is disregarded. If the seller reduces part of the price for the buyer, that reduction also applies to the pre-emptor. If the seller reduces the entire price, the reduction does not apply to the pre-emptor. If the buyer increases the price for the seller, the increase is not binding on the pre-emptor.

When pre-emptors are of equal rank, the pre-emption is divided among them by the number of persons, not by the size of their holdings.

If someone buys a house with a commodity, the pre-emptor takes it for its value. If someone buys it with a measured or weighed commodity, the pre-emptor takes it for its like.
shufah-main-1-5:
If someone sells real property for other real property, the pre-emptor takes each of them at the value of the other. If the pre-emptor learns that the property was sold for one thousand and waives his pre-emption, then discovers it was actually sold for less, or for wheat or barley worth one thousand or more, his waiver is void and he retains his pre-emption. If it turns out it was sold for dinars worth one thousand, he has no pre-emption.

If he is told that the buyer is so-and-so and he waives his pre-emption, then discovers the buyer is someone else, he retains his pre-emption.

If someone buys a house for another person, the actual buyer is the opponent in the pre-emption claim, unless he has delivered it to the principal.

If someone sells a house except for a cubit-wide strip along the boundary adjoining the pre-emptor, the pre-emptor has no pre-emption. If a share is first purchased at a price, then the remainder is purchased, the neighbor has pre-emption in the first share only, not the second.

If someone buys the house at a price and then gives a cloth in its place, the pre-emption is based on the price, not the cloth.

Using stratagems to circumvent pre-emption is not disliked according to Abu Yusuf. Muhammad said (may Allah have mercy on him):
shufah-main-1-6:
it is disliked. If the buyer builds or plants and then pre-emption is decreed for the pre-emptor, the pre-emptor has the option: if he wishes, he may take the property at the price plus the value of the building and planting as uprooted; and if he wishes, he may require the buyer to remove them. If the pre-emptor takes the property and then builds or plants, and the property is subsequently reclaimed by a rightful claimant (istihqaq), he recovers the price but does not recover the value of the building and planting.

If the house collapses, or its structure burns, or the trees of a garden dry up without anyone's doing, the pre-emptor has the option: if he wishes, he may take it for the full price, and if he wishes, he may leave it. If the buyer demolishes the structure, the pre-emptor is told: if you wish, take the plot at its proportionate share, and if you wish, leave it. He is not entitled to take the demolished materials.

If someone buys land with fruit on its palm trees, the pre-emptor takes it with its fruit. If the buyer has already harvested the fruit, its share is deducted from the pre-emptor's obligation. When pre-emption is decreed for the pre-emptor regarding a house he has not seen, he has the option of inspection. If he finds a defect, he may return it on that account, even if the buyer had stipulated exemption from it.
shufah-main-1-7: If the purchase is made at a deferred price, the pre-emptor has the option: if he wishes, he may take it at an immediate price, or if he wishes, he may wait until the deferral period expires and then take it. If the co-owners divide the real property, the neighbor has no pre-emption by virtue of the division. If someone buys a house and the pre-emptor waives his pre-emption, and then the buyer returns it by the option of inspection, stipulation, or defect by judicial decree, the pre-emptor has no pre-emption. If the buyer returns it without judicial decree, or they mutually rescind, the pre-emptor has pre-emption.
shirkah-main-1-1:
Partnership is of two types: partnership of co-ownership (shirkat al-amlak) and partnership by contract (shirkat al-uqud).

Partnership of co-ownership is a thing inherited by two persons or purchased by them. Neither may dispose of the other's share except with his permission. Each of them with respect to the other's share is like a stranger.

The second type is partnership by contract.

It is of four kinds: mufawadah (comprehensive partnership), 'inan (limited partnership), partnership of crafts, and partnership of credit.

As for the mufawadah partnership: it requires that two persons enter into partnership as equals in their capital, their authority to transact, and their religious status. It is permissible between two free adult sane Muslims. It is not permissible between a free person and a slave, a minor and an adult, or a Muslim and a non-Muslim. It is founded upon mutual agency and mutual suretyship.

What each of them purchases is upon the partnership, except food and clothing for his family. Whatever debts each of them incurs as a substitute for what is validly subject to partnership, the other is a surety for. If one of them inherits property that is validly subject to partnership, or is given a gift and receives it, the mufawadah becomes invalid and the partnership becomes an 'inan.

Partnership is not validly concluded except with dirhams, dinars, and copper coins.
shirkah-main-1-2:
that are in circulation. It is not valid with anything else unless people customarily transact with it, such as raw gold and silver ingots, in which case partnership with them is valid. If they wish to form a partnership with commodities, each of them sells half of his property for half of the other's property, and then they conclude the partnership.

As for the 'inan partnership: it is founded upon mutual agency without mutual suretyship. Disparity in capital is permissible, and they may be equal in capital but unequal in profit.

Each of them may enter into the partnership with part of his property and not all of it. It is only valid with what we have explained that the mufawadah is valid with.

It is permissible for them to form a partnership with dirhams from one party and dinars from the other. Whatever each of them purchases for the partnership, he alone is demanded for its price, not the other. He then has recourse against his partner for his share of it.

If the partnership capital or one of the two capitals is destroyed before they purchase anything, the partnership is voided. If one of them purchases with his capital and the other's capital is destroyed before purchasing, what was purchased belongs to both of them as they stipulated.

The one whose capital was destroyed has recourse against his partner for his share of the price.

Partnership is valid even if they do not mix the two capitals. Partnership is not valid if they stipulate a specified amount of dirhams from the profit for one of them.
shirkah-main-1-3:
Each of the mufawadah and 'inan partners may invest the capital as bida'ah (a trade venture without profit-sharing), give it as mudarabah, or appoint an agent to manage it. His hand over the capital is a hand of trust.

As for the partnership of crafts: two tailors or two dyers form a partnership to accept work commissions with the earnings divided between them. This is permissible. Whatever work each of them accepts is binding upon him and upon his partner.

If one of them works and the other does not, the earnings are divided equally between them.

As for the partnership of credit (shirkat al-wujuh): two men form a partnership having no capital, on the basis that they buy on their credit and sell. This partnership is valid, and each of them is the agent of the other in what he buys. If they stipulate that what is purchased is shared equally, the profit
