If the bleeding of the third menstruation stops at ten days, the right of taking back is terminated even if she has not bathed. If it stops at less than ten days, the right of taking back is not terminated until she bathes, or until a prayer time passes over her, or until she performs dry ablution (tayammum) and prays, according to Abu Hanifah and Abu Yusuf.

Muhammad said: If she performs dry ablution, the right of taking back is terminated even if she has not prayed.
talaq-bab-1-1-2:
If she bathes and neglects to wash a part of her body: if it is an entire limb or more, the right of taking back is not terminated. If it is less than an entire limb, it is terminated.

The revocably divorced woman adorns herself and beautifies herself. It is recommended for her husband not to enter upon her until he seeks her permission or she hears the sound of his sandals.

A revocable divorce does not prohibit intercourse. If the divorce is irrevocable but less than three, he may marry her during her waiting period or after it has concluded.

If the divorce is three for a free woman, or two for a slave-woman, she is not lawful to him until she marries another husband in a valid marriage, and he consummates the marriage with her, and then he divorces her or dies.

A pubescent boy suffices for making her lawful again (tahlil), just as an adult does.

The intercourse of a master with his slave-woman does not make her lawful. If a man marries her on the condition of making her lawful, the marriage is disliked (makruh), but if he divorces her after consummating the marriage, she becomes lawful to the first husband.

If a man divorces a free woman once or twice, her waiting period expires, and she marries another husband and then returns to the first, she returns with three pronouncements of divorce. The second husband cancels what is less than three divorces just as he cancels three.

Muhammad said: He does not cancel what is less than three.
talaq-bab-1-1-3: If he divorces her three times and she says: "My waiting period has expired, I married, he consummated the marriage, he divorced me, and my waiting period has expired," and the time frame could accommodate all of that, it is permissible for the husband to believe her if he predominantly thinks she is truthful.
ila-intro-1:
If a man says to his wife: "By Allah, I will not approach you" or "I will not approach you for four months," he is a person who has taken an oath of abstinence (muli). If he has intercourse with her during the four months, he has broken his oath, the expiation becomes obligatory upon him, and the oath of abstinence lapses.

If he does not approach her until four months have passed, she becomes irrevocably separated from him by one divorce.

If he swore for four months, the oath has lapsed. If he swore for an indefinite period, the oath remains. If he returns and marries her again, the oath of abstinence resumes. If he has intercourse with her, the expiation becomes obligatory. Otherwise, with the passage of four months, another divorce takes effect. If he marries her again, the oath of abstinence resumes, and with the passage of four months, another divorce takes effect. If he marries her after another husband, the oath of abstinence no longer causes divorce, but the oath remains. If he has intercourse with her, he must make expiation for his oath.

If he swears for less than four months, he is not considered one who has taken the oath of abstinence. If he swears by Hajj, fasting, charity, or manumission, or by divorce, he is considered one who has taken the oath of abstinence.

If he takes the oath of abstinence from a revocably divorced woman, he is considered one who has taken the oath. If he takes it from an irrevocably divorced woman, he is not. The period for the oath of abstinence of a slave-woman is two months.

If the one who took the oath is ill and unable to have intercourse, or the woman is ill, or there is a distance between them that prevents him from reaching her during the period of the oath, his return (fi'ah) is to say verbally: "I have returned to her." If he says that, the oath of abstinence lapses. If he recovers during the period, that verbal return is nullified, and his return must be by intercourse.

If a man says to his wife: "You are forbidden to me" -- he is asked about his intention. If he says: "I intended a lie," it is as he says.

If he says: "I intended divorce," it is one irrevocable divorce, unless he intended three. If he says: "I intended dhihar," it is dhihar. If he says: "I intended the prohibition" or "I did not intend anything by it," it is an oath by which he becomes one who has taken the oath of abstinence.
ila-bab-1-1:
If the spouses are in discord and they fear they will not uphold the limits of Allah, there is no harm in the wife ransoming herself from him with wealth by which he grants her khul'. If he does so, one irrevocable divorce takes effect by the khul' and the wealth becomes binding upon her.

If the discord is from his side, it is disliked for him to take compensation from her. If the discord is from her side, it is disliked for him to take more than what he gave her. If he does so, it is valid judicially.

If he divorces her for a sum of wealth and she accepts, the divorce takes effect, the wealth becomes binding upon her, and the divorce is irrevocable.

If the consideration in the khul' is void -- for example, if a Muslim woman takes khul' for wine or swine -- the husband receives nothing, and the separation is irrevocable. If the consideration in a divorce is void, it is revocable.

Whatever is valid as a dower is valid as consideration in khul'.

If she says to him: "Grant me khul' for what is in my hand" and he grants her khul' and there is nothing in her hand, he is owed nothing. If she says: "Grant me khul' for whatever wealth is in my hand" and there is nothing in her hand, she must return her dower to him. If she says: "Grant me khul' for whatever dirhams are in my hand" and he grants her khul' and there is nothing in her hand, she owes him three dirhams. If she says: "Divorce me three times for one thousand" and he divorces her once, she owes one-third of the thousand. If she says: "Divorce me three times on condition of one thousand" and he divorces her once, she owes nothing according to Abu Hanifah.

If the husband says: "Divorce yourself three times for one thousand" or "on condition of one thousand" and she divorces herself once, nothing takes effect upon her.

The mubara'ah (mutual release) is like khul' and khul' is like mubara'ah: both extinguish every right of each spouse against the other that is connected to the marriage, according to Abu Hanifah -- may Allah have mercy on him -- except maintenance during the waiting period. Abu Yusuf -- may Allah have mercy on him -- said: Mubara'ah extinguishes rights but khul' does not. Muhammad -- may Allah have mercy on him -- said: Neither extinguishes except what they specifically mentioned.
dhihar-intro-1-1:
If the husband says to his wife: "You are to me like the back of my mother," she has become forbidden to him. It is not lawful for him to have intercourse with her, to touch her, or to kiss her until he makes expiation for his dhihar. If he has intercourse with her before making expiation, he seeks forgiveness from Allah Most High, and he owes nothing other than the original expiation, and he must not return to her until he makes expiation.

The resumption (al-'awd) that obligates the expiation is that he resolves to have intercourse with her.

If he says: "You are to me like the belly of my mother" or "like her thigh" or "like her private part," he has committed dhihar. Likewise if he compares her to any permanently forbidden female relative whom he is forbidden to look at, such as his sister, paternal aunt, or his mother through breastfeeding. Likewise if he says: "Your head is to me like the back of my mother" or "your private part" or "your face" or "your neck" or "half of you" or "a third of you." If he says: "You are to me like my mother," he is asked about his intention.

If he says: "I intended honor," it is as he says. If he says: "I intended dhihar," it is dhihar. If he says: "I intended divorce," it is an irrevocable divorce. If he has no intention, it is nothing.

Dhihar can only be made regarding one's wife. If he makes dhihar regarding his slave-woman, it is not dhihar. If a man says to his wives: "You are to me like the back of my mother," he has committed dhihar against all of them, and he owes an expiation for each one of them.
dhihar-intro-1-2:
The expiation for dhihar is the freeing of a slave. If he cannot find one, then fasting two consecutive months. If he is unable to do that, then feeding sixty poor persons -- all of this before resuming intimate relations. It suffices to free a slave who is a disbeliever or Muslim, male or female, young or old. A blind person does not suffice, nor one whose both hands or both feet are amputated.

A deaf person suffices, and one whose one hand and one foot are amputated from opposite sides suffices. One whose both thumbs are amputated does not suffice, nor an insane person who has no understanding. Freeing a mukatab (contractually emancipated slave), an umm walad (mother of the master's child), or a mukatab who has paid part of his price does not suffice.

If he frees a mukatab who has not paid anything, it suffices. If he purchases his father or son intending the purchase as expiation, it suffices. If he frees half of a jointly-owned slave for the expiation and becomes liable for the value of the remainder and thus frees the whole, it does not suffice according to Abu Hanifah. If he frees half of his own slave for his expiation and then frees the remainder for it, it suffices.
dhihar-intro-1-3:
If he frees half of his slave for his expiation and then has intercourse with the woman against whom he made dhihar, and then frees the remainder, it does not suffice according to Abu Hanifah. If the person who made dhihar cannot find a slave to free, his expiation is fasting two consecutive months during which there is no month of Ramadan, nor the day of Eid al-Fitr, nor the day of Eid al-Adha, nor the days of Tashriq.

If he has intercourse with the woman against whom he made dhihar during the two months -- at night deliberately, or during the day forgetfully -- he must start the fast over according to Abu Hanifah and Muhammad. If he breaks the fast for one day with or without excuse, he must start over.

If a slave makes dhihar, nothing suffices for his expiation except fasting.

If the master frees a slave on his behalf or feeds poor persons on his behalf, it does not suffice.

If the person who made dhihar is unable to fast, he feeds sixty poor persons, each person receiving half a sa' of wheat, or one sa' of dates or barley, or the monetary value thereof. If he gives them lunch and dinner, it suffices regardless of how much or how little they eat. If he gives the entire amount to one poor person over sixty days, it suffices. If he gives it to one poor person in a single day, it only counts for that day. If he has intercourse with the woman against whom he made dhihar during the course of feeding, he does not need to start over.
dhihar-intro-1-4: If a person owes two expiations for dhihar and frees two slaves without designating either one for a specific expiation, it suffices for both. Likewise if he fasts four months. If he feeds one hundred and twenty poor persons, it suffices. If he frees one slave or fasts two months, he may designate it for whichever of the two he wishes.
dhihar-bab-1-1:
If a man accuses his wife of fornication and both are qualified to testify, and the wife is such that her accuser would be subjected to the prescribed punishment, or he denies the lineage of her child, and she demands the consequence of the accusation, then li'an (mutual imprecation) is incumbent upon him. If he refuses to perform it, the judge imprisons him until he performs the li'an or retracts and accepts the prescribed punishment. If he performs the li'an, it becomes incumbent upon her.

If she refuses, the judge imprisons her until she performs the li'an or affirms his accusation.

If he is qualified to testify but she is a slave-woman, a disbeliever, or has been punished for false accusation, or she is someone whose accuser would not be punished, then there is no prescribed punishment upon him for accusing her, and no li'an.

The procedure of li'an is as follows: the judge begins with the husband, who testifies four times, saying each time: "I testify by Allah that I am truthful in what I have accused her of regarding fornication."

Then he says the fifth time: "May the curse of Allah be upon him if he is a liar in what he accused her of regarding fornication." He points to her throughout. Then the woman testifies four times, saying each time: "I testify by Allah that he is a liar in what he accused me of regarding fornication." She says the fifth time: "May the wrath of Allah be upon her if he is truthful in what he accused me of regarding fornication." Once both have performed the li'an, the judge separates them, and the separation is one irrevocable divorce according to Abu Hanifah and Muhammad. Abu Yusuf said: It is a permanent prohibition.

If the accusation concerns a child, the judge negates its lineage and attributes it to its mother.

If the husband subsequently retracts and admits he lied, the judge subjects him to the prescribed punishment, and it becomes lawful for him to marry her. Likewise if he accuses another woman of fornication and is punished, or if she commits fornication and is punished.

If he accuses his wife and she is a minor or insane, there is no li'an between them.

The accusation by a mute person does not give rise to li'an.

If the husband says: "Your pregnancy is not from me," there is no li'an between them.

If he says: "You have committed fornication and this pregnancy is from fornication," they perform li'an, but the judge does not negate the pregnancy's lineage.

If a man denies his wife's child immediately after birth, or during the period when congratulations are being received, or when supplies for childbirth are being purchased, his denial is valid and li'an is performed. If he denies it after that period, li'an is performed but the lineage is established.

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: His denial is valid during the entire postnatal period (nifas). If she gives birth to twins and he denies the first and acknowledges the second, both their lineages are established and the husband is subjected to the prescribed punishment. If he acknowledges the first and denies the second, both their lineages are established and li'an is performed.
dhihar-bab-2-1-1:
If a man divorces his wife with an irrevocable or revocable divorce, or a separation occurs between them without divorce, and she is a free woman who menstruates, her waiting period is three menstrual cycles (quru') -- and the quru' means menstruation. If she does not menstruate due to youth or old age, her waiting period is three months. If she is pregnant, her waiting period lasts until she delivers.

If she is a slave-woman, her waiting period is two menstrual periods. If she does not menstruate, her waiting period is a month and a half.

If a man dies and his wife is a free woman, her waiting period is four months and ten days. If she is a slave-woman, her waiting period is two months and five days. If she is pregnant, her waiting period lasts until she delivers. If the woman divorced during terminal illness inherits, her waiting period is the longer of the two periods.

If a slave-woman is manumitted during her waiting period from a revocable divorce, her waiting period is converted to that of free women. If she is manumitted while she is irrevocably divorced or her husband has died, her waiting period is not converted. If she had ceased menstruating and observed the waiting period by months, then sees blood, what has passed of her waiting period is nullified and she must begin a new waiting period by menstruation.

The woman married in an invalid marriage and the woman with whom intercourse occurred by mistake observe their waiting period by menstruation in both separation and death.

If the master of an umm walad dies or frees her, her waiting period is three menstrual cycles.
dhihar-bab-2-1-2:
If a minor dies and his wife is pregnant, her waiting period lasts until she delivers. If the pregnancy occurred after his death, her waiting period is four months and ten days.

If a man divorces his wife during menstruation, she does not count the menstrual period in which the divorce occurred.

If a woman observing the waiting period has intercourse by mistake, she must observe another waiting period, and the two waiting periods overlap: whatever menstruation she sees counts toward both. When the first waiting period concludes but the second has not been completed, she must complete the remainder of the second waiting period.

The waiting period in divorce begins immediately after the divorce, and in death immediately after the death. If she does not learn of the divorce or death until the waiting period has passed, her waiting period has concluded.

The waiting period in an invalid marriage begins after the separation between them or after the man resolves to cease having intercourse with her.

The irrevocably divorced woman and the woman whose husband has died -- if she is an adult Muslim -- must observe mourning (ihdad), which consists of: abandoning perfume, adornment, oil, and kohl except for medical necessity. She must not apply henna, nor wear garments dyed with safflower or saffron.
dhihar-bab-2-1-3:
There is no mourning required of a non-Muslim woman or a minor girl. A slave-woman must observe mourning. There is no mourning during the waiting period of an invalid marriage, nor during the waiting period of an umm walad.

A woman observing the waiting period should not be proposed to directly, but there is no harm in making an indirect allusion to the proposal.

The revocably divorced woman and the irrevocably divorced woman may not leave their homes by night or day.

The woman whose husband has died may go out during the day and part of the night, but she may not spend the night outside her home.

The woman observing the waiting period must do so in the dwelling that is attributed to her residence at the time the separation occurred. If her share of the deceased's house does not suffice her and the heirs remove her from their portion, she relocates.

The husband may not travel with a revocably divorced woman.

If a man divorces his wife with an irrevocable divorce and then marries her during her waiting period and divorces her before consummating the new marriage, he owes a full dower and she must observe a new waiting period. Muhammad said: She receives half the dower and must complete the remainder of the first waiting period.

The lineage of a child born to a revocably divorced woman is established if she gives birth within two years or more, as long as she has not acknowledged the expiry of her waiting period. If she gives birth
dhihar-bab-2-1-4:
in less than two years, she is irrevocably separated from her husband. If she gives birth after more than two years, the lineage is established and it constitutes a taking back (raj'ah).

The lineage of a child of an irrevocably divorced woman is established if she gives birth within less than two years. If she gives birth at the completion of two years from the day of separation, the lineage is not established unless he claims the child.

The lineage of a child of a woman whose husband has died is established between the death and two years.

If a woman observing the waiting period acknowledges its expiry and then gives birth in less than six months, the lineage is established. If she gives birth at six months or more, the lineage is not established.

If a woman observing the waiting period gives birth to a child whose lineage is not established, according to Abu Hanifah, the lineage is not established unless two men testify to the birth, or one man and two women -- unless there is an apparent pregnancy or an acknowledgment from the husband, in which case the lineage is established without testimony. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: In all cases it is established by the testimony of one woman.

If a man marries a woman and she gives birth in less than six months from the day of marriage, the lineage is not established. If she gives birth at six months or more, the lineage is established if the husband acknowledges it or remains silent. If he denies the birth itself, it is established by the testimony of one woman who testifies to the birth.
dhihar-bab-2-1-5:
The maximum duration of pregnancy is two years, and the minimum is six months.

If a non-Muslim subject (dhimmi) divorces his non-Muslim wife, she has no waiting period.

If a woman pregnant from fornication marries, the marriage is valid, but the husband may not have intercourse with her until she delivers. And Allah knows best.
nafaqat-main-1:
Maintenance is obligatory upon the husband, whether his wife is Muslim or non-Muslim. If she surrenders herself in his home, he is responsible for her food, clothing, and housing.

This is determined by the condition of both of them together, whether the husband is well-off or in hardship.

If she withholds herself until he gives her her dower, she is still entitled to maintenance.

If she is disobedient (nashizah), she has no maintenance until she returns to his home.

If she is a minor from whom no benefit can be derived, she has no maintenance even if she surrenders herself to him.

If the husband is a minor who is not capable of intercourse and the wife is an adult, she is entitled to maintenance from his wealth.

If a man divorces his wife, she is entitled to maintenance and housing during her waiting period, whether the divorce is revocable or irrevocable.

The woman whose husband has died has no maintenance. Every separation that comes from the woman's side through disobedience carries no maintenance. If he divorces her and then she apostasizes, her maintenance lapses.

If she allows the husband's son to have relations with her: if it was after the divorce, she is entitled to maintenance; if it was before the divorce, she has no maintenance.

If the wife is imprisoned for a debt, or a man forcibly takes her away, or she goes on Hajj with a male relative, she has no maintenance.

If she falls ill in the husband's home, she is entitled to maintenance.

The husband is required to pay the maintenance of her servant if he is well-off, but no maintenance is assessed for more than one servant.

He must provide her with a separate dwelling in which there is none of his family, unless she chooses otherwise. If he has children from another wife, he may not have them live with her.

The husband may prevent her parents, her children from another marriage, and her family from entering upon her.
hadanah-main-1-1:
But he may not prevent them from seeing her and speaking with her at any time they choose.

If the husband is unable to provide his wife's maintenance, the spouses are not separated. She is told: "Borrow against him."

If a man is absent and has wealth in the possession of another man who knows of the marriage, the judge may allocate from that wealth the maintenance of the absent man's wife, his minor children, and his parents, and he takes a guarantor from her. The judge does not order maintenance from the absent person's wealth except for these individuals.

If the judge ruled maintenance at the rate of hardship and the husband later becomes well-off, and she disputes, the judge supplements her maintenance to the rate of the well-off.

If a period passes during which the husband did not provide maintenance and she demands it, she receives nothing unless the judge had assessed maintenance for her, or she had settled with the husband on a specific amount, in which case she is awarded the maintenance for the past period. If the husband dies after maintenance was ordered against him and months have passed, the maintenance lapses.

If he advanced her maintenance for a year and then dies, nothing is reclaimed from her. Muhammad said: The past maintenance is counted for her, and the remainder belongs to the husband.

If a slave marries a free woman, her maintenance is a debt upon him, and he may be sold to satisfy it.

If a man marries a slave-woman and her master settles her with him in a dwelling, maintenance is due. If the master does not settle her with him, there is no maintenance.
hadanah-main-1-2:
The maintenance of minor children is upon the father alone; no one shares it with him, just as no one shares with him the maintenance of the wife.

If the child is of humble status, the mother is not required to breastfeed him. The father hires a wet-nurse for him in her presence. If he hires the mother while she is still his wife or in her waiting period to breastfeed their child, it is not valid. If her waiting period has expired and he hires her to breastfeed, it is valid.

If the father says: "I will not hire her" and brings another woman, and the mother accepts the same fee as the other woman, the mother has more right to the child. If she demands more, the husband is not compelled to pay it.

The maintenance of the minor is obligatory upon the father even if they differ in religion, just as the wife's maintenance is obligatory upon the husband even if they differ in religion.

When separation occurs between the spouses, the mother has the most right to the child. If the mother is not available, the maternal grandmother has priority over the paternal grandmother. If there is no grandmother, then the paternal grandmother has priority over sisters. If there is no grandmother, then sisters have priority over paternal and maternal aunts. The full sister (through both parents) takes precedence, then the half-sister through the mother, then the half-sister through the father. Then the maternal aunts have priority over the paternal aunts, and they are ranked in the same order. Then the paternal aunts, ranked in the same order.
hadanah-main-1-3:
Any of these women who remarries loses her right, except the grandmother if her husband is the grandfather. If there is no female relative for the child and the men dispute over him, the one with the closest agnate relationship has the most right.

The mother and grandmother have more right to the boy until he eats by himself, dresses by himself, and cleans himself after using the toilet. They have more right to the girl until she begins menstruation. Women other than the mother and grandmother have more right to the girl until she reaches an age at which she is desired.

A slave-woman who is manumitted by her master, and an umm walad who is freed, have the same rights regarding the child as a free woman. A slave-woman and an umm walad before manumission have no right to the child. A non-Muslim woman under covenant has more right to her Muslim child as long as the child does not understand religions and there is no fear of the child becoming accustomed to disbelief.

If a divorced woman wishes to take her child out of the city, she may not do so unless she takes the child to her homeland where the husband had married her.

A man is required to provide maintenance for his parents and grandparents if they are poor, even if they differ with him in religion. Maintenance is not obligatory across different religions except for the wife, parents, grandparents, children, and grandchildren. No one shares with the child in providing maintenance for the parents.
hadanah-main-1-4:
Maintenance is due to every person of unmarriageable kinship if they are a poor minor, or a poor adult woman, or a poor disabled or blind male.

This is apportioned according to the shares of inheritance.

The maintenance of an adult daughter and a disabled son is obligatory upon their parents in the ratio of thirds: two-thirds upon the father and one-third upon the mother.

Their maintenance is not obligatory when there is a difference in religion, nor upon a poor person.

If the absent son has wealth, the judge orders the maintenance of his parents from it. If the father sells the son's goods for his maintenance, it is valid according to Abu Hanifah. If the father sells real property, it is not valid.

If the absent son has wealth in the hands of his parents and they spend from it, they are not liable.

If he has wealth in the hands of a stranger who spends it on them without the judge's permission, the stranger is liable.

If the judge awards maintenance to the child, parents, or relatives and a period passes, it lapses unless the judge gave permission to borrow against the person.

The master must provide maintenance for his slave and slave-woman. If he refuses and they are capable of earning, they earn and spend on themselves.

If they are not capable of earning, the master is compelled to sell them.
itq-intro-1-1: Manumission is effected by a free, adult person in his property. If he says to his slave or slave-woman: "You are free" (anta hurr), or "freed" (muharrar), or "I have freed you" (harartuka), or "I have manumitted you" (a'taqtuka), the slave is manumitted -- whether the master intended manumission or not. Likewise if he says: "Your head is free," or "your face," or "your neck," or "your body." Or if he says to his slave-woman: "Your private part is free." If he says: "I have no ownership over you" and intends freedom by it, the slave is manumitted. If he does not intend it, the slave is not manumitted. Likewise with the metaphorical expressions of manumission. If he says: "I have no authority over you" and intends manumission, the slave is not manumitted. If he says: "This is my son" and persists in that statement, or says: "This is my patron" (mawlay) or "O my patron" (ya mawlay), the slave is manumitted. If he says: "O my son" (ya ibni) or "O my brother" (ya akhi), the slave is not manumitted. If he says to a boy who could not possibly be his biological son: "This is my son," the slave is manumitted according to Abu Hanifah -- may Allah have mercy on him. According to the other two, he is not manumitted. If he says to his slave-woman: "You are divorced" and intends freedom by it, she is manumitted. If he says to his slave: "You are like a free person," the slave is not manumitted. If he says: "You are nothing but free," the slave is manumitted. If a man acquires ownership of a relative of unmarriageable kinship, the relative is manumitted automatically. If the master manumits part of his slave, that part is manumitted, and the slave must work to earn the value of the remainder for his master, according to Abu Hanifah -- may Allah have mercy on him. The other two said: The whole slave is manumitted. If a slave is jointly owned by two partners and one of them manumits his share,
itq-intro-1-2: that share is manumitted. If the manumitting partner is well-off, the other partner has the choice: he may manumit his share, or he may make the manumitting partner liable for the value of his share, or he may require the slave to work off the value. If the manumitting partner is in hardship, the other partner has the choice: he may manumit his share or require the slave to work off the value. This is according to Abu Hanifah -- may Allah have mercy on him. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: The partner has no option except to demand compensation from the manumitting partner if he is well-off, or to require the slave to work off the value if the partner is in hardship. If two men purchase the son of one of them, the father's share is manumitted and no compensation is owed by him. Likewise if they both inherit the slave. The other partner has the choice: he may manumit his share or require the slave to work off the value. If each of two partners testifies that the other manumitted the slave, the entire slave is manumitted and the slave must work to pay each partner the value of his share, whether both partners are well-off or in hardship, according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: If both are well-off, the slave owes no labor. If both are in hardship, he must work for both. If one is well-off and the other in hardship, he works for the well-off partner and not for the one in hardship. Whoever manumits his slave for the sake of Allah Most High, or for Satan, or for an idol, the slave is manumitted.
itq-intro-1-3:
The manumission of a coerced person and a drunk person takes effect. If manumission is attached to ownership or a condition, it is valid, just as it is valid with divorce. If a slave comes out of the territory of war to us as a Muslim, he is manumitted. If a man manumits a pregnant slave-woman, her unborn child is manumitted. If he manumits only the unborn child, it is manumitted but the mother is not.

If a man manumits his slave for a sum of money and the slave accepts, the slave is manumitted and the money becomes binding upon him. If he says: "If you pay me one thousand, you are free," it is valid and the slave becomes authorized to conduct business. If the slave brings the money, the judge compels the master to accept it and the slave is manumitted.

The child of a slave-woman from her master is free. Her child from her husband is a slave belonging to her master. The child of a free woman from a slave is free.
itq-bab-1-1: If the master says to his slave: "When I die, you are free," or "you are free to take effect after my death," or "you are a mudabbar," or "I have granted you tadbir," the slave has become a mudabbar. It is not permissible to sell him or give him away. The master may employ him and hire him out. If the slave is female, the master may have intercourse with her. He may also arrange the slave's marriage. When the master dies, the mudabbar is manumitted from one-third of his estate if the third suffices. If the master has no other property, the mudabbar must work to earn two-thirds of his value. If the master has debts, the mudabbar must work to earn his entire value for the creditors. The child of a female mudabbar is a mudabbar. If the manumission is made conditional on a specific manner of death -- such as his saying: "If I die from this illness" or "this journey" or "such-and-such disease" -- the slave is not a mudabbar and may be sold. If the master dies in the manner he described, the slave is manumitted just as a mudabbar is manumitted.
itq-bab-2-1:
If a slave-woman gives birth from her master, she has become his umm walad (mother of his child). It is not permissible to sell her or transfer ownership of her. The master may have intercourse with her, employ her, hire her out, and arrange her marriage. The lineage of her child is not established unless the master acknowledges it. If she subsequently gives birth to another child, its lineage is established without acknowledgment. If he denies it, it is negated by his word. If he arranges her marriage and she gives birth, the child follows her status. When the master dies, she is manumitted from the entirety of his estate, and she is not required to work off any amount for creditors even if the master has debts.

If he had intercourse with another's slave-woman through marriage, and she gives birth from him, then he acquires ownership of her, she becomes his umm walad. If the father has intercourse with his son's slave-woman and she gives birth and he claims the child, the lineage is established, she becomes his umm walad, and he owes her value. He does not owe the bridal payment (uqr) nor the value of her child. If a grandfather has intercourse while the father is still alive, the lineage is not established. If the father is deceased, the lineage is established from the grandfather just as it is from the father. If a slave-woman is jointly owned by two partners and she gives birth and one claims the child, the lineage is established from him and she becomes his umm walad.

He owes half the bridal payment and half her value, and he owes nothing of the child's value. If both claim the child, the lineage is established from both and she is umm walad for both of them. Each of them owes half the bridal payment, which is offset against the other. The child inherits from each of them the inheritance of a full son, and they inherit from the child the inheritance of one father. If the master has intercourse with the slave-woman of his mukatab and she gives birth and he claims the child: if the mukatab affirms him, the child's lineage is established from him, he owes the bridal payment, and he owes the value of her child, but she does not become his umm walad. If the mukatab denies the lineage, it is not established.
mukatab-main-1-1:
If the master enters into a kitabah (emancipation contract) with his slave or slave-woman for an amount of wealth he stipulates, and the slave accepts, the slave becomes a mukatab. The payment may be stipulated as immediately due, deferred, or in installments. The kitabah of a minor slave is valid if the minor understands buying and selling.

When the kitabah is validly established, the mukatab leaves the master's physical control but does not leave his ownership. The mukatab is permitted to buy, sell, and travel. He may not marry except with the master's permission. He may not make gifts or give charity except for small amounts, nor may he stand as guarantor.

If a child is born to him from his slave-woman, the child enters into his kitabah and has the same legal status as him, and the child's earnings belong to the mukatab. If the master marries his slave to his slave-woman and then enters into kitabah with both of them, and she gives birth from the slave, the child enters into her kitabah and the child's earnings belong to her.

If the master has intercourse with his female mukatab, he owes her the bridal payment (uqr). If he commits an offense against her or her child, he is liable for the offense. If he destroys her property, he must compensate her.

If the mukatab purchases his father or son, they enter into his kitabah. If he purchases his umm walad, her child enters into the kitabah and she may not be sold. If he purchases a relative of unmarriageable kinship who is not a direct ancestor or descendant, that relative does not enter into his kitabah according to Abu Hanifah.
mukatab-main-1-2:
If the mukatab is unable to pay an installment, the judge examines his situation. If he has a debt to collect or wealth that is arriving, the judge does not hasten to declare him incapable and waits two or three days for him.

If he has no means and the master requests his incapacitation, the judge declares him incapable and annuls the kitabah according to Abu Hanifah -- may Allah have mercy on him. The other two -- may Allah have mercy on them -- said: Not until he defaults on two consecutive installments. When the mukatab is declared incapable, he returns to the status of slavery and whatever he has in his possession belongs to his master.

If the mukatab dies and has wealth, the kitabah is not annulled. His kitabah payment is settled from his earnings, and he is deemed to have been manumitted in the last moment of his life. If he leaves no assets to cover the payment but leaves a child born during the kitabah, the child continues to pay the father's kitabah on its installments. When the child pays, the father is deemed to have been manumitted before his death, and the child is manumitted. If the mukatab leaves a child who was purchased into the kitabah, the child is told: "Either pay the kitabah immediately, or you are returned to slavery."

If a Muslim enters into kitabah with his slave for wine, swine, or the slave's own value, the kitabah is invalid.

If the slave delivers the wine, he is manumitted, and he must then work to pay his value. The amount is not reduced below the specified amount, nor is it increased.
mukatab-main-1-3:
If the kitabah is for an unspecified animal, the kitabah is valid. If a master enters into a single kitabah with two slaves, each being a guarantor for the other, the kitabah is valid. Whichever of them pays, both are manumitted, and the one who paid may recover half of what he paid from the other.

If the master manumits his mukatab, the slave is manumitted by his manumission and the kitabah payment is waived.

If the mukatab's master dies, the kitabah is not annulled. The mukatab is told: "Pay the amount to the master's heirs on its installments." If one of the heirs manumits him, that manumission does not take effect. If all the heirs manumit him, he is manumitted and the kitabah payment is waived.

If the master enters into kitabah with his umm walad, it is valid. If the master dies, the kitabah payment is waived. If a female mukatab gives birth from the master, she has the choice: she may continue with the kitabah if she wishes, or she may declare herself incapable and become his umm walad.
mukatab-main-1-4:
If the master enters into kitabah with his female mudabbar, it is valid. If the master dies and has no other wealth, she has the choice between working to earn two-thirds of her value or the entire kitabah amount. If the master makes his female mukatab a mudabbar, the tadbir is valid, and she has the choice: she may continue with the kitabah if she wishes, or she may declare herself incapable and become a mudabbar. If she continues with her kitabah and the master dies and has no other wealth, she has the choice between working to earn two-thirds of the kitabah amount or two-thirds of her value, according to Abu Hanifah.

If the mukatab manumits his slave for a sum of money, it is not valid.

If he makes a gift in exchange for compensation, it is not valid. If he enters into kitabah with his own slave, it is valid. If the second mukatab pays before the first is manumitted, his patronage (wala') belongs to the original master. If the second pays after the first mukatab's manumission, his patronage belongs to the first mukatab.
walaa-main-1:
If a man manumits his slave, the patronage (wala') belongs to him. Likewise if a woman manumits her slave. If he stipulates that the freed slave is released without patronage (sa'ibah), the stipulation is void and the patronage belongs to the one who manumitted.

If the mukatab pays and is manumitted, his patronage belongs to the master.

Likewise if the slave is manumitted after the master's death, the patronage belongs to the master's heirs. When the master dies, his mudabbar slaves and umm walad slaves are manumitted, and their patronage belongs to him. Whoever acquires ownership of a relative of unmarriageable kinship, that relative is manumitted, and the patronage belongs to the owner.

If a man's slave marries another man's slave-woman, and the owner of the slave-woman manumits her while she is pregnant by the slave, she is manumitted and her unborn child is manumitted. The patronage of the child belongs to the mother's master and never transfers from him. If she gives birth after manumission at more than six months, the child's patronage belongs to the mother's master. If the slave is then manumitted, he draws the patronage of his child, and it transfers from the mother's master to the father's master.

If a non-Arab man marries a freed Arab woman and she bears him children, the patronage of her children belongs to her patrons according to Abu Hanifah.

Patronage of manumission constitutes agnate succession (ta'sib). If the freed person has agnate relatives by blood, they have priority over the patron. If he has no agnate relatives by blood, his inheritance goes to the patron.

If the patron dies and then the freed person dies, his inheritance goes to the patron's sons, not his daughters. Women have no share of patronage except for those they manumitted, or those manumitted by someone they manumitted, or those they entered into kitabah with, or those whose kitabah was arranged by someone they entered into kitabah with. If the patron leaves a son and the sons of another son, the freed person's inheritance goes to the son, not the grandsons. Patronage belongs to the eldest.

If a man accepts Islam at the hand of another man and enters into a patronage agreement (muwaalah) on the condition that the patron inherits from him and pays blood-money on his behalf, or if he accepts Islam at the hand of someone else and then enters into patronage with the first, the patronage is valid and the blood-money liability is upon his patron. If the client dies with no heir, his inheritance goes to the patron. If he has an heir, the heir takes precedence. The client may transfer his patronage to another as long as the patron has not paid blood-money on his behalf. Once the patron has paid blood-money, the client may not transfer his patronage. A freed person whose patronage is through manumission may not enter into a patronage agreement with anyone.
ayman-main-1-1:
Oaths are of three types: a submerging oath (yamin ghamus), a binding oath (yamin mun'aqidah), and an idle oath (yamin laghw). The submerging oath is: swearing about a past matter while deliberately lying.

The one who takes this oath is sinful, and there is no expiation for it except seeking forgiveness (istighfar).

The binding oath is: swearing about a future matter -- that one will do something or will not do something. If one breaks this oath, the expiation becomes obligatory.

The idle oath is: swearing about a past matter believing it to be as he said, while the reality is otherwise. We hope that Allah will not hold a person accountable for this.

The intentional swearer, the coerced, and the forgetful are equal in this regard.

Whoever does the sworn act under coercion or forgetfulness is treated the same.

An oath by Allah Most High or by one of His names -- such as al-Rahman (the Most Merciful) and al-Rahim (the Most Compassionate) -- or by an attribute of His essence -- such as the might of Allah, His majesty, and His grandeur -- constitutes a valid oath. However, saying "by the knowledge of Allah" does not constitute an oath. If one swears by an attribute of action, such as the anger of Allah or His displeasure, it does not constitute an oath.

Whoever swears by other than Allah does not constitute an oath -- such as swearing by the Prophet, the Quran, or the Ka'bah.
ayman-main-1-2:
Swearing is done using the particles of oath. The particles of oath are: the waw (wa) as in "wallahi" (by Allah), the ba' as in "billahi" (by Allah), and the ta' as in "tallahi" (by Allah). The particles may be elided and one is still considered to have sworn, as when one says: "Allahi, I will not do such-and-such." Abu Hanifah said: If one says "wa haqqi-llah" (by the right of Allah), it is not an oath.

If one says: "I swear" (uqsimu), or "I swear by Allah" (uqsimu billahi), or "I take an oath by Allah" (ahlifu billahi), or "I testify" (ashhadu), or "I testify by Allah" (ashhadu billahi), he is considered to have sworn. Likewise, saying: "By the covenant of Allah and His compact," and "I have a vow" (alayya nadhrun) or "a vow to Allah," and "If I do such-and-such, I am a Jew, a Christian, or a disbeliever" -- all constitute oaths.

If one says: "May the anger of Allah be upon me" or "His displeasure," or "I am a fornicator, a wine-drinker, or a consumer of usury" -- these do not constitute oaths.

The expiation for an oath is: freeing a slave -- what suffices for dhihar suffices here.

Alternatively, one may clothe ten poor persons, each with one garment or more, the minimum being a garment in which prayer would be valid. Alternatively, one may feed ten poor persons in the same manner as the feeding in the expiation for dhihar. If one is unable to do any of these three, one must fast three consecutive days. If the expiation is given before the oath is broken, it does not suffice.
ayman-main-1-3:
Whoever swears to commit a sin -- such as not praying, not speaking to his father, or killing someone -- should break his oath and make expiation.

If a disbeliever takes an oath and then breaks it while still a disbeliever or after accepting Islam, there is no penalty upon him.

Whoever declares something he owns as forbidden to himself, it does not actually become forbidden. If he then partakes of it, he owes the expiation of an oath. If one says: "Everything lawful to me is forbidden," it applies to food and drink unless he intends otherwise.

Whoever makes an unconditional vow (nadhr) must fulfill it.

If one attaches the vow to a condition and the condition is fulfilled, one must fulfill the vow itself. It is narrated that Abu Hanifah retracted this position and said: If one says "If I do such-and-such, then I owe a Hajj, or a year's fasting, or charity of all I own," the expiation of an oath suffices instead. This is also the position of Muhammad.

Whoever swears not to enter a house: if he enters the Ka'bah, a mosque, a synagogue, or a church, he has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to speak: if he recites Quran in prayer, he has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to wear a garment while he is wearing it, and removes it immediately, has not
ayman-main-1-4:
broken his oath. Likewise if he swears not to ride this animal while he is riding it and dismounts immediately. If he remains for a while, he has broken his oath. If he swears not to enter this house while he is inside it, he has not broken his oath by staying until he leaves and then enters again.

Whoever swears not to enter a house and enters a ruined house has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to enter this house and enters it after it is demolished and becomes open land has broken his oath. If he swears not to enter this room and enters it after it is demolished, he has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to speak to so-and-so's wife, and so-and-so divorces her, and then he speaks to her, he has broken his oath.

If he swears not to speak to so-and-so's slave or not to enter so-and-so's house, and the owner sells his slave and house, and then he speaks to the slave and enters the house, he has not broken his oath. If he swears not to speak to the owner of this cloak and the owner sells it and he then speaks to the former owner, he has broken his oath. Likewise if he swears not to speak to this young man and speaks to him after he becomes an old man, he has broken his oath. Or if he swears not to eat the meat of this lamb and it becomes a ram and he eats it, he has broken his oath. If he swears not to eat from this palm tree, it applies to its fruit. If he swears not to eat from these unripe dates and they ripen and he eats them, he has not broken his oath. If he swears not to eat unripe dates and eats ripe dates, he has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to eat ripe dates and eats unripe dates that have partially ripened, he has broken his oath according to Abu Hanifah.
ayman-main-1-5:
Whoever swears not to eat meat and eats fish has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to drink from the Tigris and drinks from it using a vessel has not broken his oath -- until he drinks directly from it by bending down, according to Abu Hanifah. Whoever swears not to drink from the water of the Tigris and drinks from it using a vessel has broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to eat from this wheat and eats bread made from it has not broken his oath.

If he swears not to eat from this flour and eats bread made from it, he has broken his oath. If he eats the flour in its raw state, he has not broken his oath. If he swears not to speak to someone and speaks to him in a place where the person can hear but is asleep, he has broken his oath. If he swears not to speak to him except with his permission, and the person gives permission but the swearer does not know of it and speaks to him, he has broken his oath.

If the governor makes a man swear to inform him of every troublemaker who enters the city, this oath applies only during the governor's term of office.

Whoever swears not to ride so-and-so's animal and rides the animal of so-and-so's slave has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to enter this house and stands on its roof or enters its vestibule has broken his oath.

If he stands in the doorway such that when the door is closed he would be outside, he has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to eat roasted food -- it applies to meat, not to eggplant and carrots.

Whoever swears not to eat cooked food -- it applies to what is cooked from meat.
ayman-main-1-6:
Whoever swears not to eat heads -- the oath applies to what is roasted in ovens and sold in the market.

Whoever swears not to eat bread -- the oath applies to what the people of the land customarily eat as bread. If he eats pastry bread or rice bread in Iraq, he has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to sell, buy, or hire out, and then appoints an agent for that, has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to marry, divorce, or manumit, and then appoints an agent for that, has broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to sit on the ground and sits on a carpet or mat has not broken his oath.

Whoever swears not to sit on a bed and sits on a bed that has a carpet on it has broken his oath. If another bed is placed on top of the bed and he sits on the upper one, he has not broken his oath.

If he swears not to sleep on a mattress and sleeps on it with a covering over it, he has broken his oath. If another mattress is placed on top of it, he has not broken his oath.

Whoever takes an oath and says "God willing" (in sha' Allah) connected to his oath, he is not bound by the oath. If he swears to visit someone "if I am able," this refers to the ability of health, not absolute capability.

If he swears not to speak to someone "for a while" (hinan) or "for a time" (zamanan), or "the while," or "the time," it means six months. Likewise "an age" (dahr) according to Abu Yusuf and Muhammad.
ayman-main-1-7:
If he swears not to speak to him "for days" (ayyaman), it means three days. If he swears not to speak to him "the days" (al-ayyam), it means ten days according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: It means the days of the week. If he swears not to speak to him "the months" (al-shuhur), it means ten months according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Twelve months.

If one swears not to do something, one must abandon it forever. If one swears to do something, doing it once fulfills the oath.

Whoever swears that his wife shall not go out except with his permission: if he grants permission once and she goes out, then goes out again without permission, he has broken his oath. Permission is required for each outing. If he says: "Unless I give you permission" and gives her permission once, and she goes out again without permission, he has not broken his oath.

If one swears not to have lunch: lunch is eating from dawn to noon. Dinner is from the noon prayer to midnight. The pre-dawn meal (suhur) is from midnight to dawn.

If one swears to repay a debt "soon," it means within a month. If he says "in a long time," it means more than a month.

Whoever swears not to reside in this house and leaves it himself but leaves his family and belongings there has broken his oath.
ayman-main-1-8:
Whoever swears to ascend to the sky or to turn this stone into gold, his oath is binding and he immediately breaks it.

Whoever swears to repay someone's debt today and pays it, then the creditor finds some of the coins to be of inferior quality (zuyuf), or substandard (nabirajah), or subject to another's claim, the oath is not broken. If the coins turn out to be lead or counterfeit (stuqah), the oath is broken.

Whoever swears not to collect his debt "dirham by dirham" -- if he collects part of it, he has not broken his oath until he collects it in separate installments. If he collects his debt in two weighings without any activity between them other than the work of weighing, he has not broken his oath, and that is not considered separation.

Whoever swears to visit Basra and does not visit it until he dies, he breaks his oath in the last moment of his life.
wasaya-main-1-1:
A bequest is not obligatory, but it is recommended. A bequest to an heir is not valid unless the other heirs approve it. A bequest of more than one-third is not valid, nor is a bequest to the killer.

A Muslim may make a bequest to a non-Muslim, and a non-Muslim may make a bequest to a Muslim.

Acceptance of a bequest is after death. If the beneficiary accepts it during the testator's lifetime or rejects it, that is void.

It is recommended that a person bequeath less than one-third.

If a person appoints an executor and the executor accepts in the testator's presence but rejects in his absence, it is not a valid rejection. If he rejects in the testator's presence, it is a valid rejection.

The bequeathed item is owned upon acceptance, except in one case: if the testator dies and then the beneficiary dies before accepting, the bequeathed item enters the possession of the beneficiary's heirs.

If a person appoints a slave, a non-Muslim, or a corrupt person as executor, the judge removes them from the executorship and appoints others.

If a person appoints his own slave as executor and among the heirs there are adults, the appointment is not valid.

If a person appoints someone who is unable to fulfill the executorship, the judge appoints someone to assist him.
wasaya-main-1-2:
If a person appoints two executors, one may not act without the other according to Abu Hanifah and Muhammad -- except for purchasing the deceased's shroud and preparing him for burial, feeding and clothing the minors, returning a specific deposit, paying a debt, executing a specific bequest, freeing a specifically designated slave, and litigating regarding the deceased's rights.

If a person bequeaths one-third of his wealth to one person and one-third to another, and the heirs do not approve, the third is divided between them equally. If he bequeaths one-third to one and one-sixth to another, the third is divided between them in thirds. If he bequeaths all his wealth to one and one-third to another, and the heirs do not approve, the third is divided between them into four shares according to Abu Yusuf and Muhammad. Abu Hanifah said: The third is divided between them equally. Abu Hanifah does not count the beneficiary who was bequeathed more than one-third as competing for the excess, except in the case of a preferential sale, the working off of value (si'ayah), and unrestricted dirhams.

If a person makes a bequest while he has debts that encompass all his wealth, the bequest is not valid unless the creditors release the debt.

If a person bequeaths "the share of his son," the bequest is void. If he bequeaths "the equivalent of the share of his son," it is valid. If he has two sons, the beneficiary receives one-third.
wasaya-main-1-3:
Whoever manumits a slave during his illness, or sells at a preferential price, or gives a gift -- all of that is valid and is calculated from one-third, and the beneficiary competes with other bequest recipients.

If the preferential sale comes first and then manumission, the preferential sale takes priority according to Abu Hanifah. If manumission comes first and then the preferential sale, they are equal. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Manumission takes priority in both cases.

Whoever bequeaths "a share" (sahm) of his wealth, the beneficiary receives the smallest share among the heirs, unless it is less than one-sixth, in which case it is completed to one-sixth.

If he bequeaths "a portion" (juz') of his wealth, the heirs are told: "Give him whatever you wish."

Whoever makes bequests consisting of obligations to Allah Most High, the obligatory ones are given priority whether the testator mentioned them first or last -- such as Hajj, zakat, and expiations. As for non-obligatory ones, whichever the testator mentioned first takes priority.

Whoever bequeaths that the obligatory Hajj be performed on his behalf, a man from his city is sent to perform Hajj on his behalf, riding. If the bequest does not cover the expenses, Hajj is performed on his behalf from wherever the funds suffice.

Whoever leaves his city intending Hajj and dies on the way and bequeaths that Hajj be performed on his behalf, it is performed from his city according to Abu Hanifah.

The bequest of a minor and a mukatab -- even if the mukatab leaves enough to pay -- is not valid.

The testator may retract his bequest.
wasaya-main-1-4:
If he explicitly states the retraction, or performs an act indicating retraction, it is a retraction. Denying the bequest is not a retraction.

If a person bequeaths to his neighbors, they are the immediately adjacent ones according to Abu Hanifah.

If a person bequeaths to his in-laws (ashhar), the bequest goes to every relative of unmarriageable kinship of his wife. If he bequeaths to his in-laws (akhtan), the in-law is every female relative of unmarriageable kinship of his.

If a person bequeaths to his relatives, the bequest goes to the nearest then the next nearest among every relative of unmarriageable kinship. Parents and children are not included.

It goes to two persons or more. If he makes such a bequest and has two paternal uncles and two maternal uncles, the bequest goes to the two paternal uncles according to Abu Hanifah. If he has one paternal uncle and two maternal uncles, the paternal uncle gets half and the two maternal uncles get half. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: The bequest goes to everyone who can be traced to the furthest ancestor in Islam.

If a person bequeaths to someone one-third of his dirhams or one-third of his sheep, and two-thirds are destroyed and one-third remains, and that amount falls within one-third of the remaining estate, the beneficiary receives all of what remains. If he bequeaths one-third of his garments and two-thirds are destroyed and one-third remains, and that falls within one-third of the remaining estate, the beneficiary is only entitled to one-third of the remaining garments.
wasaya-main-1-5:
If a person bequeaths one thousand dirhams to someone and he has both liquid assets and debts, if the thousand can come from one-third of the liquid assets, it is given to the beneficiary. If it cannot, one-third of the liquid assets is given to him, and whenever any of the debts are collected, he takes one-third of what is collected until he has received the full thousand.

A bequest to an unborn child and of an unborn child is valid if the child is born within less than six months from the date of the bequest.

If a person bequeaths a slave-woman to someone except for her unborn child, the bequest and the exception are both valid.

If a person bequeaths a slave-woman to someone and she gives birth after the testator's death but before the beneficiary accepts, and then the beneficiary accepts, and both the mother and child can come from the third, both belong to the beneficiary. If they cannot come from the third, the beneficiary competes for the third and takes his share from both of them, according to Abu Yusuf and Muhammad.

Abu Hanifah said: He takes his share from the mother first, and if anything remains, he takes from the child.

A bequest of the service of a slave and the right to reside in a house for a specified number of years is valid, and a bequest of that in perpetuity is also valid. If the slave's full value can come from the third, the slave is handed over for service. If the testator has no other property, the slave serves the heirs for two days and the beneficiary for one day. If the beneficiary dies, the slave returns to the heirs. If the beneficiary dies during the testator's lifetime, the bequest is voided.
wasaya-main-1-6:
If a person bequeaths to the children of so-and-so, the bequest is divided among them equally: males and females share alike.

If a person bequeaths to the heirs of so-and-so, the bequest is divided among them: the male receives the share of two females.

If a person bequeaths one-third of his wealth to Zayd and Amr, and Amr is already deceased, the entire third goes to Zayd.

If he says: "One-third of my wealth is between Zayd and Amr," and Zayd is deceased, Amr receives half of the third.

If a person bequeaths one-third of his wealth and then acquires more wealth, the beneficiary is entitled to one-third of what the testator owns at the time of death. And Allah knows best.
faraid-intro-1-1:
The males upon whose inheritance there is consensus are ten: the son, the son's son however far the line descends, the father, the grandfather (the father's father) however far the line ascends, the brother, the brother's son, the paternal uncle, the paternal uncle's son, the husband, and the male patron (mawla al-ni'mah).

From among the females, seven: the daughter, the son's daughter, the mother, the grandmother, the sister, the wife, and the female patron (mawlat al-ni'mah).

Four categories do not inherit: the slave, the killer from the killed, the apostate, and people of two different religions. The fixed shares defined in the Book of Allah Most High are six: one-half, one-quarter, one-eighth, two-thirds, one-third, and one-sixth.

One-half is the share of the full sister (through both parents), and the half-sister through the father if there is no full sister, and the husband if the deceased has no child or son's child.

One-quarter is the share of the husband with a child or son's child, and of the wives if the deceased has no child or son's child.

One-eighth is the share of the wives with a child or son's child.

Two-thirds is the share of every two or more of those whose individual share is one-half, except the husband.

One-third is the share of the mother if the deceased has no child, no son's child, and no
faraid-intro-1-2:
two or more brothers and sisters. It is assigned to her in two specific cases -- namely, a husband and both parents, and a wife and both parents -- where she receives one-third of what remains after the share of the husband or wife. It is the share of every two or more children of the mother: their males and females share equally therein.

One-sixth is the share of seven: each of the parents with a child or son's child; the mother with brothers and sisters from any side; the grandmothers; the grandfather with a child or son's child; the son's daughters with a daughter; the half-sisters through the father with a full sister; and the single child of the mother.

The grandmothers are excluded by the mother. The grandfather and brothers and sisters are excluded by the father.

The children of the mother are excluded by four: a child, a son's child, the father, and the grandfather.

If the daughters take the full two-thirds, the son's daughters are excluded unless there is a son's son at their level or below who makes them residuary heirs.

If the full sisters take the full two-thirds, the half-sisters through the father are excluded unless there is a half-brother through the father who makes them residuary heirs.

Chapter: The Nearest Agnate Heirs

The nearest agnate heirs are: the sons, then their sons, then the father, then the grandfather, then the sons of the father -- who are the brothers.
faraid-intro-1-3:
Then the sons of the grandfather -- who are the paternal uncles -- then the sons of the grandfather's father.

If sons of the same father are equal in degree, the one who is related through both parents takes precedence.

The son, the son's son, and the brothers share with their sisters such that the male receives the share of two females. All other agnate heirs inherit exclusively as males, to the exclusion of their females.

If there is no agnate heir by lineage, the agnate heir is the male patron who manumitted, then the nearest agnate of the patron.
faraid-bab-1-1:
What remains after the shares of daughters goes to the sons' sons and their sisters, the male receiving the share of two females.

What remains after the shares of two full sisters goes to the half-brothers and half-sisters through the father, the male receiving the share of two females.

If a person leaves a daughter and sons' sons, the daughter receives one-half and the remainder goes to the sons' sons and their sisters, the male receiving the share of two females. Likewise, what remains after the full sister's share goes to the half-brothers and half-sisters through the father, the male receiving the share of two females.

If a person leaves two paternal cousins, one of whom is also a half-brother through the mother, the half-brother through the mother receives one-sixth and the remainder is divided between both of them.

The shared case (al-mushtarakah) is when a woman dies leaving a husband, a mother or grandmother, two half-sisters through the mother, and a full brother. The husband receives one-half, the mother receives one-sixth, the children of the mother receive one-third, and the full brothers receive nothing.
faraid-bab-2-1:
What remains after the shares of the fixed-share holders, if there is no agnate heir, is returned to them in proportion to their shares -- except to the husband and wife.

The killer does not inherit from the killed. All forms of disbelief constitute one religious community, and its members inherit from each other.

A Muslim does not inherit from a non-Muslim, nor does a non-Muslim inherit from a Muslim. The wealth of an apostate goes to his Muslim heirs. What he earned during apostasy is spoils (fay').

If a group drowns together or a wall collapses on them and it is not known who died first, the wealth of each one goes to his living heirs.

If a Zoroastrian has two relationships to the deceased -- such that if those relationships were held by two different people, one would inherit alongside the other -- then the Zoroastrian inherits by both relationships. The Zoroastrian does not inherit through the invalid marriages that they consider lawful in their religion.

The agnate heirs of a child born of fornication and a child of mutual imprecation (li'an) are the patron of the mother.

If a person dies leaving an unborn child, his estate is held until his wife delivers, according to Abu Hanifah.

The grandfather takes precedence in inheritance over the brothers according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: He shares with them, unless the sharing would reduce his portion below one-third.

If grandmothers are present together, one-sixth goes to the nearest of them.

The grandfather excludes his own mother.

The mother of the maternal grandfather does not inherit by fixed share. Every grandmother excludes her own mother.
faraid-bab-3-1:
If the deceased has no agnate heir and no fixed-share holder, his distant kindred (dhawu al-arham) inherit from him. They are ten: the child of a daughter, the child of a sister, the daughter of a brother, the daughter of a paternal uncle, the maternal uncle, the maternal aunt, the mother's father, the paternal uncle through the mother, the paternal aunt, and the child of a brother through the mother -- and all who derive their relationship through them.

The most entitled among them is the child of the deceased, then the children of the parents or one of them -- who are the daughters of brothers and children of sisters -- then the children of the grandparents or one of them -- who are the maternal uncles, maternal aunts, and paternal aunts.

If the children of a father are equal in degree, the one who derives through an heir has priority, and the nearest has priority over the more distant. The mother's father has priority over the child of a brother or sister.

The manumitter has more right to what remains after the fixed-share holders if there is no other agnate heir. The patron of contractual allegiance also inherits.

If the manumitter leaves the freed slave's father and son, his estate goes to the son. Abu Yusuf said: The father receives one-sixth and the remainder goes to the son. If he leaves the freed slave's grandfather and brother, the estate goes to the grandfather according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: It is divided between them.

Patronage is neither sold nor gifted.
faraid-bab-4-1-1:
If the case contains one-half and one-half, or one-half and a remainder, its base is two. If it contains one-third and a remainder, or two-thirds and a remainder, its base is three. If it contains one-quarter and a remainder, its base is four. If it contains one-eighth and a remainder, its base is eight. If it contains one-sixth and a remainder, or one-half and one-third, or one-sixth, its base is six, and it may increase (ta'ul) to seven, eight, nine, or ten.

If one-quarter is combined with one-third or one-sixth, its base is twelve, and it may increase to thirteen, fifteen, or seventeen.

If one-eighth is combined with two-thirds or one-sixth, its base is twenty-four, and it may increase to twenty-seven.

If the case divides evenly among the heirs, it is resolved. If the shares of a group do not divide evenly among them, multiply their number by the base of the case (and its increase if applicable), and the result is the number from which the case is resolved. For example: a wife and two brothers -- the wife receives one-quarter, which is one share, and the two brothers receive the remainder, which is three shares that do not divide evenly between them. Multiply two by the base of the case, yielding eight, and from that the case is resolved.

If their shares are compatible with their number, multiply the compatible portion of their number by the base of the case. For example: a wife and six
faraid-bab-4-1-2:
brothers -- the wife receives one-quarter, which is one share, and the brothers receive three. Multiply the compatible portion of their number by the base of the case, yielding eight, and from that the case is resolved.

If the shares of two or more groups do not divide evenly, multiply one group's number by the other's, then the result by the third group, then the result by the base of the case.

If the numbers are equal, one suffices for the other -- as in the case of two wives and two brothers: multiply two by the base of the case.

If one number is a factor of the other, the larger suffices -- as in four wives and two brothers: multiplying by four suffices.

If one number shares a common factor with the other, multiply the compatible portion of one by the entirety of the other, then the result by the base of the case. For example: four wives, a sister, and six paternal uncles -- six and four share a common factor of two. Multiply half of one by the entirety of the other, then the result by the base of the case, yielding forty-eight, and from that the case is resolved.

Once the case is resolved, multiply each heir's shares by the estate, then divide the result by the number from which the case was resolved, and the result is that heir's entitlement.
faraid-bab-4-1-3:
If the estate has not been divided when one of the heirs dies: if the deceased heir's share from the first estate divides evenly among his heirs, then both cases are resolved from the base of the first. If it does not divide evenly, resolve the second deceased's estate using the method described, then multiply one case by the other if there is no common factor between the second deceased's shares and the base from which his estate was resolved. If there is a common factor, multiply the compatible portion of the second case by the first, and the result resolves both cases.

Everyone who has a share from the first case has it multiplied by the compatible portion of the second case. Everyone who has a share from the second case has it multiplied by the compatible portion of the second deceased's estate. When the case of successive deaths is resolved and you wish to know each person's share in units of the dirham, divide the number from which the case was resolved by forty-eight, and the result indicates the fraction of a dirham for each heir's shares. And Allah knows best what is correct.
jinayat-main-1-1:
Homicide is of five types: intentional, quasi-intentional, accidental, what is treated as accidental, and killing by indirect cause.

Intentional homicide is when one deliberately strikes with a weapon or what functions as a weapon in severing body parts, such as a sharpened piece of wood, stone, or fire. Its legal consequence is:

Sin and retribution (qisas), unless the heirs pardon. There is no expiation (kaffarah) for it.

Quasi-intentional homicide, according to Abu Hanifah, is when one deliberately strikes with what is neither a weapon nor what functions as a weapon. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: If he strikes with a large stone or a large piece of wood, it is intentional. Quasi-intentional is when one deliberately strikes with what does not typically kill. The legal consequence according to both opinions is: sin, expiation, no retribution, and an aggravated blood money (diyah) payable by the paternal relatives (aqilah).

Accidental killing is of two types: mistake in intent, which is when one shoots at a person thinking it is game, and it turns out to be a human being; and mistake in action, which is when one shoots at a target and hits a human being. Its legal consequence is: expiation and blood money payable by the paternal relatives, and there is no sin in it.

What is treated as accidental: such as a sleeping person who rolls over onto someone and kills them — its ruling is the same as accidental killing.
jinayat-main-1-2:
As for killing by indirect cause: such as one who digs a well or places a stone on property not his own — its consequence, when a human being perishes thereby, is: blood money payable by the paternal relatives, and there is no expiation for it.

Retribution (qisas) is obligatory for the killing of every person whose blood is permanently inviolable, when killed intentionally.

A free person is killed for a free person, a free person for a slave, and a Muslim for a dhimmi (protected non-Muslim). A Muslim is not killed for a person granted safe-conduct (musta'man). A man is killed for a woman, an adult for a child, and a healthy person for a blind or disabled person. A man is not killed for his son, nor for his slave, nor his mudabbar (slave promised freedom upon death), nor his mukatab (slave in a manumission contract), nor for his son's slave. Whoever inherits a right of retribution against his father — that right is extinguished.

Retribution is carried out only by the sword.

If the mukatab is killed intentionally and he has no heir except the master, and he left behind enough wealth to fulfill his contract, then the master has the right of retribution. If he left wealth and his heir is other than the master, there is no retribution for them, even if they join with the master.

If a pledged slave is killed, retribution is not carried out until the pledgor and the pledgee agree.

Whoever wounds a man intentionally, and the victim remains bedridden until he dies, then retribution is due. Whoever cuts off another's hand intentionally from the joint — his hand is cut off. Likewise for the foot, the soft part of the nose, and the ear.
jinayat-main-1-3:
Whoever strikes a man's eye and gouges it out — there is no retribution upon him. But if the eye remained intact and its sight was lost, then retribution is due: a mirror is heated for him, wet cotton is placed on his face, and his eye is exposed to the mirror until its sight is destroyed.

Retribution applies to the tooth, and to every head wound in which equivalence is possible. There is no retribution for any bone except the tooth. In offenses short of taking life, there is no quasi-intentional category — it is either intentional or accidental.
