There is no retribution between a man and a woman in offenses short of taking life, nor between a free person and a slave, nor between two slaves.

Retribution in the limbs applies between a Muslim and a non-Muslim. Whoever cuts a man's hand from the middle of the forearm, or inflicts a deep abdominal wound (ja'ifah) upon him, and he recovers from it — there is no retribution upon the offender.

If the hand of the victim and the hand of the offender are both paralyzed, or missing fingers, the victim has a choice: if he wishes, he may have the defective hand cut off with nothing further due to him; and if he wishes, he may take the full compensation (arsh).

Whoever inflicts a head wound upon a man that covers the entire area between his temples, while the wound would not cover the space between the offender's temples — the victim has a choice: if he wishes, he may claim retribution to the extent of his wound, starting from whichever side he chooses; and if he wishes, he may take the monetary compensation (arsh).
jinayat-main-1-4:
There is no retribution for the tongue, nor for the male organ, unless the glans is cut off.

If the killer and the heirs of the victim settle upon a sum of money, retribution is extinguished and the money becomes due, whether it be little or much. If one of the partners pardons or settles his share for compensation, the right of the rest to retribution is extinguished, and they are entitled to their share of the blood money. If a group kills one person intentionally, retribution is exacted from all of them.

If one person kills a group, and the heirs of all the victims are present, he is killed for all of them and they are entitled to nothing further. If only one heir is present, the killer is executed for that victim and the rights of the rest are extinguished. If a person upon whom retribution is obligatory dies,

the retribution is extinguished. If two men cut off a man's hand, there is no retribution upon either of them, and they owe half the blood money. If one person cuts off the right hands of two men, and both are present, they may cut off his hand and take from him half the blood money, dividing it equally between them. If only one of them is present and cuts off his hand, the other is entitled to half the blood money from him.

If a slave confesses to intentional killing, retribution is due upon him.

Whoever shoots a man intentionally and the arrow passes through him into another, and both die — retribution is due for the first victim, and the blood money for the second is upon the shooter's paternal relatives.
diyat-intro-1-1:
If a man kills another in a quasi-intentional manner, the aggravated blood money is upon his paternal relatives (aqilah), and expiation is upon him. The blood money for quasi-intentional killing according to Abu Hanifah and Abu Yusuf is one hundred camels in four categories: twenty-five bint makhad (one-year-old female camels), twenty-five bint labun (two-year-old female camels), twenty-five hiqqah (three-year-old female camels), and twenty-five jadh'ah (four-year-old female camels). Aggravation

is established only in camels specifically. If the blood money is assessed in something other than camels, it is not aggravated.

Accidental killing obligates blood money upon the paternal relatives and expiation upon the killer.

The blood money for accidental killing is one hundred camels in five categories: twenty bint makhad, twenty ibn makhad (one-year-old male camels), twenty bint labun, twenty hiqqah, and twenty jadh'ah. According to Abu Hanifah, blood money is established only from these three types. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: from cattle, two hundred head of cattle; from sheep, two thousand sheep; from garments, two hundred sets of garments, each set consisting of two pieces. The blood money of a Muslim and a dhimmi is the same.
diyat-intro-1-2:
For the life, the full blood money is due. For the soft part of the nose, the full blood money. For the tongue, the full blood money. For the male organ, the full blood money. For the intellect — if one strikes his head and his intellect is lost — the full blood money. For the beard, if it is shaved and does not grow back, the full blood money. For the hair of the head, the full blood money. For the two eyebrows, the full blood money. For the two eyes, the full blood money. For the two hands, the full blood money. For the two feet, the full blood money. For the two ears, the full blood money. For the two lips, the full blood money. For the two testicles, the full blood money. For the two breasts

of a woman, the full blood money.

For each one of these paired organs, half the blood money. For the four eyelids, the full blood money. For one eyelid, one quarter of the blood money. For each finger of the hands and feet, one-tenth of the blood money. All fingers are equal. Every finger has three joints, so for one joint, one-third of the blood money of the finger. What has two joints — for one of them, half the blood money of the finger. For each tooth, five camels. All teeth and molars are equal.

Whoever strikes a limb and destroys its function, the full blood money is due,

as if he had cut it off — such as a hand that becomes paralyzed, or an eye that loses its sight.

Head wounds are ten types: al-harisah (scratch), al-dami'ah (weeping), al-damiyah (bleeding), al-badi'ah (flesh-exposing), al-mutalahimah (deep flesh wound), al-simhaq (membrane-exposing), al-mudihah (bone-exposing), al-hashimah (bone-fracturing), al-munaqilah (bone-displacing), and al-ammah (brain-membrane reaching).
diyat-intro-1-3:
For the bone-exposing wound (mudihah), retribution applies if it was intentional. There is no retribution for the remaining types of head wounds. For what is less than a mudihah, an equitable assessment (hukumat adl) applies.

For the mudihah, if it was accidental: half of one-tenth of the blood money. For the hashimah (bone-fracturing): one-tenth of the blood money. For the munaqilah (bone-displacing): one-tenth plus half of one-tenth of the blood money. For the ammah (brain-membrane reaching): one-third of the blood money. For the ja'ifah (deep abdominal wound): one-third of the blood money.

If it penetrates through, then it constitutes two deep wounds, and two-thirds of the blood money is due. For the fingers of one hand: half the blood money.

If he cuts them off along with the palm, the full blood money is due. If he cuts them from the middle of the forearm, half the blood money is due for the palm, and an equitable assessment for the excess. For an extra finger, an equitable assessment applies.

For the eye of an infant, his male organ, or his tongue — when their soundness is not established — an equitable assessment applies. Whoever inflicts a mudihah upon a man and thereby causes him to lose his intellect or the hair of his head — the compensation for the mudihah is subsumed within the blood money.

If his hearing, sight, or speech is lost, the compensation for the mudihah is due in addition to the blood money. Whoever cuts off a man's finger and a neighboring finger becomes paralyzed as a result — compensation is due for both, and there is no retribution according to Abu Hanifah.

Whoever knocks out a man's tooth and another grows in its place — the compensation is extinguished. Whoever inflicts a head wound upon a man
diyat-intro-1-4:
and the wound heals, leaving no trace, and the hair grows back — the compensation is extinguished according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf said: the compensation for pain is due. Muhammad said: the doctor's fee is due. Whoever wounds a man — retribution is not exacted from him until the wound heals.

Whoever cuts off a man's hand accidentally and then kills him before the wound heals — the full blood money is due and

the compensation for the hand is extinguished.

Every intentional offense in which retribution is waived due to a legal doubt (shubhah) — the blood money is upon the killer's own wealth. Every compensation established through settlement — it is upon the killer's own wealth.

If a father kills his son intentionally, the blood money is payable from his own wealth over three years. Every offense to which the offender confesses — it is upon his own wealth, and his testimony is not accepted against his paternal relatives.

The intentional act of a child or an insane person is treated as accidental, and the blood money is upon the paternal relatives.

Whoever digs a well in the Muslims' public road or places a stone, and a person perishes thereby — the blood money is upon his paternal relatives. If an animal perishes thereby, the liability is upon his own wealth. If one extends a projecting structure, a gutter, or a drainpipe over the road, and it falls upon a person causing death — the blood money is upon his paternal relatives. There is no expiation upon the digger of a well or the placer of a stone.
diyat-intro-1-5:
Whoever digs a well on his own property and a person perishes thereby — he is not liable. The rider is liable for what the riding animal tramples with its hooves, and for what it strikes with its forelegs, or bites. He is not liable for what it kicks with its hind legs or strikes with its tail.

If the animal defecates or urinates on the road and a person slips and is harmed thereby — he is not liable. The driver is liable for what the animal strikes with its forelegs or hind legs. The one leading the animal is liable for what it strikes with its forelegs only, not its hind legs.

Whoever leads a string of camels is liable for what they trample. If there is also a driver with him,

then liability falls upon both of them. When a slave commits an accidental offense, his master is told: either surrender him on account of it, or ransom him.

If he surrenders him, the claimant of the offense takes ownership of him. If he ransoms him with the compensation — then if the slave offends again, the ruling on the second offense is the same as the first. If he offends again, the master is told: either surrender him to the claimants of both offenses, who divide him in proportion to their claims, or ransom him with the compensation for each of them. If the master frees him without knowledge of the offense, he is liable for the lesser of the slave's value or the compensation. If the master sells him or frees him after learning of the offense, the full compensation becomes due upon him.
diyat-intro-1-6:
If a mudabbar (slave promised freedom upon death) or an umm walad (mother of the master's child) commits an accidental offense, the master is liable for the lesser of the slave's value or the compensation. If the slave commits another offense and the master has already paid the value to the claimant of the first offense by court order, then nothing further is due upon him, and the claimant of the second offense pursues the claimant of the first offense to share in what was taken. If the master paid the value without court order, the second claimant has a choice: he may pursue either the master or the claimant of the first offense.

If a wall leans toward the Muslims' public road and its owner is demanded to demolish it, and witnesses are called against him, and he fails to demolish it within a time in which he could have done so, until it collapses — he is liable for whatever is destroyed by it, whether life or property.

It makes no difference whether the demand for demolition is made by a Muslim or a dhimmi. If the wall leans toward a man's house, the right to demand demolition belongs exclusively to the house owner. If two riders collide and both die, the paternal relatives of each are liable for the blood money of the other.

If a man kills a slave accidentally, he owes its value, not exceeding ten thousand dirhams. If its value is ten thousand or more, he is assessed ten thousand minus ten dirhams.

For a female slave whose value exceeds the blood money: five thousand minus ten dirhams.
diyat-intro-1-7:
For the hand of a slave: half his value, not exceeding five thousand minus five dirhams. Everything that is proportionally fixed in the blood money of a free person is proportionally fixed in the value of a slave.

If someone strikes the belly of a woman and she miscarries a dead fetus, he owes a ghurrah.

The ghurrah is half of one-tenth of the blood money. If the fetus is delivered alive and then dies, the full blood money is due. If the fetus is delivered dead and then the mother dies, the blood money and a ghurrah are both due. If the mother dies and then the fetus is delivered dead, the blood money is due for the mother and nothing for the fetus.

What is due for the fetus is inherited from the fetus. For the fetus of a female slave, if it is male: half of one-tenth of its value had it been alive; and one-tenth of its value if female. There is no expiation for the fetus.

The expiation for quasi-intentional and accidental killing is: freeing a believing slave. If one cannot find a slave, then fasting two consecutive months. Feeding does not suffice for this expiation.
diyat-bab-1-1:
If a slain person is found in a neighborhood and it is not known who killed him, fifty men from among them are made to swear —

chosen by the claimant — by Allah that they did not kill him and do not know his killer. Once they have sworn, the blood money is assessed upon the people of the neighborhood. The claimant is not made to swear, nor is the offense attributed to him. If the people of the neighborhood do not reach fifty, the oaths are repeated among them until fifty oaths are completed.

Neither a minor, nor an insane person,

nor a woman, nor a slave is included in the compurgation. If a dead person is found with no marks of violence upon him, there is no compurgation and no blood money. Likewise, if blood is flowing from his nose, his backside, or his mouth. But if blood is coming from his eyes or his ears, he is considered a homicide victim.

If the slain person is found on an animal being driven by a man, the blood money is upon that man's paternal relatives, not the people of the neighborhood. If the slain person is found in someone's house, the compurgation is upon that person and the blood money upon his paternal relatives.

Tenants are not included in the compurgation along with property owners according to Abu Hanifah, and it falls upon the original settlers,

not subsequent purchasers, even if only one of them remains. If the slain person is found on a ship, the compurgation is upon those aboard — both passengers and crew. If the slain person is found in the mosque of a neighborhood, the compurgation is upon its people. If found in the congregational mosque or the main thoroughfare, there is no compurgation, and the blood money is upon the public treasury. If found in open land with no habitation nearby, it is uncompensated (hadr). If found between two villages, it is upon the nearer of the two. If found in the middle of the Euphrates where the current carries him, it is uncompensated.

If the body is retained on the riverbank, then it falls upon the nearest villages from that

location.

If the claimant accuses a specific person from the neighborhood, the compurgation is not lifted from them. If he accuses someone from outside the neighborhood, the compurgation is lifted from them.

If the person being sworn says: "So-and-so killed him," he is made to swear by Allah that he did not kill him and does not know any killer other than the named person.

If two witnesses from the neighborhood testify against a man from outside it that he killed the victim, their testimony is not accepted.
diyat-bab-2-1:
The blood money in quasi-intentional and accidental killing, and every blood money arising from the act of killing itself, is upon the paternal relatives (aqilah).

The aqilah consists of those registered in the military rolls (diwan).

If the killer is from the people of the diwan, the blood money is taken from their stipends over three years. If the stipends are distributed in more or less than three years, they are collected from whatever is distributed. Whoever is not from the people of the diwan — his aqilah is his tribe, and it is apportioned among them over three years. No individual is assessed more than four dirhams per year, though it may be less. If the tribe is insufficient for this, the nearest related tribes are added to them.

The killer is included among the aqilah and pays the same share as each of them.

The aqilah of a freed slave is the tribe of his former master. The patron by contract of clientage (mawla al-muwalat) — his patron and his patron's tribe bear his blood money obligations. The aqilah does not bear less than half of one-tenth of the blood money, but they bear half of one-tenth and above. What falls below that is upon the offender's own wealth.

The aqilah does not bear the offense of a slave, nor an offense to which the offender confesses — unless they confirm it — nor what is established through settlement.

If a free person commits an accidental offense against a slave, it is upon his paternal relatives.
hudud-intro-1-1:
Fornication (zina) is established by evidence and by confession.

As for evidence: four witnesses must testify against a man or a woman regarding fornication. The ruler (imam) questions them about the fornication:

What is it? How did it occur? Where did it happen? With whom? And when? If they clarify all of this and say: "We saw him penetrate her in her vagina like a kohl stick in its container," and the judge investigates them and they are found upright in secret and in public, he rules according to their testimony.

As for confession: the sane adult confesses to fornication four times in four separate sessions of the confessor. Each time he confesses, the judge turns him away. When his confession is completed four times, the ruler asks him about the fornication: What is it? How did it occur? Where did it happen? With whom? When he clarifies all of this, the fixed punishment (hadd) is imposed upon him.

If the fornicator is muhsan (a person who has been in a valid consummated marriage), he is stoned to death with stones. He is taken out to open ground; the witnesses begin the stoning, then the ruler, then the people. If the witnesses refuse to begin, the punishment is dropped.

If he was a confessor, the ruler begins, then the people. The body is washed, shrouded, and the funeral prayer is performed over it.
hudud-intro-1-2:
If he is not muhsan and is a free person, his punishment is one hundred lashes. The ruler orders him to be struck with a whip that has no knot, with moderate strikes. His clothes are removed and the strikes are distributed over his body parts, except his head, face, and private parts. If he is a slave, he is lashed

fifty times. Likewise for a female slave.

If the confessor retracts his confession before the punishment is carried out upon him or during it, his retraction is accepted and he is released.

It is recommended for the ruler to prompt the confessor to retract, saying: "Perhaps you only touched or kissed."

Men and women are equal in this, except that a woman's clothes are not removed from her, only the fur and padded garments. If a pit is dug for her during stoning, it is permissible.

The master does not carry out the fixed punishment upon his slave except with the ruler's permission.

If one of the witnesses retracts after the judgment but before the stoning, they are all given the hadd punishment for false accusation, and the stoning is dropped. If he retracts after the stoning, only the retracting witness is given the hadd punishment,

and he is liable for one-quarter of the blood money. If the number of witnesses falls below four, they are all given the hadd punishment for false accusation.

The conditions for ihsan (which requires stoning) are: that the accused be free, adult, sane, Muslim, and have married a woman in a valid marriage and consummated it, both being in the state of ihsan.
hudud-intro-1-3:
Lashing and stoning are not combined for the muhsan, nor are lashing and exile combined for the unmarried offender — unless the ruler deems it beneficial, in which case he exiles the person for a period he sees fit. If a sick person commits fornication and stoning is his punishment, he is stoned. If lashing is his punishment, he is not lashed until he recovers.

If a pregnant woman commits fornication, she is not punished until she delivers. If her punishment is lashing, then until she recovers from childbirth. If her punishment is stoning, she is stoned.

If witnesses testify to a stale charge of a fixed punishment, and their delay in presenting it to the ruler was not caused by their distance from him, their

testimony is not accepted — except in the case of false accusation (qadhf) specifically.

Whoever has intercourse with a non-related woman in what is below the vagina is given a discretionary punishment (ta'zir).

There is no fixed punishment upon one who has intercourse with his son's or grandson's slave-girl, even if he says: "I knew she is forbidden to me." If he has intercourse with his father's, mother's, or wife's slave-girl, or if a slave has intercourse with his master's slave-girl, and he says: "I knew she is forbidden to me" — the fixed punishment is applied. If he says: "I thought she was lawful for me" — the fixed punishment is applied.

If a woman other than his wife is brought to a man and the women say: "This is your wife," and he has intercourse with her, there is no fixed punishment upon him,

but the dower (mahr) is due.
hudud-intro-1-4:
If one finds a woman on his bed and has intercourse with her, the fixed punishment applies. If one marries a woman whose marriage to him is unlawful and has intercourse with her, the fixed punishment does not apply. Whoever commits anal intercourse with a woman, or commits the act of the people of Lut (sodomy), there is no fixed punishment upon him according to Abu Hanifah, but he receives a discretionary punishment (ta'zir). Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: it is treated like fornication. Whoever has intercourse with an animal, there is no fixed punishment upon him.

Whoever commits fornication in the territory of war or the territory of rebels, and then comes to us, we do not carry out the fixed punishment upon him.
hudud-bab-1-1:
Whoever drinks wine and is apprehended while the smell of it is still present, and witnesses testify to that against him, or he confesses — the fixed punishment is upon him. If he confesses after the smell has disappeared, he is not punished.

Whoever becomes intoxicated from nabidh (date wine) is punished.

There is no fixed punishment upon one from whom the smell of wine is detected or who vomits it. The drunkard is not punished until it is established that he became intoxicated from nabidh and drank it voluntarily. He is not punished until the intoxication passes.

The fixed punishment for wine-drinking and intoxication is eighty lashes, distributed over his body as we described regarding fornication. If he is a slave, his punishment is forty lashes.

Whoever confesses to drinking wine or intoxication and then retracts — he is not punished.

Drinking is established by the testimony of two witnesses or by a single confession. The testimony of women alongside men is not accepted in this matter.
hudud-bab-2-1:
If a man accuses a muhsan man or a muhsan woman of fornication with explicit words, and the accused demands the punishment, the judge lashes the accuser eighty lashes if he is a free person, distributing the strikes over his body parts. His clothes are not removed except for fur and padded garments. If he is a slave, he is lashed forty times.

Ihsan (for the purposes of this punishment) requires: that the accused be free, sane, adult, Muslim, and chaste — meaning free from having committed fornication.

Whoever denies another's lineage and says: "You are not your father's son," or "O son of a fornicatress," and his mother is deceased and was muhsan, and the son demands the punishment — the accuser is punished. No one may demand the punishment for false accusation of a deceased person except one whose lineage is impugned by the accusation.

If the accused was muhsan, his disbelieving son or slave son may demand the punishment. A slave may not demand the punishment from his master for accusing his free mother.

If one confesses to false accusation and then retracts, the retraction is not accepted.

Whoever says to an Arab: "O Nabataean" — he is not punished. Whoever says to a man: "O son of the water of the sky" — he is not considered an accuser. If he attributes him to his uncle, his maternal uncle, or his mother's husband — he is not considered an accuser.

Whoever was involved in unlawful intercourse not involving ownership — whoever accuses him is not punished. The woman who has been subjected to li'an (mutual imprecation) on account of a child — whoever accuses her is not punished.

Whoever accuses a slave, a female slave, or a non-Muslim of fornication, or accuses a Muslim

of something other than fornication, saying: "O corrupt one," or "O disbeliever," or "O wicked one" — he receives a discretionary punishment (ta'zir). If he says: "O donkey" or "O pig" — he is not given ta'zir.

Ta'zir: its maximum is thirty-nine lashes and its minimum is three lashes. Abu Yusuf said: ta'zir may reach seventy-five lashes. If the ruler sees fit to combine imprisonment with beating in ta'zir, he may do so.

The severest beating is ta'zir, then the hadd for fornication, then the hadd for drinking, then the hadd for false accusation.

Whoever is given the hadd punishment or ta'zir by the ruler and dies — his blood is uncompensated (hadr).

If a Muslim is given the hadd for false accusation, his testimony is rejected even if he repents. If a non-Muslim is given the hadd for false accusation and then accepts Islam, his testimony is accepted. And Allah knows best.
sariqah-main-1-1:
If a sane adult steals ten dirhams or what is worth ten dirhams — whether minted or unminted — from a secured place (hirz) in which there is no legal doubt (shubhah), amputation (qat') is obligatory upon him.

The slave and the free person are equal regarding amputation.

Amputation is obligatory based on a single confession or the testimony of two witnesses.

If a group participates in a theft and each person's share amounts to ten dirhams, they are amputated. If one's share is less than that, he is not amputated.

There is no amputation for what is found abundantly and without value in the Muslim lands, such as wood, reeds, grass, fish, birds, and game. Likewise for what spoils quickly, such as fresh fruits, milk, meat, watermelon, fruit on trees, and unharvested crops.

There is no amputation for intoxicating beverages.
sariqah-main-1-2:
Nor for birds, nor for stealing a Mushaf (Quran copy) — even if it has ornamental metalwork — nor for a gold crucifix, nor for chess pieces, nor for backgammon pieces. There is no amputation for stealing a free child even if he is wearing jewelry. There is no amputation for stealing an adult slave, but there is amputation for stealing a young slave. There is no amputation for books of any kind except accounting ledgers. There is no amputation for stealing a dog, a cheetah, a tambourine, a drum, or a flute. Amputation applies for teak wood, ebony, and sandalwood. When utensils or doors are made from wood, amputation applies for stealing them. There is no amputation for a betrayer of trust (male or female), nor for a grave robber,

nor for a plunderer, nor for a snatcher.

A thief is not amputated for stealing from the public treasury, nor from property in which the thief has a share. Whoever

steals from his parents, his children, or a person with whom he shares an unmarriageable kinship (mahram) is not amputated. Likewise if one spouse steals from the other, or a slave from his master, or from his master's wife, or from the husband of his female master, or a master from his mukatab, or a thief from war spoils.

Secured places (hirz) are of two types: places that are inherently secure
sariqah-main-1-3:
such as houses and buildings; and places secured by a guard. Whoever steals something from a secured place or an unsecured place while its owner is present guarding it — amputation is obligatory upon him. There is no amputation for one who steals from a bathhouse or from a house that people are permitted to enter.

Whoever steals goods from a mosque while their owner is present — amputation applies. There is no amputation for a guest who steals from his host. If a burglar breaks into a house, enters, takes the property, and passes it to another person outside — there is no amputation for either of them. If he throws it into the road and then exits and takes it — he is amputated.

Likewise if he loads it onto a donkey and drives it out. If a group enters the secured place and some of them take the property — all of them are amputated.

Whoever breaks into a house and reaches his hand inside to take something — he is not amputated. If he reaches his hand into a money-changer's box or into another person's sleeve and takes money — he is amputated.

The right hand of the thief is amputated at the wrist and cauterized. If he steals a second time, his left foot is amputated. If he steals a third time, he is not amputated but is imprisoned indefinitely until he repents.

If the thief's left hand is paralyzed, or amputated, or his right foot has been

amputated — he is not amputated.
sariqah-main-1-4:
The thief is not amputated unless the victim of the theft is present and demands the stolen property. If he gifts it to the thief, or sells it to him, or if its value drops below the minimum threshold — he is not amputated.

Whoever steals an item and is amputated for it, and returns it, and then steals it again while it is in the same condition — he is not amputated. But if it has changed from its original state — for example, if it was yarn when he first stole it and was amputated for it, and he returned it, and then it was woven, and he stole it again — he is amputated. If the thief is amputated while the item is still in his possession, it is returned. If it has perished, he is not liable. If the thief claims that the stolen item is his property, the amputation is dropped from him even if he does not produce evidence.

If a group goes out with the ability to resist — or a single person who can resist — intending highway robbery, and they are apprehended before taking property or killing anyone, the ruler imprisons them until they show repentance. If they take the property of a Muslim or a dhimmi, and the amount taken when divided among

them gives each person ten dirhams or more, or its equivalent in value, the ruler amputates their hands and feet on opposite sides.
sariqah-main-1-5:
If they kill but do not take property, the ruler executes them as a fixed punishment (hadd). Even if the heirs pardon them, their pardon is disregarded. If they kill and take property, the ruler has a choice:

he may amputate their hands and feet on opposite sides and then execute and crucify them; or he may execute them; or he may crucify them — they are crucified alive and their bellies are pierced with a spear until they die. They are not crucified for more than three days.

If among them is a minor, an insane person, or an unmarriageable relative (mahram) of the victim, the fixed punishment is dropped from the rest, and the matter of the killing reverts to the heirs: if they wish, they execute them; and if they wish, they pardon.

If only one person carried out the act, the fixed punishment is applied to the entire group.
ashribah-main-1:
Prohibited beverages are four: wine (khamr), which is grape juice that has fermented, become strong, and thrown off froth; grape juice that has been cooked until less than two-thirds has evaporated; the soaking of dates and raisins when it has become strong; and the nabidh (fermented drink) of dates and raisins — if either is minimally cooked, it is lawful even if it becomes strong, provided one drinks an amount that one reasonably believes will not intoxicate, without amusement or revelry. There is no harm in mixing two types. The nabidh of honey, figs, wheat, barley, and millet is lawful even if not cooked.

Grape juice, if cooked until two-thirds have evaporated and one-third remains, is lawful even if it becomes strong. There is no harm in making nabidh in gourds (dubba'), glazed jars (hantam), pitch-coated vessels (muzaffat), or hollowed-out wood vessels (naqir).

If wine turns into vinegar, it becomes lawful — whether it turned to vinegar on its own or by something being added to it. It is not disliked to cause wine to become vinegar.
sayd-dhabaih-main-1-1:
Hunting with a trained dog, a cheetah, a falcon, and all trained birds of prey is permissible.

The training of a dog is: that it refrains from eating the prey three times.

The training of a falcon is: that it returns when called.

If one sends his trained dog, or his falcon, or his hawk, and mentions the name of Allah Most High upon it at the time of sending, and it catches the game and wounds it and it dies — it is lawful to eat. If the dog eats from it, it may not be eaten. If the falcon eats from it, it may be eaten. If the sender reaches the game while it is still alive, he must slaughter it. If he neglects to slaughter it until it dies, it may not be eaten. If the dog strangles it without wounding it, it may not be eaten.

If an untrained dog, or a dog belonging to a Magian (Zoroastrian), or a dog over which the name of Allah was not mentioned, participates — it may not be eaten.

If a man shoots an arrow at game and mentions the name of Allah at the time of shooting, whatever is hit may be eaten if the arrow wounds it and it dies. If he reaches it alive, he must slaughter it. If he neglects to slaughter it until it dies, it may not be eaten. If the arrow strikes and the animal struggles on until it disappears from view, and the hunter continuously pursues it until he finds it dead — it may be eaten. If he abandons the pursuit and later finds it dead — it may not be eaten. If one shoots at game and it falls into water and dies, it may not be eaten.
sayd-dhabaih-main-1-2:
Likewise, if it falls onto a roof or a mountain and then tumbles to the ground — it may not be eaten. If it falls directly onto the ground, it may be eaten.

What is struck by the broad side of a throwing stick (mi'rad) may not be eaten. If it wounds it, it may be eaten. What is struck and killed by a pellet (bunduqah) may not be eaten.

If one shoots at game and severs a limb from it, the game is eaten but the severed limb is not. If it is cut into thirds, and the larger portion is from the rear —

it is eaten. If the larger portion is from the head side, the larger portion is eaten and the smaller is not. The game of a Magian, an apostate, or an idolater may not be eaten.

If one shoots game and hits it but does not incapacitate it or remove it from the state of being able to escape, and then another person shoots and kills it — it belongs to the second, and it may be eaten.

If the first shooter had incapacitated it and then the second shoots and kills it — it may not be eaten, and the second is liable for its value to the first, minus the depreciation caused by his wound.

Hunting is permissible for animals whose meat is eaten as well as those

whose meat is not eaten.

The slaughtered animal of a Muslim and a Person of the Book (kitabi) is lawful. The slaughtered animal of a Magian, an apostate, an idolater, or a person in the state of ihram (pilgrim sanctity) may not be eaten. If the slaughterer deliberately omits the mention of Allah's name, the slaughtered animal is carrion and may not be eaten. If he omits it forgetfully, it may be eaten.

Slaughter is performed at the throat and the upper chest.
sayd-dhabaih-main-1-3:
The vessels (channels) that are severed in slaughter are four: the trachea (hulqum), the esophagus (mari'), and the two jugular veins (wadajan). If all four are severed, the meat is lawful. If the majority of them are severed, it is likewise according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: the trachea, the esophagus, and at least one jugular vein must be severed.

Slaughter with a sharp reed,

a flint, and anything that causes blood to flow is permissible — except a tooth still attached or a nail still attached.

It is recommended that the slaughterer sharpen his blade. Whoever pushes the knife to the spinal cord or severs the head — that is disliked, but the slaughtered animal may still be eaten. If one slaughters a sheep from the back of the neck, and the animal is still alive when the vessels are severed — it is permissible but disliked. If it dies before the vessels are severed, it may not be eaten.

What has become domesticated from wild game — its proper slaughter is by cutting the throat. What has become wild from livestock — its proper slaughter is by wounding and bleeding.

The recommended method for camels is nahr (spearing at the base of the neck).

If one slaughters a camel by cutting the throat, it is permissible but disliked.

The recommended method for cattle and sheep is slaughter by cutting the throat. If one slaughters them by nahr, it is permissible but disliked.

Whoever slaughters a she-camel or slaughters a cow or a sheep and finds a dead fetus in its belly — it may not be eaten, whether it has hair or not.

It is not permissible to eat any predator that has fangs, nor any bird that has talons. There is no harm in the crop-eating crow. The piebald crow that eats carrion may not be eaten.
sayd-dhabaih-main-1-4:
It is disliked to eat hyena, lizard (dabb), and all types of vermin.

It is not permissible to eat the meat of domestic donkeys and mules. The meat of horses is disliked according to Abu Hanifah. There is no harm

in eating rabbit. If an animal whose meat is not eaten is slaughtered, its meat and skin become ritually pure,

except for humans and pigs — slaughter has no purifying effect on them.

From aquatic animals, only fish may be eaten. Eating fish that floats dead (tafi) is disliked. There is no harm in eating catfish (jarith) and eel (marmahy).

Locusts may be eaten, and no slaughter is required for them.
udhiyah-main-1:
The sacrificial animal (udhiyah) is obligatory upon every free Muslim who is resident and financially able on the Day of Sacrifice — for himself and for his minor children. He slaughters a sheep for

each one of them, or he may slaughter a camel or a cow on behalf of seven persons. There is no sacrifice upon the poor or the traveler. The time for the sacrifice begins at the break of dawn on the Day of Sacrifice, except that the people of cities may not slaughter until the imam has performed the Eid prayer. As for the people of the countryside, they may slaughter after dawn.

It is permissible on three days: the Day of Sacrifice and the two days following it.

One may not sacrifice a blind animal, a one-eyed animal, a lame animal that cannot walk to the place of slaughter, or an emaciated animal. An animal with a severed ear or tail is not acceptable, nor one that has lost most of its ear. If the greater part of the ear and tail remains, it is acceptable.

It is permissible to sacrifice a hornless animal, a castrated animal, a mangy animal, and a confused animal.

The sacrifice may be from camels, cattle, or sheep. From all of these, the thaniyy (two-year-old for sheep, five-year-old for camels, two-year-old for cattle) and above is acceptable, except for sheep — for which the jadh'ah (six-month or one-year-old) is acceptable.

One may eat from the meat of the sacrifice, feed both the wealthy and the poor, and store it.

It is recommended not to give less than one-third in charity. One should give away the skin in charity or make household items from it.

It is best for one to slaughter his own sacrifice if he knows how to slaughter.

It is disliked to have a Person of the Book slaughter it.

If two men make a mistake and each slaughters the other's sacrifice, it suffices for both and neither owes the other anything.
bughat-main-1:
If a group of Muslims seizes control of a territory and rebels against the authority of the ruler (imam),

he invites them to return to the community and addresses their grievances. He does not initiate fighting against them until they initiate it. If they begin fighting, he fights them until their forces are dispersed. If they have a base to retreat to, he finishes off their wounded and pursues their fleeing. If they do not have a base to retreat to, he does not finish off their wounded and does not pursue their fleeing. Their offspring are not taken captive and their property is not taken as spoils. There is no harm in fighting them with their own weapons

if the Muslims need them. The ruler confiscates their property and neither returns it to them nor distributes it until they repent, at which point it is returned.

Whatever the rebels collected from the territories they controlled — of land tax (kharaj) and tithes ('ushr) — the ruler does not collect it a second time. If they spent it in its proper place, it suffices for whoever paid it. If they did not spend it in its proper place, the people are advised as a matter between them and Allah Most High to repeat the payment.
siyar-main-1-1:
Jihad is a communal obligation (fard kifayah): if a sufficient group undertakes it, the obligation is lifted from the rest. If no one undertakes it, all the people are sinful for abandoning it.

Fighting the disbelievers is obligatory even if they do not initiate hostilities. Jihad is not obligatory upon a minor, a slave, a woman, a blind person, a cripple, or an amputee.

If the enemy attacks a territory, it becomes obligatory upon all Muslims to defend it. The woman goes out without her husband's permission and the slave without his master's permission.

When Muslims enter the territory of war and besiege a city or fortress, they invite its people to Islam. If they accept, fighting ceases. If they refuse, they are invited to pay the poll tax (jizyah). If they agree, they have the same rights as Muslims and the same obligations.

It is not permissible to fight those who have not yet received the invitation to Islam until they are first invited. It is recommended to invite those who have already received the invitation, though it is not obligatory. If they refuse, the Muslims seek Allah's help against them, wage war upon them, set up catapults against them, burn them, flood them with water, cut down their trees, and destroy their crops. There is no harm in shooting at them even if there is a Muslim prisoner or merchant among them. If they use Muslim children or prisoners as human shields, the Muslims do not cease
siyar-main-1-2:
shooting at them, but they aim at the disbelievers.

There is no harm in taking women and copies of the Quran with the Muslim army if it is a large force that can be relied upon for their protection. It is disliked to take them with a small detachment that cannot guarantee their safety.

A woman does not fight except with her husband's permission, nor a slave except with his master's permission — unless the enemy launches a sudden attack.

Muslims should not betray trusts, nor commit theft from spoils, nor

mutilate the dead, nor kill women, decrepit old men, children, blind persons, or cripples — unless these individuals have a strategic role in the war, or the woman is a queen. They do not kill insane persons.

If the ruler sees benefit for the Muslims in making peace with the enemy or a group of them, there is no harm in it. If he makes peace for a period and then sees that breaking the peace is more beneficial, he gives them notice and fights them.

If they initiate treachery, he fights them without giving notice, provided the treachery was by their collective agreement.

If their slaves come out to the Muslim camp, they are free.

There is no harm in the army foraging in the territory of war and eating whatever food they find.
siyar-main-1-3:
They may use firewood, apply oil, and fight with whatever weapons they find, without formally dividing any of it. It is not permissible for them to sell any of it or take personal ownership of it.

Whoever among them accepts Islam protects by his Islam: himself, his minor children, and every property in his possession or deposited with a Muslim or a dhimmi. If we conquer the territory, his real estate is spoils, his wife is spoils, her pregnancy is spoils, and his children are spoils.

Weapons should not be sold to the people of the territory of war, nor should supplies be sent to them. They are not ransomed with prisoners according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: Muslim prisoners may be exchanged for them. It is not permissible to release them unconditionally.

When the ruler conquers a land by force, he has a choice: he may divide it among the fighters, or he may leave its people upon it and impose the land tax (kharaj). Regarding prisoners, he has a choice: he may execute them, enslave them, or leave them as free persons under the protection of the Muslims.

It is not permissible to return them to the territory of war.

If the Muslims wish to return and have livestock they cannot transport to the territory of Islam, they slaughter them and burn them. They do not hamstring them or leave them behind.
siyar-main-1-4:
Spoils are not divided in the territory of war until they are brought to the territory of Islam. The reserve forces and the fighting forces in the army are equal.

If reinforcements arrive in the territory of war before the spoils are brought to the territory of Islam, they share in them. The market people of the army have no share in the spoils unless they fight.

If a free man or woman — or a group or the people of a fortress or city — are granted safe-conduct (aman) by a person, their safe-conduct is valid and no Muslim may kill them — unless there is a harmful consequence in it, in which case the ruler gives them notice.

The safe-conduct of a dhimmi is not valid, nor that of a prisoner, nor of a merchant who enters their territory.

The safe-conduct of a slave is not valid according to Abu Hanifah unless his master permits him to fight. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: his safe-conduct is valid.

If the Turks overpower the Byzantines, capture them, and take their property — they own it. If we then overpower the Turks, it is lawful for us. If they overpower and secure our property in their territory — they own it. If Muslims then recapture it and find it before distribution — it is returned to its owners without cost. If they find it after distribution — the original owners may take it

by paying its assessed value, if they wish.
siyar-main-1-5:
If a merchant enters the territory of war and purchases such property and brings it to the territory of Islam, the original owner has a choice: if he wishes, he takes it for the price the merchant paid; and if he wishes, he leaves it.

The people of the territory of war cannot gain ownership over us by conquest of our mudabbars, mukatabs, mothers of our children, and our free persons. But we can gain ownership over theirs in all such cases. If a slave of a Muslim runs away and enters their territory and they seize him — they do not own him according to Abu Hanifah.

If a stray camel wanders to them and they take it — they own it.

If the ruler does not have transport

to carry the spoils, he distributes them among the fighters as a temporary allocation (qismat ida') so that they carry them to the territory of Islam, then he recollects them and divides them.

It is not permissible to sell spoils before division.

Whoever among the fighters dies in the territory of war has no share in the spoils. Whoever dies after the spoils are brought to the territory of Islam — his share goes to his heirs.

There is no harm in the ruler transferring shares during the battle and incentivizing fighting through extra awards (nafl), such as saying: "Whoever kills an enemy combatant, his spoils belong to him," or telling a detachment: "I have designated for you one quarter after the fifth."
siyar-main-1-6:
After the spoils are secured, extra awards are given only from the fifth (khums). If the spoils of the slain are not designated for the killer, they are part of the general spoils, and the killer and others are equal in them.

The spoils of the slain consist of: the clothing, weapons, and mount of the slain enemy.

When Muslims leave the territory of war, they may not use fodder or eat from the spoils.

Whatever remains of fodder or food is returned to the spoils.

The ruler divides the spoils:

He sets aside one-fifth and divides the remaining four-fifths among the fighters: a cavalryman receives one share and a foot soldier receives one share according to Abu Hanifah. They (Abu Yusuf and Muhammad) said: a cavalryman receives three shares. A share is allocated for only one horse. Arabian horses and mixed-breed horses are treated equally. No share is allocated for a riding camel or a mule.

Whoever enters the territory of war as a cavalryman and his horse dies receives the share of a cavalryman. Whoever enters on foot and then buys a horse receives the share of a foot soldier.

No share is allocated for a slave, a woman, a dhimmi, or a minor — but they receive a discretionary grant (radhkh) as the ruler sees fit.

As for the fifth, it is divided into three portions: a portion for orphans, a portion for the destitute,

and a portion

for wayfarers (ibn al-sabil). The poor among the Prophet's relatives are included among them and given priority. Nothing is given to their wealthy.
siyar-main-1-7:
Allah Most High's mention in the verse about the fifth is merely to begin the speech with His blessed name. The share of the Prophet (peace be upon him) ceased with his death, just as the exclusive selection (safi) ceased. The share of the near relatives — they were entitled to it during the Prophet's lifetime through their support, and after his death through their poverty.

If one or two individuals enter the territory of war raiding without the ruler's permission and take something, it is not subject to the fifth (khums).

If a group with the capability to defend itself enters and takes something, the fifth is applied even if the ruler did not give them permission.

If a Muslim enters the territory of war as a merchant, it is not lawful for him to take any of their property or shed any of their blood.

If he betrays them and takes something and leaves with it, he owns it — but it is sinfully acquired, and he is commanded to give it in charity.
siyar-main-1-8:
If a combatant non-Muslim (harbi) enters our territory with safe-conduct (aman), he is not permitted to stay in our territory for a year. The ruler tells him: "If you remain for the full year, I will impose the poll tax (jizyah) upon you." If he stays, the jizyah is collected from him and he becomes a dhimmi, and he is not permitted to return to the territory of war. If he returns to the territory of war and leaves a deposit with a Muslim or a dhimmi, or a debt owed to him by them — his protection has become void by his return, and whatever property he has in the territory of Islam is at risk. If he is captured or killed, his debts are extinguished and the deposit becomes spoils.

What the Muslims gain from the property of the enemy without fighting is spent on the welfare of the Muslims, just as the land tax (kharaj) is spent.

The land of the Arabs is entirely tithe land ('ushriyyah), which extends from al-Udhayb to the farthest reach of Hajar

in Yemen at Mahrah to the border

of Syria. The Sawad (Iraq) is entirely land-tax land, extending from al-Udhayb to the pass of Hulwan, and from al-Ulth to Abadan.

The land of the Sawad is owned by its inhabitants: their sale of it and their transactions regarding it are valid.

Every land whose people accepted Islam while upon it, or that was conquered by force and divided among the fighters, is tithe land.

Every land that was conquered by force and whose people were left upon it is land-tax land.

Revival of Barren Land
siyar-main-1-9:
Whoever revives barren land — according to Abu Yusuf it is classified according to its surrounding area: if it is in the vicinity of land-tax land, it is subject to the land tax; if in the vicinity of tithe land, it is subject to the tithe. Basrah, according to him, is tithe land by the consensus of the Companions (may Allah be pleased with them).

Muhammad said: If he revives it with a well he digs, a spring he uncovers, or water from the Tigris, Euphrates, or the great rivers that no one owns,

it is tithe land. If he revives it with water from canals dug by the non-Arab rulers, such as the Canal of the King or the Canal of Yazdegerd, it is land-tax land.

The land tax that Umar (may Allah be pleased with him) imposed on the people of the Sawad: for every jarib (unit of land) that water reaches — one Hashimi qafiz (which equals one sa') and one dirham. For a jarib of alfalfa: five dirhams. For a jarib of continuous grapevines and continuous date palms: ten dirhams.

For all other crops, the assessment is based on capacity. If the land cannot bear what was imposed upon it, the ruler reduces it.

If water overwhelms the land-tax land, or is cut off from it, or the crops are destroyed by a disaster — no land tax is owed. If the owner leaves the land idle — the land tax is still owed.

Whoever among the land-tax people accepts Islam — the land tax is still collected from him as before.
siyar-main-1-10:
It is permissible for a Muslim to purchase land-tax land from a dhimmi, and the land tax is collected from him. There is no tithe on the produce of land-tax land.

The poll tax (jizyah) is of two types:

A poll tax established by mutual agreement and treaty, which is set according to what is agreed upon. And a poll tax that the ruler initiates when he overcomes the disbelievers and leaves them on their properties. He imposes upon the obviously wealthy: forty-eight dirhams per year, collecting four dirhams per month from him.

Upon the middle-income person: twenty-four dirhams, two dirhams per month. Upon the working poor: twelve dirhams, one dirham per month.

The poll tax is imposed upon the People of the Book, the Magians, and the idol-worshippers from among the non-Arabs. It is not imposed upon the idol-worshippers among the Arabs, nor upon apostates.

There is no poll tax upon a woman, a minor, a cripple, a blind person, a non-working pauper, or monks who

do not mix with people.

Whoever accepts Islam while poll tax is due upon him — it is dropped from him. If two years overlap, the poll taxes merge into one.

It is not permissible to build new churches or synagogues in the territory of Islam. If old churches and synagogues fall into ruin, they may be rebuilt.
siyar-main-1-11:
The dhimmis are required to distinguish themselves from Muslims in their clothing, their mounts, their saddles, and their caps. They may not ride horses or carry weapons.

Whoever refuses to pay the poll tax, or kills a Muslim, or insults the Prophet (peace be upon him), or commits fornication with a Muslim woman — his covenant is not broken. The covenant is only broken if he joins the territory of war, or if they seize a location and wage war against us.

If a Muslim apostates from Islam, Islam is presented to him. If he has a doubt, it is addressed. He is imprisoned for three days. If he accepts Islam,

well and good. Otherwise he is executed. If someone kills him before Islam is presented to him, that is disapproved, but there is no liability upon the killer.

As for a woman, if she apostates, she is not executed but is imprisoned until she accepts Islam.

The ownership of the apostate over his property is suspended by his apostasy. If he accepts Islam, his property returns to its former state. If he dies, is killed, or joins the territory of war while an apostate — what he acquired during his Islam is transferred to his Muslim heirs.

What he acquired during his apostasy is spoils (fay').
siyar-main-1-12:
If he joins the territory of war as an apostate and the judge rules on his departure, his mudabbars and mothers of his children are freed, the debts owed to him become payable, what he acquired during Islam is transferred to his Muslim heirs, debts incurred during Islam are paid from what he acquired during Islam, and debts incurred during apostasy are paid from what he acquired during apostasy.

Whatever he sold, purchased, or transacted regarding his property during his apostasy

is suspended: if he accepts Islam, his transactions become valid; if he dies, is killed, or joins the territory of war — they are void.

If the apostate returns to the territory of Islam as a Muslim after the ruling on his departure, whatever he finds in the hands of his heirs from

his property in its original form, he takes back.

As for a female apostate — her transactions regarding her property during her apostasy are valid.

The Christians of Banu Taghlib: double what is taken from Muslims in zakat is taken from their property. It is taken from their women as well, but not from their children.
siyar-main-1-13: Whatever the ruler collects from the land tax, from the property of Banu Taghlib, from gifts given by the enemy to the ruler, and from the poll tax — all of it is spent on the welfare of the Muslims. Border fortresses are reinforced from it, bridges and dams are built, Muslim judges and scholars are given sufficient stipends from it, and the provisions of the fighters and their dependents are paid from it.
dawa-main-1-1:
The claimant (mudda'i) is: the one who is not compelled to litigate if he abandons his claim.

The defendant (mudda'a alayhi) is: the one who is compelled to litigate.

A claim is not accepted until the claimant specifies something known in its type and quantity. If it is a physical item in the defendant's possession, he is required to produce it so that the claimant may point to it. If it is not present, he describes its value. If he claims real estate, he specifies its boundaries and states that it is in the defendant's possession and that he is demanding it. If it is a right owed as a debt, he states that he is demanding it.

Once the claim is valid, the judge asks the defendant about it. If he acknowledges it, the judge rules against him accordingly.

If he denies it, the judge asks the claimant for evidence (bayyinah). If he produces it, the judge rules accordingly. If he is unable to do so and requests that his opponent be sworn, the oath is administered.

If the claimant says: "I have evidence" and requests the oath — the oath is not administered according to Abu Hanifah.

The oath is not referred back to the claimant.

The evidence of the possessor is not accepted for absolute ownership.

If the defendant refuses to swear (nukul), the judge rules against him by his refusal, and what was claimed against him becomes binding.

The judge should say to him: "I will present the oath to you three times. If you swear, well and good. Otherwise, I will rule against you with what was claimed." After repeating the offer three times, he rules against him by his refusal.
dawa-main-1-2:
If the claim concerns marriage, the denier is not made to swear according to Abu Hanifah.

Oaths are not administered in matters of marriage, resumption of conjugal relations (raj'ah),

resumption of marital rights in ila' (oath of abstinence), enslavement, claiming a slave-mother (istilad), lineage, patronage (wala'), or fixed punishments (hudud).

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: oaths are administered in all of these except fixed punishments and retribution.

If two persons claim an item in the possession of a third, each asserting it is his, and both produce evidence — it is divided between them.

If each of two men claims to have married a woman and both produce evidence — neither evidence is accepted, and the matter is referred to the woman to confirm one of them.

If two persons each claim to have purchased a slave from someone and both produce evidence — each has the choice: to take half the slave for half the price, or to decline. If the judge awards it to both and one says he does not want it — the other cannot take the whole. If one of them specifies a date, the earlier one prevails. If neither specifies a date and one of them has physical possession — the possessor prevails.

If one claims purchase and the other claims a gift with possession, and both produce

evidence with no dates — purchase prevails.
dawa-main-1-3:
If one claims purchase and a woman claims he married her in exchange for the item — they are treated equally.

If one claims a pledge with possession and the other a gift with possession — the pledge prevails. If two outsiders produce evidence of ownership with dates, the one with the earlier date prevails. If they both claim purchase from the same person and produce evidence with dates — the earlier one prevails. If each produces evidence of purchase from a different person and they mention dates — they are treated equally. If the outsider produces evidence of ownership with a date, and the possessor produces evidence of ownership with an earlier date — the possessor prevails.

If the outsider and the possessor each produce evidence of birth of the animal (nitaj) — the possessor prevails. Likewise for the weaving of cloth that is only woven once — and every cause of ownership that does not recur is treated the same. If the outsider produces evidence of ownership and the possessor produces evidence of having purchased it from the outsider — the possessor prevails. If each produces evidence of purchase from the other with no dates — the two testimonies cancel each other out.

If one claimant produces two witnesses and the other produces four — they are treated equally.

Whoever claims a right of retribution (qisas) against another and is denied,
dawa-main-1-4:
the defendant is made to swear. If he refuses to swear regarding something short of taking life — retribution is imposed upon him. If he refuses in a case involving life, he is imprisoned until he confesses or swears. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: the compensation (arsh) is imposed in both cases.

If the claimant says: "I have evidence present," the opponent is told: "Provide a personal surety for three days." If he does, well and good. Otherwise, the claimant is ordered to accompany him — unless the claimant is a stranger passing through, in which case he accompanies him only for the duration of the judge's session.

If the defendant says: "This item was deposited with me by an absent person," or "pledged with me," or "I seized it from him," and produces evidence of this — there is no litigation between him and the claimant.

If he says: "I purchased it from the absent person" — he remains a party to the litigation.

If the claimant says: "It was stolen from me" and produces evidence, and the possessor says: "So-and-so deposited it with me" and produces evidence — the litigation is not dismissed.

If the claimant says: "I purchased it from so-and-so" and the possessor says: "That same so-and-so deposited it with me" — the litigation is dismissed without evidence.
dawa-main-1-5:
Oaths are sworn by Allah Most High alone, and are reinforced by mentioning His attributes. Oaths are not administered by divorce or by manumission. A Jew is sworn by Allah Who revealed the Torah to Musa. A Christian is sworn by Allah Who revealed the Gospel to 'Isa. A Magian is sworn by Allah Who created fire.

They are not made to swear in their houses of worship.

It is not required to make the oath solemn for a Muslim by specifying a time or place.

Whoever claims that he purchased another's slave for one thousand and is denied — the defendant is sworn: "By Allah, there is no subsisting sale between you." He is not sworn: "By Allah, I did not sell." In cases of usurpation, the oath is: "By Allah, you are not obligated to return it to him." He is not sworn: "By Allah, I did not usurp." In marriage: "By Allah, there is no subsisting marriage between you

at this time." In a claim of divorce: "By Allah, she is not irrevocably separated from you at this moment for the reason you claim." He is not sworn: "By Allah, I did not divorce her."
dawa-main-1-6:
If a house is in a man's possession and one claimant claims all of it and the other claims half, and both produce evidence — according to Abu Hanifah, the one claiming all gets three-quarters and the one claiming half gets one-quarter. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: it is divided between them in thirds. If the house were in both their hands, the one claiming all of it would receive: half by right of judgment and half not by right of judgment.

If two people dispute an animal and each produces evidence that it was born in his possession and they specify dates, and the animal's age matches one of the dates — that person prevails. If the matter is ambiguous, it is divided between them.

If two people dispute an animal and one is riding it while the other is holding its bridle — the rider prevails. Likewise if they dispute a camel and one of them has cargo on it — the one with the cargo prevails. Likewise if they dispute a shirt and one is wearing it while the other is holding its sleeve — the wearer prevails.

If the buyer and seller disagree about the sale — the buyer claiming one price and the seller claiming more, or the seller acknowledging a certain amount of the sold goods and the buyer claiming more — and one of them produces evidence, the judge rules in his favor.
dawa-main-1-7:
If both produce evidence, the evidence establishing the higher amount prevails. If neither has evidence, the buyer is told:

Either accept the price the seller claims, or we rescind the sale. And the seller is told: Either deliver what the buyer claims, or we rescind the sale. If they do not reach agreement, the judge makes each of them swear to the other's claim, starting with the buyer's oath. When both have sworn, the judge rescinds the sale. If one of them refuses to swear, the other's claim is binding upon him.

If they disagree about the term of payment, a condition of option (khiyar), or partial payment of the price — there is no mutual oath between them. The statement of the one denying the option and the term is accepted with his oath.

If the sold item has perished and they then disagree — they do not take mutual oaths according to Abu Hanifah and Abu Yusuf, and the buyer's statement is accepted. Muhammad said: they take mutual oaths and the sale is rescinded based on the value of the perished item.

If one of two slaves has perished and they disagree about the price — they do not take mutual oaths according to Abu Hanifah unless the seller agrees to waive the share of the perished slave. Abu Yusuf said:
dawa-main-1-8:
They take mutual oaths and the contract is rescinded regarding the living slave. Muhammad (may Allah have mercy on him) said: they take mutual oaths on both, the contract is rescinded, and the living slave is returned along with the value of the perished one.

If the spouses disagree about the dower — the husband claiming he married her for one thousand and the wife saying: "You married me for two thousand" — whichever of them produces evidence, that evidence is accepted.

If both produce evidence, the wife's evidence prevails. If neither has evidence, they take mutual oaths according to Abu Hanifah, but the marriage is not rescinded. Instead, the dower equivalent to the customary dower of her peers (mahr al-mithl) is applied:

If the customary dower equals what the husband acknowledged or less, the husband's claim is upheld. If it equals what the wife claimed or more, the wife's claim is upheld. If the customary dower is more than what the husband acknowledged but less than what the wife claimed, she is awarded the customary dower.

If they disagree about a lease before the leased benefit has been consumed, they take mutual oaths and the contract is rescinded. If they disagree after the benefit has been fully consumed, they do not take mutual oaths, and the tenant's statement is accepted.

If they disagree after partial consumption of the leased benefit, they take mutual oaths and the contract is rescinded for what remains, and the tenant's statement is accepted regarding the past period.
dawa-main-1-9:
If the master and the mukatab (slave in a manumission contract) disagree about the amount of the manumission payment — they do not take mutual oaths according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: they take mutual oaths and the contract is rescinded.

If the spouses disagree about the household goods: what is suitable for men belongs to the husband, what is suitable for women belongs to the wife, and what is suitable for both belongs to the husband.

If one of them dies and the survivor disputes with the heirs:

what is suitable for men and women belongs to the surviving spouse. Abu Yusuf said: the wife is given what a woman of her status would typically have as her trousseau, and the rest belongs to the husband.

If a man sells a slave-girl and she bears a child, and the seller claims the child: if she delivered within less than six months from the date of sale, the child is the seller's and the woman is his umm walad (mother of the master's child), and the sale is rescinded with the return of the price. If the buyer also claims the child — whether simultaneously with or after the seller's claim — the seller's claim prevails. If she delivered after more than six months, the seller's claim is not accepted unless the buyer confirms it.
dawa-main-1-10:
If the child dies and the seller claims him, having been delivered within less than six months — the status of umm walad is not established for the mother. If the mother dies and the seller claims the child, having been delivered within less than six months — the lineage of the child is established from him, the seller takes the child, and returns the entire price according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: he returns the portion attributable to the child and does not return the portion attributable to the mother.

Whoever claims the lineage of one of twins — the lineage of both is established from him. And Allah knows best.
shahadat-intro-1-1:
Testimony is an obligation that witnesses are required to fulfill. It is not permissible for them to conceal it when the claimant demands it.

As for testimony regarding fixed punishments (hudud), the witness has a choice between

concealing and disclosing, and concealing is preferable — except that he must testify about the property in cases of theft, saying: "He took it" rather than: "He stole it."

Testimony is of varying levels: testimony regarding fornication requires four male witnesses, and the testimony of women is not accepted in it. Testimony regarding the remaining fixed punishments and retribution is accepted from two men, and the testimony of women is not accepted.

For all other rights, the testimony of two men,

or one man and two women, is accepted — whether the right is financial or non-financial, such as marriage, divorce, agency (wakalah), and bequests.

In matters of childbirth, virginity, and defects in women in places that men do not see — the testimony of a single woman suffices.

In all of these, uprightness (adalah) is required,

as is the formal wording of testimony. If the witness does not use the formal wording of testimony and says: "I know" or "I am certain," his testimony is not accepted.
shahadat-intro-1-2:
Abu Hanifah said: the judge relies on the outward uprightness of the Muslim, except in fixed punishments and retribution, where he investigates the witnesses. If the opposing party impugns them, he investigates them. Abu Yusuf

and Muhammad said: they must be investigated both privately and publicly in all cases.

What a witness may bear testimony to is of two types:

The first: what takes legal effect on its own — such as a sale, a confession, usurpation, killing, and a judge's ruling. If a witness hears or sees it, he may testify to it even if he was not specifically asked to witness it. He says: "I testify that he sold" — not: "He made me a witness."

The second: what does not take legal effect on its own — such as testimony upon testimony. If one hears a witness testify to something, he may not testify upon that witness's testimony

unless the witness specifically asks him to bear testimony. Likewise, if he hears a witness delegating his testimony to another witness, the listener may not testify.

It is not permissible for a witness, if he recognizes his handwriting, to testify unless he actually remembers the testimony.

The testimony of a blind person is not accepted, nor that of a slave, nor of one who has been punished for false accusation (qadhf) even if he repents. Nor is the testimony of a parent for his child or grandchild, nor a child's testimony for his parents or grandparents.

The testimony of one spouse for the other is not accepted.
shahadat-intro-1-3:
The testimony of a master for his slave is not accepted, nor for his mukatab. The testimony of a partner for his partner in matters related to their partnership is not accepted.

The testimony of a man for his brother or uncle is accepted.

The testimony of an effeminate man (mukhannath) is not accepted, nor that of a professional mourner, a professional female singer, one who habitually drinks for amusement, one who plays with pigeons, one who sings for people,

nor one who commits a major sin entailing a fixed punishment, nor one who enters the bathhouse without a waist-cloth, nor one who consumes usury, nor a gambler with backgammon or chess, nor one who engages in undignified conduct such as urinating on the road or eating on the road.

The testimony of one who publicly insults the early Muslims (the Salaf) is not accepted. The testimony of people of theological innovation is accepted, except for the Khattabiyyah sect.

The testimony of the People of the Book (dhimmis) against each other is accepted even if their religions differ. The testimony of a combatant non-Muslim (harbi) against a dhimmi is not accepted.

If a person's good deeds outweigh his bad deeds and he avoids major sins — his testimony is accepted even if he commits a minor sin.

The testimony of the uncircumcised,

the castrated, and the child of fornication is accepted. The testimony of a person of ambiguous gender (khuntha) is permissible.

If the testimony matches
shahadat-intro-1-4:
the claim, it is accepted. If it contradicts it, it is not accepted. Agreement between the two witnesses in wording and meaning is required according to Abu Hanifah.

If one testifies to one thousand and the other to two thousand — the testimony is not accepted. If one testifies to one thousand and the other to one thousand five hundred, and the claimant claims one thousand five hundred — their testimony is accepted for one thousand.

If one of the witnesses testifies to one thousand and says: "He repaid five hundred of it" — his testimony for one thousand is accepted, but his statement that five hundred was repaid is not heard unless another witness testifies to it as well. The witness who knows of the repayment should not testify to one thousand until the claimant acknowledges that he received five hundred.

If two witnesses testify that Zayd was killed on the Day of Sacrifice in Makkah, and two others testify that he was killed on the Day of Sacrifice in Kufah, and all are present before the judge — he does not accept either testimony. If one testimony precedes the other and he rules on it, and the other then appears — it is not accepted. The judge does not hear testimony regarding character defects (jarh) nor rule based on that.

It is not permissible for a witness to testify to something he did not personally observe — except for lineage, death, marriage, consummation, and a judge's appointment. He may testify to these matters if informed by someone he trusts.
shahadat-intro-1-5:
Testimony upon testimony (shahada 'ala shahada) is permissible in every right that is not dropped by doubt (shubhah). It is not accepted in fixed punishments and retribution.

Two witnesses may testify upon the testimony of two other witnesses, but one witness upon one witness is not accepted.

The method of delegated testimony is that the original witness says to the branch witness: "Bear testimony upon my testimony that I testify that so-and-so son of so-and-so confessed before me to such-and-such and made me a witness against himself." If he does not say "and made me a witness against himself," it is still valid. The branch witness says when giving testimony: "I testify that so-and-so son of so-and-so made me bear testimony upon his testimony that he testifies that so-and-so confessed before him to such-and-such, and he said to me: Bear testimony upon my testimony to that effect."

The testimony of branch witnesses is not accepted unless the original witnesses have died, or are absent at a distance of three days' travel or more, or are ill with an illness that prevents them from attending the judge's session.

If the original witnesses

verify the uprightness of the branch witnesses — that is sufficient.

If they remain silent about their verification — it is still valid, and the judge examines their condition. If the original witnesses deny the testimony, the branch witnesses' testimony is not accepted.

Abu Hanifah said regarding a false witness: he is publicly exposed in the marketplace but not given ta'zir. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: he is given a sound beating and imprisoned.
shahadat-bab-1-1:
If witnesses retract their testimony before judgment is passed — the testimony is void. If judgment has been passed based on their testimony and they then retract, the judgment is not reversed, but they become liable for whatever they caused to be lost through their testimony. Retraction is only valid before the judge.

If two witnesses testify to a financial claim and the judge rules accordingly, then they retract — they are liable for the amount testified against the defendant. If one retracts, he is liable for half. If three testify to a financial claim and one retracts — there is no liability upon him. If another then retracts, the two retractors are liable for half the amount.

If one man and two women testify, and one woman retracts — she is liable for one quarter of the claim. If both retract — they are liable for half.

If one man and ten women testify, and eight women retract — there is no liability upon them. If another woman retracts, the women are liable for one quarter of the claim. If the man and all the women retract — the man is liable for one-sixth and the women for five-sixths according to Abu Hanifah. Abu Yusuf and Muhammad said: the man is liable for half and the women for half.

If two witnesses testify regarding a woman's marriage with a dower equal to the customary dower of her peers, and then retract — there is no liability upon them. Likewise if they testify regarding a man's marriage with a dower equal to the customary dower. If they testify to more than the customary dower and then retract — they are liable for the excess.

If they testify to a sale at fair market value or above and then retract — they are not liable. If the sale was below market value — they are liable for the shortfall.

If they testify that a man divorced his wife before consummation and then retract — they are liable for half

the dower. If after consummation — they are not liable. If they testify that he freed his slave and then retract — they are liable for his value.
